INTRODUCTION BY THE AUTHOR

To the Reader Who Seeks Truth in Ancient Whispers:

This is the story of the Stone of Destiny—that ancient seat upon which Scottish and English monarchs are still crowned, whose origins reach back through mists of time to the very Temple of Solomon. It is the story of Jeremiah the Prophet, whose mission extended far beyond his recorded prophecies to encompass a divine commission that would span continents and centuries. Most importantly, it is the story of two princesses who carried the blood of David to the shores of Ireland, establishing there a throne that would endure through the ages.

I present this fictionalised chronicle through the eyes of Baruch ben Neriah, Jeremiah's faithful scribe, whose historical existence and intimate knowledge of these events makes him the perfect witness to such extraordinary occurrences. Though I’ve woven informed speculation to bridge gaps in the historical record, the core of this fantasy epic is firmly grounded in biblical evidence, textual analysis, and cultural traditions.

Since this narrative is framed as an epic fantasy, some may find it affirms long-held but seldom-expressed beliefs. I encourage you to read with an open mind, ready to entertain the idea that the ancient world was far more interconnected, intentional, and marvellous than our current perspectives might allow.

Mark Twain, who wrote in Following the Equator (1897): “Truth is stranger than fiction, but it is because Fiction is obliged to stick to possibilities; Truth ain’t.” In the tangled threads of ancient history, where empires rise and fall, where royal bloodlines face extinction only to resurface in unexpected places, where divine promises span generations and geographical boundaries, perhaps the most fantastic explanation is sometimes the only one that adequately accounts for all the evidence.

Whether you read this as history, as theological reflection, or simply as an adventure across the ancient world, I hope you will find, as I have, that some truths are too large to be contained within the narrow confines of conventional historical narrative. They require the broader canvas of epic storytelling, where the sweep of divine purpose can be glimpsed across the centuries.

The Stone and the Sceptre endure. The promises remain unbroken. And somewhere in the mists of Irish antiquity, the echo of ancient Hebrew prayers still whispers across the emerald hills, testifying to a faithfulness that spans the ages.

Kerman Gild
 
PROLOGUE: THE SCRIBE'S TESTAMENT

Written by Baruch ben Neriah in the Fifteenth Year of the Reign of Eochaidh Herremon, High King of all Éire

To all who shall come after, and to the generations yet unborn who shall inherit the promises made to the fathers:

I, Baruch ben Neriah, son of Maaseiah, grandson of the scribe whose faithful service preserved the words of the prophet Jeremiah through the darkest hours of Judah's history, do set my hand to write this testament while strength yet remains in these aged fingers and clarity still dwells within this mind that has witnessed wonders beyond mortal comprehension.

Fifteen years have passed since our feet first touched the shores of the Emerald Isle, fifteen years since the sacred Stone of Destiny was placed upon the Hill of Tara, fifteen years since the ancient prophecies found their fulfilment in ways that exceeded even the vision of those who first spoke them. The kingdom established through divine providence has flourished beyond our highest hopes, the royal bloodline has taken root in soil blessed by the Most High, and the purposes of the Eternal have been accomplished through instruments of clay who marvelled daily at the privilege of serving such glorious ends.

Yet as I approach the threshold of my own departure from this earthly tabernacle, the burden weighs heavily upon my heart to preserve in faithful account the extraordinary events that brought us to these western shores. The mighty works of the Lord must not perish with those who witnessed them; the testimonies of His faithfulness must endure for generations who will face their own trials and need encouragement drawn from our experience of divine deliverance.

The chronicle that follows is not the product of imagination or the embellishment of legend, but the careful record of one who was present during every stage of our remarkable journey. I have striven to set down the truth without addition or omission, that those who come after might know how the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob fulfilled His promises to David even in the midst of exile and apparent defeat.

From the burning ruins of Jerusalem to the mystical shores of Ireland, from the depths of despair to the heights of triumph, this is the story of how the sacred bloodline was preserved and transplanted according to purposes that reach back to the foundations of the world and forward to the ultimate establishment of divine kingdom upon the earth.

May the Lord bless this account to the strengthening of faith and the encouragement of hearts that trust in His unfailing promises. May future generations read these words and take courage from the testimony that He who began the good work will surely complete it according to His perfect will and timing.

Written in the City of Tara, in the land of Éire, by the hand of Baruch ben Neriah, faithful chronicler of the mighty works of the Most High among His people.

Blessed be the Name of the Lord forever and ever. Amen.
 
Chapter 1: The Last Days of Zedekiah's Reign

As chronicled by Baruch ben Neriah

In which the ruling Davidic line of kings ceases to exist in Judah along with Jerusalem’s fall

The siege of Jerusalem had begun in the tenth month of the ninth year of King Zedekiah's reign, when Nebuchadnezzar's vast host first encamped against our walls. Now, as I record these events, it was drawing toward its terrible conclusion in the eleventh year of his reign. From my vantage point atop one of Jerusalem's eastern towers, where I often retreated to transcribe Jeremiah's prophecies in solitude, the encircling Babylonian forces appeared as numerous as the stars in the firmament. Their camp fires dotted the landscape like a malevolent constellation, each light a harbinger of our coming doom.

The air of Jerusalem, in those final, suffocating months of the siege, hung thick with the stench of despair and the acrid smoke of distant fires. By then, the Babylonian siege had tightened its coils around the Holy City for a year and a half—a serpent slowly crushing the life from its prey. The once-bustling markets stood empty, the songs of merchants and children replaced by the moans of the dying and the desperate prayers of the living. The mighty walls that had for centuries protected the City of David now served only to pen us in with our suffering, like beasts awaiting slaughter.

I descended from the tower, my sandals scraping against stone steps worn smooth by generations of watchmen before me. The parchment of Jeremiah's latest warning felt heavy in my hands—heavier than the mere weight of lambskin and ink should allow. These were words that could save the city, if only they would be heeded. But I knew, with the bitter certainty that had become my constant companion, that they would not.

The streets I traversed to reach the palace were nearly unrecognizable from those I had known since childhood. Jerusalem, once the jewel of Judah, had become a haunt for scavengers and shadows. Famine, a more insidious foe than any battering ram, stalked the alleys, turning the faces of our people into gaunt masks of suffering. I passed a woman sitting motionless against a wall, a bundle clutched to her breast. Only when I drew closer did I realize the bundle was a child, and neither mother nor infant had drawn breath for some time. I paused, whispered a prayer, and continued on my grim errand. There would be time for proper mourning later—if any of us survived to perform it.

The palace guards, once resplendent in polished armour, now stood at their posts in tarnished breastplates over gaunt frames. They knew me well as Jeremiah's scribe and admitted me without question, though their hollow eyes held a mixture of hope and resentment. Hope that the prophet might yet have some word of deliverance; resentment that his previous warnings had proven all too accurate.

In the inner courtyard, a scene of surreal contrast greeted me. A cluster of nobles lounged on cushions beneath a canopy, dining on what appeared to be fresh bread and figs—luxuries that had not been seen in the common markets for months. Their laughter, shrill and desperate, cut through the sombre air like a discordant note in a funeral dirge. Among them sat Mattaniah, who had changed his name to Zedekiah upon taking the throne, a king whose weakness had become his defining characteristic.

King Zedekiah did evil in the sight of the LORD, according to all that Jehoiakim his brother has done. He was a weak king, one elevated beyond his capacity. The youngest son of the good King Josiah, he had never expected to wear the crown. When Nebuchadnezzar deposed his young nephew Jehoiachin after only three months of rule and placed Zedekiah (then named Mattaniah) on the throne as a vassal king, Zedekiah found himself playing a role for which he had neither the training nor the temperament. Now, in his eleventh year as king, with his city dying around him, he clung to his throne amidst the crumbling grandeur of his palace, deaf to the pleas of his starving subjects and, more tragically, to the urgent warnings of the Lord's prophet.

As I approached, Zedekiah's eyes met mine, and in them I saw a momentary flash of recognition—not of me personally, but of what my presence signified. Another prophecy. Another warning. Another opportunity to save his people that he would squander through indecision and fear. He quickly looked away, focusing with exaggerated interest on the words of Hananiah the priest, who was regaling the gathering with assurances of divine deliverance.

"The Lord dwelleth in Zion," Hananiah was saying, his voice carrying the practiced cadence of one who has convinced himself of a pleasant falsehood. "He shall not forsake the city of His sanctuary. Pharaoh of Egypt hath vowed succour, and it shall come ere the ramparts yield. Take heart, O Sovereign! The God of Hosts contendeth for Jerusalem!"

A chorus of approving murmurs followed this pronouncement, but they rang hollow, like the sound of wind through dead branches. I clutched the scroll tighter, my knuckles whitening. The true word of the Lord burned within me, demanding voice.

"My lord King," I said, stepping forward into the circle of light cast by the braziers. "The prophet Jeremiah sendeth word."

A hush fell over the gathering, as if the name of Jeremiah himself carried a weight of dread. Zedekiah's face tightened, but he motioned for me to continue. I unrolled the scroll and read the words I had transcribed that very morning:

"Thus saith the Lord, the God of Israel: 'Deceive not yourselves, saying, "The Chaldeans shall surely depart from us," for they shall not depart. For though ye had smitten the whole army of the Chaldeans that fight against you, and there remained but wounded men among them, yet should they rise up every man in his tent, and burn this city with fire. Behold, I set before you the way of life, and the way of death. He that abideth in this city shall die by the sword, by the famine, and by the pestilence: but he that goeth out, and falleth to the Chaldeans that besiege you, he shall live, and his life shall be unto him for a prey. For I have set my face against this city for evil, and not for good, saith the Lord: it shall be given into the hand of the king of Babylon, and he shall burn it with fire.'"

The silence that followed my recitation was profound, a void into which no one seemed willing to speak. Then, like distant thunder, a low murmuring began among the nobles. It grew in volume and vitriol until one man—Shephatiah, son of Mattan, a courtier known for his hatred of Jeremiah—leapt to his feet.

"How long wilt thou suffer this traitor's minion to dishearten our kin?" he demanded, pointing an accusatory finger at me though his words were directed at the king. "The utterances of Jeremiah weaken the hands of the warriors and the people who abide in this city! This man seeketh not the weal of this people, but the woe thereof!"

Others joined in the chorus of condemnation. "He conspireth with the Chaldeans!" "He undermineth our resolve!" "Silence him ere all hope be lost!"

Zedekiah, his face a study in conflicted emotions, raised a hand to quiet the tumult. "What would ye have me do?" he asked, his voice betraying the weakness of a man who already knew the answer but hoped someone else would speak it for him.

"Cast him into the cistern," Shephatiah urged, his eyes gleaming with malice. "Both the prophet and his scribe. Let them await deliverance there, if they be so certain of our doom."

The king's shoulders sagged, whether in relief at having a decision made for him or in sorrow at his own cowardice, I could not tell. "Let it be as thou hast spoken," he murmured, not meeting my gaze. "Take the scribe to the cistern where his master awaiteth. Perchance there, in the silence and the gloom, they shall receive a more hopeful vision."

Rough hands seized me, and I did not resist. As they led me away, I saw the king turn back to his cup of wine, drinking deeply as if to drown the truth he could not bear to face. In that moment, I pitied him more than I feared for myself, for I knew the Lord was with Jeremiah and, by extension, with me. Zedekiah had no such comfort; he stood alone, a hollow crown upon a hollow man.

The cistern where they cast me was a place of profound darkness and clinging mud. Jeremiah was already there, having been consigned to this pit days earlier after a particularly dire prophecy. We sat together in the gloom, two voices of truth silenced by those who preferred comforting lies.

"They have read thy words, master," I told him, my voice echoing strangely in the confined space. "The king heard, but heeded the counsel of fools instead."

Jeremiah's sigh seemed to emanate not just from his lungs but from his very soul. "It is as the Lord hath shown me," he said. "This people hath loved to wander; they have not restrained their feet. Therefore, the Lord doth not accept them; He will now remember their iniquity, and visit their sins."

We fell silent then, conserving our strength in that dismal pit. Hours passed, perhaps days—time lost meaning in the unchanging darkness. Then, unexpectedly, a shaft of light pierced our prison as the cover was removed. A face appeared, silhouetted against the sky: Ebed-melech the Ethiopian, a eunuch in the king's service known for his kindness and his fear of the Lord.

"The king hath relented," he called down to us. "Or rather, he hath been persuaded. I have spoken on thy behalf, O prophet of God, and ye are to be drawn up from this pit of death."

Ropes were lowered, along with cloths to pad our armpits against the rough fibres. Slowly, painfully, we were drawn up into the light, blinking like creatures of the deep suddenly exposed to the sun. My legs, stiff from disuse and the cold mud, barely supported me as I was brought before the king once more.

Zedekiah had summoned Jeremiah to a private chamber, away from the poisonous influence of his counsellors. I accompanied my master, as was my duty, though I remained in the shadows, ready to record whatever transpired. The king's face was haggard, the crown sitting awkwardly upon his brow as if it had grown too heavy for his head.

"I would ask thee a thing," Zedekiah said to Jeremiah, his voice low and urgent. "Hide nothing from me."

Jeremiah, though weakened by his ordeal, stood straight before the king, his eyes clear and penetrating. "If I declare it unto thee, wilt thou not surely put me to death? And if I give thee counsel, wilt thou not hearken unto me?"

Zedekiah swore an oath: "As the Lord liveth, that made us this soul, I will not put thee to death, neither will I give thee into the hand of the men that seek thy life."

Only then did Jeremiah speak the word of the Lord once more: "Thus saith the Lord, the God of hosts, the God of Israel; If thou wilt assuredly go forth unto the king of Babylon's princes, then thy soul shall live, and this city shall not be burned with fire; and thou shalt live, and thine house: But if thou wilt not go forth to the king of Babylon's princes, then shall this city be given into the hand of the Chaldeans, and they shall burn it with fire, and thou shalt not escape out of their hand."

I watched the king's face as he absorbed these words. There was a moment—brief, but unmistakable—when resolve seemed to harden his features. For that fleeting instant, I dared to hope that he would at last embrace wisdom, that he would save his people through humility and surrender.

But then his expression crumbled, fear overwhelming whatever courage had momentarily taken root. "I am afraid of the Jews that are fallen to the Chaldeans," he confessed, his voice barely above a whisper. "lest they deliver me into their hand, and they mock me."

Jeremiah assured him, a note of desperation entering his voice. "They shall not deliver thee. Obey, I beseech thee, the voice of the Lord, which I speak unto thee: so it shall be well unto thee, and thy soul shall live."

But I could see that the moment had passed. The king's fear had mastered him once more. He dismissed us with a distracted wave, already retreating into the false security of his court's delusions. As we left the royal presence, Jeremiah turned to me, his eyes filled with a profound sadness.

"Write this," he instructed me. "Write that soon the walls shall fall, and the city with them. Write that the king shall flee by night with his household, but they shall be overtaken in the plains of Jericho. Write that Zedekiah's sons shall be slain before his eyes, and then his eyes shall be put out, that the last thing he beholdeth in this world may be the death of his lineage. Write that he shall be bound in chains and carried to Babylon, where he shall die. Write all this, that when it cometh to pass, the people may know that the Lord hath spoken."

I did as I was commanded, my heart heavy with the knowledge of what was to come. And come it did, with a terrible swiftness that spoke of divine wrath long held in check, now released in its full fury.

It was the ninth day of the fourth month, in the eleventh year of Zedekiah's reign, when the city wall was finally breached—the very day that fulfilled Jeremiah's prophecies of our doom. The Babylonian soldiers poured through the gap like floodwaters through a broken dam, their battle cries a cacophony that tore at the very fabric of our world. I remember the final, terrible breach as if it were yesterday rather than the distant past of nearly five decades. The Babylonian legions surged through the shattered northern gate like a tide of bronze and iron, their momentum unstoppable, their vengeance terrible.

Fire, the very breath of judgment, consumed our palaces, our homes, and then, with a roar that rent the heavens, the glorious Temple of Solomon, the House of the Lord, built by the wisdom of ages, crumbled into a pyre. The gold melted, running in rivulets between the paving stones; the sacred vessels were seized as plunder; the great bronze pillars, Jachin and Boaz, which had stood as sentinels at the Temple entrance since the days of Solomon, were broken apart for easier transport to Babylon.

I stood beside Jeremiah as the Temple burned, watching as centuries of sacred history were devoured by insatiable flames. The prophet wept openly, his tears carving clean tracks through the soot that covered all our faces. Yet even in his grief, there was a terrible acceptance, the recognition of divine justice long forewarned, now fulfilled.

"Write this," Jeremiah had instructed me that morning, his eyes reflecting the flames that consumed our city. "Write that the people may remember the judgment, but also the promise that follows."

And so I wrote, my hands steady even as my heart quailed within me. I recorded the fall of Jerusalem, the destruction of the Temple, the suffering of the people—all as Jeremiah had foretold. But there was more to come, events even more personal and painful than the fall of stone walls and wooden gates.

Zedekiah, true to his nature until the end, had indeed attempted flight. Under cover of darkness, he and his household, along with what remained of his royal guard, had slipped out through a small postern gate in the palace wall. They fled toward the Arabah, hoping perhaps to lose themselves in the wilderness, or to seek sanctuary with the Ammonites beyond the Jordan.

But the Lord's word would not be thwarted. The Babylonian scouts, alert and numerous, spotted the fleeing party. Swift riders gave chase, and on the plains of Jericho, where once our ancestors had triumphantly entered the Promised Land, the last king of David's line was captured like a common thief in the night.

Word reached us through those who had witnessed the king's capture and the events that followed. Zedekiah and his sons were brought to Riblah, where Nebuchadnezzar had established his field headquarters. There, the mighty king of Babylon sat in judgment upon the vassal who had betrayed his oath.

The scene is seared into my memory, though I witnessed it only through the accounts of others: Zedekiah, our last king, dragged before Nebuchadnezzar at Riblah. The Babylonian monarch, resplendent in his triumph, sat upon a portable throne of cedar inlaid with gold and ivory, his eyes cold as winter stars. His posture was not that of a conqueror made magnanimous by victory, but of a ruler deeply offended by betrayal, determined to make an example that would echo through generations.

"Behold the king who thought to defy the might of Babylon," Nebuchadnezzar declared, his voice carrying across the assembled host. "Behold the fool who brake his sacred oath and sought alliance with Egypt. Let his punishment be a lesson to all who would oppose the will of Nebuchadnezzar, king of kings, lord of the four quarters of the world."

What followed haunts my dreams still. Zedekiah's sons—mere boys, the youngest barely five summers—were brought forth, weeping and struggling in the grip of the Babylonian guards. The eldest, perhaps understanding what was to come, stood with a dignity beyond his years, chin raised in defiance even as tears streamed down his cheeks. The youngest clung to his brother, confusion and terror warring on his small face.

Before their father's horrified gaze, they were slaughtered, one by one, their blood staining the dusty earth of Riblah. Each death was deliberate, unhurried, ensuring that Zedekiah would bear witness to every moment of his line's extinction. The guards who carried out this grim work were not cruel men by nature—I could see the conflict in their eyes—but they were soldiers, bound to obey, and the king of Babylon had decreed this punishment.

"No!" Zedekiah's anguished cry seemed to pierce the very heavens. "Take me in their stead! They are but innocent children!"

Nebuchadnezzar merely watched, unmoved, as a sculptor might observe the progress of his chisel upon stone. When the last prince had fallen, the Babylonian king gestured to his guards. "Now," he commanded, "let this be the final sight his eyes shall behold."

The guards moved forward with cruel efficiency, their bronze daggers glinting in the merciless sun. Zedekiah's screams echoed across the plain as they took his sight, leaving him in permanent darkness, with only the memory of his sons' deaths to accompany him into exile. They bound him in bronze fetters, a blinded, childless king, the last of his line—or so the world believed.

The Davidic line, which God had sworn would endure forever, seemed utterly extinguished, its sceptre fallen, its throne overturned. The promise spoken to David—"Thy house and thy kingdom shall be established for ever before thee: thy throne shall be established for ever"—now seemed as hollow as the wind whistling through the burned ruins of Jerusalem.

Yet, even amidst this desolation, a flicker of hope remained, a promise whispered by the Lord to His servant Jeremiah, a secret kept from the mighty Nebuchadnezzar, whose triumph was not as complete as he imagined.

In the days following Jerusalem's fall, while the city still smouldered and the survivors wandered like ghosts among the ruins, Jeremiah received a visitor. Nebuzaradan, the captain of Nebuchadnezzar's guard and the officer charged with supervising the destruction of Jerusalem, came seeking the prophet. I was present, transcribing the meeting as was my duty as scribe.

Nebuzaradan was not what I had expected of a Babylonian commander. He was a man of middle years, his face weathered by sun and war, but his eyes held an unexpected thoughtfulness. His armour, though of finest craftsmanship, bore the marks of actual battle, not merely the ornamental polish of a court officer. He regarded Jeremiah with what appeared to be genuine respect—perhaps even awe.

"Thou art the prophet who counselled submission to Babylon," he said, his Aramaic heavily accented but comprehensible. "Thou didst speak truth when others peddled lies."

Jeremiah bowed his head slightly. "I spake only what the Lord commanded me to speak."

Nebuzaradan considered this, stroking his beard thoughtfully. There was genuine curiosity in his expression, the look of a man confronted with a philosophy both foreign and intriguing. "Whatever the case," he said at length, "my lord Nebuchadnezzar hath instructed that thou be treated with honor. Thou mayest go where thou wilt—to Babylon, where thou shalt be given a stipend and comfortable quarters, or thou mayest remain here under the governance of Gedaliah, whom the king hath appointed as governor."

It was a remarkable offer, especially in the wake of such destruction. Most conquered peoples were given no choice; they were either slaughtered or enslaved according to the whim of their conquerors. That Jeremiah—a citizen of the defeated nation—should be given such consideration spoke volumes about the respect his prophecies had earned, even among enemies.

Jeremiah's answer, however, surprised even me, who had long grown accustomed to the prophet's unexpected pronouncements. "I thank thy lord for his generosity," the prophet said, "yet mine work here is not finished. There are yet those among my people who have need of spiritual guidance in these dark days. I would remain to minister unto the remnant that surviveth."

Nebuzaradan frowned, clearly puzzled by any man who would refuse comfort in Babylon for the desolation of Judah, yet he nodded. "As thou wishest. Thou shalt have the protection of Babylon so long as thou dost cause no trouble."

After the Babylonian had departed, I turned to Jeremiah, my curiosity overcoming my usual reserve. "Master, what didst thou mean? What planting remains in this wasteland?"

We stood amidst the ruins of what had once been a thriving marketplace. Around us, the few civilians who had survived the city's fall picked through the debris, seeking anything of value that might have escaped the Babylonian plunderers. The air was thick with dust and the acrid scent of burnt timber. It seemed an unlikely place for any kind of planting or growth.

Jeremiah's eyes, however, held a light I had not seen in years, a gleam of divine purpose that transcended our present sorrow. "The Lord God, though He chastiseth His people, doth not abandon His covenant," he said, his voice low but vibrant with conviction. "The seed of David, though seemingly crushed, is not utterly lost."

He drew me closer, lowering his voice still further. "The Lord hath shown me a land veiled in mist and ancient song, an isle of emerald green far to the west, beyond the pillars of Hercules. It is there that the sceptre of Judah shall be replanted, there that the throne of David shall continue until the coming of Him to whom it rightfully belongeth."

As he spoke these words, a strange peace settled over me, a certainty that transcended the devastation surrounding us. The fall of Jerusalem, the destruction of the Temple, the exile of our people—these were not the end of the story, but merely the closing of one chapter and the opening of another. The Lord's purposes, like the roots of a mighty cedar, ran deeper than mortal eyes could see and spread further than mortal minds could comprehend.

In that moment, standing amid the ashes of all we had known, I felt the first stirrings of a hope that would carry us across lands and seas, through perils and wonders, to a destiny as yet unrevealed but divinely assured. The last days of Zedekiah's reign had ended in fire and blood, but from those ashes, a new chapter in the eternal covenant between God and His people was about to begin—a chapter that I, Baruch ben Neriah, would be privileged to record for generations yet unborn.

The sun was setting over the broken walls of Jerusalem, casting long shadows across the ruins. As darkness fell, I looked to Jeremiah, whose weathered face now bore a look not of defeat, but of determined resolve. "Come," he said, gathering his meagre possessions. "We have much to prepare, and little time remaineth. The daughters of Zedekiah await, and with them, the future of the covenant."
 
Chapter 2: The Prophet's Burden

As chronicled by Baruch ben Neriah

In which Jeremiah leads the surviving royal daughters and a remnant from Jerusalem, preparing for a journey to fulfil the Davidic covenant in exile

Dawn broke over Jerusalem with a sickly, jaundiced light that seemed to emanate not from the sun but from the still-smouldering ruins themselves. It had been seven days since the fall of the city, seven days since the Temple had collapsed in flames, seven days of wandering among the ashes of what had once been the glory of Israel. The air remained thick with dust and the cloying stench of death—a miasma that clung to our clothing, our skin, our very souls.

I found Jeremiah where I had left him the previous evening: kneeling amid the rubble of what had once been the Chamber of Hewn Stone, where the Sanhedrin had met in more peaceful times. His weathered hands sifted through the debris, occasionally setting aside a fragment of scroll or a shard of ceremonial vessel with reverent care. Though nearing his seventieth year, the prophet moved with the deliberate energy of a man half his age, driven by a purpose that transcended mere physical endurance.

"Master," I called softly, offering him the small bundle of bread and dried figs I had managed to procure from a Babylonian supply wagon. The occupiers, surprisingly, had begun distributing food to the surviving inhabitants—perhaps out of pragmatism rather than mercy, for dead subjects cannot pay tribute.

Jeremiah looked up, his eyes red-rimmed from smoke and sleeplessness, but clear with an inner fire that had sustained him through decades of ignored prophecies and persecution. "Ah, Baruch," he said, his voice hoarse. "Come, sit. There is much to discuss."

I settled beside him, watching as he broke the bread with fingers stained black with soot. He offered me half, which I accepted with quiet gratitude. We ate in silence for a moment, the simple act of nourishment a reminder that life, however altered, continued even in the wake of catastrophe.

"The immediate aftermath of Jerusalem's fall is a chaos of ash and despair," I observed, my scribe's instinct already framing our present circumstances in words meant for future generations. "Remnants of our people wander through the ruins, their eyes hollow with grief."

Jeremiah nodded, his gaze sweeping over the devastation that stretched in every direction. "Yea, but grief must not blind us to purpose. The Lord hath not preserved us merely to lament. There is work yet to be done, Baruch. Vital work."

I had known Jeremiah since my youth, had served as his scribe and companion through the tumultuous reigns of four kings. I had transcribed his prophecies of doom when prosperity made them seem absurd; I had recorded his counsel of submission when rebellion seemed the only path to freedom. I had watched as each dire warning was fulfilled with terrible precision. Now, amid the ruins of all he had tried to prevent, I recognized the familiar gleam of divine communication in his eyes.

"What work, master? What purpose remains in this wasteland?"

Instead of answering directly, Jeremiah rose and beckoned me to follow. We picked our way through the rubble-strewn streets, past hollow-eyed survivors who scavenged among the debris, past Babylonian soldiers who watched with impassive vigilance from makeshift guard posts. The city that had once hummed with commerce and worship and the everyday sounds of life was now a place of whispers and weeping, of shuffling feet and the occasional crash as weakened structures continued to collapse under their own weight.

Jeremiah led me to a small dwelling near the city's eastern wall—one of the few structures left relatively intact by the Babylonian destruction. It had once belonged to Huldah the prophetess, I recalled, though she had died some years before. Now it served as a refuge for those whom Jeremiah had gathered under his protection.

At our approach, a figure emerged from the doorway—Ebed-melech the Ethiopian, the courageous eunuch who had rescued Jeremiah from the cistern when Zedekiah's officials had left him to die. His dark face bore new scars from the fighting, but his eyes lit with relief at the sight of us.

"They have come, my lord," he said to Jeremiah, his Aramaic heavily accented but clear. "Just before dawn. They are resting now."

Jeremiah nodded, a weight seeming to lift from his shoulders. "The Lord is faithful," he murmured. "Even in judgment, He remembereth mercy."

We entered the humble dwelling, the dimness within a relief after the harsh light outside. As my eyes adjusted, I discerned several figures huddled on pallets in the far corner—women, from their dress, attended by a pair of haggard-looking maidservants who dabbed their brows with damp cloths.

"Who are they?" I whispered to Jeremiah.

"Hope," he replied simply. "Come, let me show thee the future of the House of David."

We approached cautiously, so as not to startle the newcomers. As we drew closer, I could see that the central figures were young women—girls, really—perhaps sixteen and fourteen summers old respectively. Their garments, though travel-stained and torn, were of fine material, and even in their disheveled state, they carried themselves with the unconscious dignity of those born to privilege.

"These are the daughters of King Zedekiah," Jeremiah said softly. "Tea Tephi, the elder, and Scota, the younger. They were spirited away from the palace before the final breach, by the foresight and courage of their governess."

I stared in disbelief. It had been reported—and I had recorded in my chronicles—that all of Zedekiah's children had perished at Riblah, slaughtered before their father's eyes before he himself was blinded. To find any of the royal lineage alive seemed a miracle beyond hope.

"How...?" I began.

"The Lord preserveth a remnant," he replied. "Always a remnant. Come, let me show thee the future of the House of David."

The elder girl, Tea Tephi, stirred at the sound of our voices. She sat up, her movements deliberate and controlled despite her obvious exhaustion. Her face, though smudged with dirt and marked by recent tears, bore a striking resemblance to her father—the same high forehead and aquiline nose that marked the Davidic line. But while Zedekiah's eyes had always held a hint of indecision, a wavering that had been his undoing, Tea Tephi's gaze was direct and steady, a flicker of Davidic fire burning behind the shock and grief.

"Prophet," she acknowledged Jeremiah, her voice soft but clear. "The mercy of the Lord is indeed great, that He hath brought us unto thee in safety."

"Princess Tea Tephi," Jeremiah bowed slightly, according her the respect due to her station. "Thy journey hath been arduous, but thou art safe now."

"Safe?" Her laugh held no mirth. "Is any place safe now? Our city lieth in ruins, our people scattered, our father..." Her voice broke, and for a moment I glimpsed the frightened child beneath the composed exterior. "They say he hath been blinded and taken in chains to Babylon."

"It is true," Jeremiah confirmed gently. "The judgment that fell upon thy father was severe, but it was long foretold. He brake his oath to Nebuchadnezzar, an oath sworn in the name of the Lord. Such faithlessness could not go unpunished."

"And my brothers? We heard rumours on the road..."

"They are gone," Jeremiah said, his own voice thick with sorrow. "The Lord hath permitted the immediate kingship in Judah to be ended, as a sign of His judgment upon this generation for their persistent unfaithfulness. The male line of succession in Jerusalem is cut off, but the eternal covenant itself remains unbroken."

"Then it is finished," Tea Tephi whispered. "The covenant is broken. The promise to David—that his throne would endure forever—hath come to nothing."

"Nay." Jeremiah's voice rang with sudden authority, causing even the slumbering Scota to stir. "The covenant standeth eternally secure, for it was made unconditionally by the Lord Himself. Though individual kings may be disciplined and earthly thrones overturned, the ultimate promise to David remaineth unshakeable, and its fulfilment shall come through channels none have foreseen."

"What nonsense is this?" she demanded, sitting up abruptly. "What inheritance remaineth to us? Our home is ash, our family scattered or dead. We are refugees, dependent on charity!"

"Peace, sister," Tea Tephi murmured, placing a gentle hand on Scota's arm. "This is Jeremiah, the prophet of whom Father spake. He tried to warn our people of what was to come."

"Much good his warnings did," Scota retorted bitterly. "Where was the Lord when the Babylonians breached our walls? Where was He when our brothers were slaughtered?"

"Where He hath always been," Jeremiah replied, unruffled by the girl's outburst. "Sovereign over all, working His purpose even through the darkest hours of judgment. The Lord chastiseth those He loveth, Princess Scota. This calamity hath come because thy father and his predecessors turned from His ways, not because He abandoned His people."

"I feel... something," she whispered. "A connection. As if the stone knoweth me."

"It recognizeth the blood of the covenant that floweth in thy veins," Jeremiah affirmed. "This stone hath served as the coronation seat for the kings of Israel since the time of Solomon. Though the Temple is fallen and Jerusalem laid waste, this sacred object remaineth as a tangible link to the promises of God. And now, it must journey with us to a new land, where it will once again bear the weight of David's descendants upon a throne established forever."

"Our father spake of this," she murmured. "He said King David composed his psalms upon this very instrument, that its music could soothe troubled spirits and give voice to the deepest longings of the heart."

"Yea," Jeremiah nodded, pleased by her recognition. "And though it lieth silent now, the day will come when its music will rise again in a distant land, heralding the continuation of thy father's line."

"What dost thou mean, Prophet? What building remains possible in this desolation?"

"Not here," Jeremiah replied, his eyes still holding that faraway look of one who sees beyond the present circumstance. "The Lord hath shown me a land veiled in mist and ancient song, an isle of emerald green far to the west, beyond the pillars of Hercules. It is there that the sceptre of Judah shall be replanted, there that the throne of David shall continue until the coming of Him to whom it rightfully belongeth."

"Thou speakest in riddles, old man. An isle beyond the pillars of Hercules? Thou wouldst have us journey to the very edge of the world based on a dream?"

"Not a dream," Jeremiah corrected gently. "A vision from the Lord, as clear and certain as any I have ever received. The covenant with David will not fail, though its path now turneth in unexpected directions."

"This journey thou dost propose," she said carefully, "to this isle beyond the western sea—it would be arduous even for seasoned travellers, let alone for a company that includeth women and children. How are we to accomplish such a thing? We have no ships, no resources, no protection beyond these walls."

"The Lord who parted the Red Sea for Moses can surely make a way where there seemeth to be none. But thou art right to consider the practical challenges. We cannot remain in Jerusalem—Nebuchadnezzar's governor, Gedaliah, though a just man, cannot protect us indefinitely, especially once it becometh known that daughters of Zedekiah still live."

"Egypt," I suggested. "Many of our people flee there, seeking refuge from Babylonian rule. Perhaps we could join one of the caravans heading south, lose ourselves among the multitude until we can find passage across the Great Sea."

"I have prophesied against seeking safety in Egypt. 'Those who go down to Egypt for help are doomed,' saith the Lord. The sword that ye fear will overtake thee there."

"Not to remain," I clarified quickly, "but as a waystation. From Egypt's ports, we might secure passage on a westward-bound vessel."

"The Lord worketh in unexpected ways. Perhaps this is indeed the path He hath prepared. We shall journey to Egypt, but not to settle. Our destiny lieth beyond, in lands yet unseen."

"This is no small burden you have taken upon yourself," I observed quietly. "To shepherd two royal princesses, accustomed to luxury and courtly life, through a shattered land, then across perilous seas—it would test the strongest of men, let alone one who has already borne the weight of prophecy for four decades."

"The Lord doth not call the equipped, Baruch; He equippeth the called. My strength alone would indeed fail, but 'they that wait upon the Lord shall renew their strength; they shall mount up with wings as eagles; they shall run, and not be weary; and they shall walk, and not faint.'"

"Still, the practical challenges remain daunting. How shall we transport these sacred relics safely? How shall we protect the princesses from those who would do them harm? How shall we find this distant isle of thy vision?"

"We shall gather a small, loyal company," Jeremiah replied. "Not many—a large group would attract unwanted attention—but enough to provide protection and assistance. Thou, of course, must come as scribe and witness, to record the fulfilment of prophecy for future generations. Ebed-melech hath already pledged his service; his strength and courage will be invaluable. There are others, too, who still cling to the Lord's truth amidst the apostasy—we will find them in the days to come."

"As for the journey itself, we must trust in divine guidance. The same God who led our ancestors through the wilderness with a pillar of cloud by day and fire by night can surely direct our steps across the Great Sea."

"We are a fragile remnant," I mused, "a tiny ark carrying the most precious cargo: the hope of Israel, the promise of an everlasting throne."

"Yea," Jeremiah agreed, "and though the way be long and fraught with perils, the outcome is assured. The Lord hath sworn by His own name that David's throne shall endure forever. Through Tea Tephi and Scota, that promise will find fulfilment in ways none could have foreseen."

"Remember," he instructed quietly, "we are simply a family group fleeing the destruction of Jerusalem, seeking safety in Egypt like so many others. Speak to no one unless absolutely necessary. Keep your heads down and your voices low."

"Thou, Baruch, must continue to record our journey, but discreetly. The account thou dost compile will one day serve as testimony to the Lord's faithfulness across generations."

"I will not fail in my duty, master."

""Daughters of David, the path before us is long and fraught with perils. There will be times when thy courage faileth and thy faith wavereth. In those moments, remember who ye are and what ye carry within thee—not just royal blood, but the very covenant of God, the promise of an enduring throne. Though kingdoms rise and fall around thee, though the world itself seemeth to crumble, that promise standeth immutable."

"But how can we, being daughters, carry forward a royal line that passeth through males?" Tea Tephi asked with the practical concern of one who understood protocol.

"Remember the daughters of Zelophehad," Jeremiah replied gently, "who came before Moses when their father died leaving no sons. The Lord commanded that when a man dieth having no son, his inheritance shall pass to his daughter. This precedent, established in the Law, provideth the path by which David's covenant continueth through his female descendants when the male line is cut off. The promise endureth, even when it must flow through unexpected channels.""

"We will remember, Prophet."

"Am I to dress like a peasant, then?" she demanded, the imperious tone of a lifetime of privilege reasserting itself.

"Thou art to dress like one who wisheth to remain alive," Jeremiah replied mildly. "The Babylonians still search for any surviving members of the royal household. Thy fine clothes would mark thee as surely as a herald's announcement."

"He is right, sister. Father would want us to survive, even if it meaneth sacrificing our pride."

And so, with the last light fading from the western sky, we set forth to gather the scattered remnants of David's line and to begin our long journey toward a distant shore where ancient promises would find new fulfilment. Behind us lay the ashes of Jerusalem; before us, though we could not yet see it, lay the dawn of a new age, the planting of a royal seed in soil far from the land of its fathers but no less blessed by the hand of the Lord God of Israel.
 
Chapter 3: Chaos in the Camp of the Remnants

From the Chronicles and Collected Accounts of Baruch ben Neriah

In which Scota's adventure outside the refugee camp leads to a fateful encounter with Gathelus and new alliances on the Egyptian frontier

I, Baruch ben Neriah, scribe to the prophet Jeremiah, have taken great pains to faithfully record not only those events which I witnessed with my own eyes, but also those related to me by trusted companions and participants. What follows is such an account—a tapestry woven from multiple threads: my own observations at our camp near Migdol, Princess Scota's vivid retelling of her adventure as she later confided it to me, and the testimonies of those who witnessed the pivotal moments when I could not be present. Though not all words herein were spoken exactly as written, I have endeavoured to capture the essence of truth in these events, as befits a chronicler of our people's journey.

The shadow of dusk settled upon our encampment near Migdol, that ancient fortress on the Egyptian frontier, casting long, sombre silhouettes against the ruddy sands. Our fractious exodus from the smouldering ruins of Jerusalem had been determined not by divine wisdom, but by the stubborn will of men who feared Babylon more than they trusted the living God.

Johanan ben Kareah, once a captain of valour in the Jewish remnant, had, against the vehement prophecies of Jeremiah, led our beleaguered company out of Judea. His destination: the fortress of Migdol, that ancient bulwark upon the Egyptian frontier. The journey had been arduous, the terrain unforgiving, yet perhaps the most difficult aspect had been the weight of knowing we travelled against the express command of the Lord.

I witnessed with my own eyes how Jeremiah had stood before the captains, his aged frame quivering with the intensity of divine revelation. The desert wind had whipped his greying beard, and his eyes, usually so gentle, had burned with prophetic fire. "Thus saith the Lord," he had proclaimed, his voice rising above the murmurs of dissent, "The sword that ye feared will overtake you there, and the famine of which ye were afraid will follow close after you in Egypt, and there ye shall die."

Yet the captains, their hearts hardened by fear and pride, had refused to heed the prophet's warning. They saw safety in Egypt's might, in Pharaoh's chariots and horsemen, forgetting that it was from Egypt's bondage that the Lord had once delivered their forefathers. And so, they dragged us all toward the very peril God had forbidden, their souls deaf to the echoes of history, blind to the patterns that had defined Israel's relationship with the Almighty since the days of Moses.

Our camp now lay in the shadow of Migdol's ancient walls, a temporary haven that felt more like a prison to those of us who knew the truth of Jeremiah's prophecy. The air was thick with the cacophony of fear and defiance – arguments that circled endlessly like carrion birds over a battlefield.

On the particular evening when our tale begins, I was seated near the prophet Jeremiah as he conversed with Princess Tea Tephi, the elder daughter of the ill-fated King Zedekiah. Even in our reduced circumstances, she maintained a quiet dignity that belied her youthful years. Her slender fingers worked a small loom with practiced precision, her brow furrowed in concern as she engaged in hushed, earnest discourse with the prophet. Her hair, as black as a starless night, was bound in a simple braid, adorned only with a single ribbon of royal blue – a subtle reminder of her lineage, of the blood of David that flowed through her veins.

"The captains speak of seeking an audience with Pharaoh Hophra," she murmured to Jeremiah, her voice low but resolute. "They believe he will grant us sanctuary, perhaps even restore a semblance of our former glory."

Jeremiah's weathered hand rested upon his staff, his knuckles white with tension. "They seek refuge in the shadow of reeds," he replied, his tone heavy with sorrow. "The Lord hath shown me that Egypt shall be but a temporary respite, Princess. The hand of Babylon shall reach even here, for Nebuchadnezzar is but an instrument of divine judgment, and no earthly kingdom can stand against the decree of the Most High."

Tea Tephi nodded solemnly, her eyes reflecting wisdom beyond her years. Unlike many who had dismissed Jeremiah's prophecies, she had witnessed their fulfilment in the terrible fall of Jerusalem. She had seen the mighty walls breached, the Temple – that magnificent House of the Lord – consumed by ravenous flames. She had watched as her father, the once-proud King Zedekiah, was brought low, forced to witness the slaughter of his sons before his own eyes were cruelly put out. Only she and her younger sister, Scota, had been spared, hidden away by loyal servants until Jeremiah could safely retrieve them from the chaos.

I heard Tea Tephi's voice change suddenly, concern evident in her tone. "What of my sister?" she inquired, realizing that Scota was not within sight. Her dark eyes scanned the encampment with growing worry. "Hast thou seen her, Prophet?"

Jeremiah's gaze followed hers, but Scota was nowhere to be found among the cluttered tents and bustling refugees. This momentary inattention was unusual for Tea Tephi, who had taken on the mantle of guardian for her younger sibling since their father's capture. Yet the weight of responsibility that now rested upon her slender shoulders – as the last acknowledged heir of David's line – often consumed her thoughts, leaving little room for the vigilance required to keep track of her headstrong sister.

As Jeremiah summoned me closer and began to instruct me to organize a search, I could not yet know the extraordinary events unfolding beyond our camp—events that Scota would later recount to me in such vivid detail that I feel compelled to record them here as if I had witnessed them myself. To understand what transpired that evening, one must first understand Scota herself.

According to all who knew her, Scota, younger daughter of King Zedekiah, was a spirit untamed by convention or tragedy. Where Tea Tephi was thoughtful and measured, Scota was impulsive and bold. Where the elder sister carried herself with the quiet gravitas of royalty, the younger embodied the wild, curious energy of youth. Though born to the same royal household, their temperaments could not have been more different.

Even in their reduced circumstances, Scota maintained a fierce love for the finer things that had once adorned her father's palace. The exquisite silks from distant lands, the fragrant oils pressed from rare flowers, the delicate jewels that had once adorned her mother's throat – these memories she cherished. Yet paradoxically, she disdained the sheltered existence that had been her birthright, chafing against the constraints of royal decorum like a desert horse against an unwanted bridle.

Her spirit was as free and untameable as the wild desert winds, her curiosity as boundless as the azure skies that stretched above the arid landscape. She listened to no voice of authority save two: her beloved sister Tea, whom she adored with the pure, uncomplicated love of a younger sibling, and the prophet Jeremiah, whom she had come to regard more as a true father than King Zedekiah had ever been. The king, though not unkind, had been a distant figure, consumed by matters of state and the political machinations that ultimately led to Jerusalem's downfall. Jeremiah, with his gentle wisdom and unwavering faith, had filled that void in her young heart.

As Scota would later tell it, on this particular evening, the endless arguments in the camp – the shouting, the cursing, the palpable despair – had begun to grate upon her sensitive nerves. The dust that permeated everything, the cramped conditions, the sheer monotony of their refugee existence – all of it had become suddenly, overwhelmingly intolerable.

According to her own account, which she related to me many evenings later with a curious mixture of pride and sheepish acknowledgment, seeing her chance while her sister was deep in conversation with Jeremiah, a familiar mischievous glint illuminated her dark eyes.

"I had reached my limit, Baruch," she confided to me, her expressive hands punctuating her words. "The endless arguments, the dust that found its way into everything, the crushing monotony of our existence—I needed to breathe, even for a moment."

With practiced stealth, she slipped into her modest tent and emerged moments later, clad in one of her manservant's drab woollen cloaks, its voluminous hood pulled low over her face, concealing her delicate features and the telltale cascade of ebony hair that marked her as royalty.

"The rough fabric itched terribly against my skin," she recalled with a wry smile. "So different from the fine linens I had once worn in Jerusalem's palace. But it served my purpose well—I became just another shadow in a camp full of refugees."

Like a desert fox slipping between the scattered stones of a mountainside (her own comparison, which she used with particular relish), Scota moved through the encampment, her light footsteps barely disturbing the sand beneath. Neither her ever-fussing maidservants nor the distracted guards noted her passing. The wilderness of Migdol stretched before her, vast and untamed, a canvas of golden sands and distant, hazy mountains that called to her adventurous spirit.

"The moment I crossed that invisible boundary between the ordered chaos of the camp and the wild freedom of the desert," she told me, her eyes alight with the memory, "my heart soared with exhilaration. I felt truly alive for the first time since Jerusalem fell."

The landscape unfolded before her like a mysterious scroll, revealing wonders she had only glimpsed from afar. Ancient, weathered rocks sculpted by countless years of wind and sand into fantastic shapes; sparse, resilient vegetation that defied the harsh conditions, pushing green tendrils up through the arid soil; distant gazelles moving with liquid grace across the golden plain. Her mind was captivated by this unfamiliar world, so different from the stone streets and enclosed gardens of Jerusalem.

"I lost all sense of time," she admitted. "Each new discovery led to another, each ridge revealing a vista more captivating than the last. It was only when I noticed the sun's position in the sky, its golden light gradually taking on the warmer hues of approaching evening, that a prickle of unease finally penetrated my absorption with the desert's wonders."
With a start, Scota realized she had travelled much farther than intended, and worse, had paid little attention to landmarks that might guide her return. She turned, scanning the horizon in the direction she believed the camp must lie, but the uniform desert landscape offered few distinguishing features.

"My heart began to race," she recounted, her voice dropping to a whisper as if reliving that moment of dawning horror. "I tried to retrace my steps, but the wind had already erased my footprints from the sand. It was then I knew with a sinking feeling in the pit of my stomach that I was utterly, hopelessly lost."

While Scota faced her predicament in the desert, back at our camp, I witnessed Jeremiah intervene in yet another dispute over provisions. He raised his hands for silence, his voice carrying the weight of divine authority. "The Lord hath brought us out of Jerusalem's destruction not to perish in petty squabbles but to fulfil His greater purpose. Each person shall receive an equal share, according to the number of mouths in their family. Those who are able shall contribute their labour according to their skills and strength. This is the way of justice, the path of the Law that was given to Moses at Sinai."

But my thoughts were increasingly drawn to the absent princess. As Jeremiah resolved the immediate conflict, I approached him quietly. "Prophet, what of Princess Scota? She has been gone too long."

It was then that our search began in earnest—though we could not know that Scota's adventure was already unfolding in ways none of us could have imagined.

According to her later account, the approaching dusk brought a chill to the desert air, a stark contrast to the day's heat. The beautiful landscape that had so enthralled her now seemed suddenly vast and indifferent to her plight.

"I am not easily given to fear," she insisted when telling me this part of her tale, her chin lifting with that characteristic pride. "I am, after all, a daughter of kings, with the blood of warriors and rulers flowing through my veins. But as the shadows lengthened and strange, unfamiliar sounds began to emerge from the gathering darkness, I felt a tendril of anxiety curling around my heart."

Just as despair threatened to overwhelm her young spirit, a beacon of hope appeared on the distant horizon: fires, a cluster of them, flickering like earthbound stars against the deepening gloom. Relief flooded through her veins, washing away the encroaching fear.

"I actually spoke aloud to myself," she admitted with a small laugh. "'Ah, I have found our camp at last!' I quickened my pace toward those welcome lights, my spirits soaring with relief."

As she drew nearer, however, that initial relief gradually transformed into confusion, then bewilderment. The configuration of the fires was wrong, the layout of tents unfamiliar. And the banners—here Scota's eyes had lit up with scholarly enthusiasm as she described them to me—were unlike any she had ever seen.

"If there is one subject in which I pride myself on expertise," she said without a trace of false modesty, "it is the identification of noble houses' heraldic symbols, emblems, and banners. From my earliest years, I could discern the lineage of a distant chieftain from a single, faded standard fluttering in the wind. But the flags before me that night were utterly alien to my experienced eye."

One prominently displayed banner depicted a clenched hand with a scarlet cord bound tightly around its wrist—a symbol she had never encountered in all her studies of Near Eastern heraldry. Others bore the sigil of a rampant lion, rendered in bold red against a golden field, crowned majestically upon its noble head.

"I remember whispering to myself, 'Naught resembling Egyptian or Babylonian heraldry have I seen. Perchance Greek?'" she recalled. "My scholarly curiosity momentarily overtook my fear, though I remained hidden behind a rocky outcropping, unwilling to reveal myself until I knew more about these strangers."

Scota's account of what happened next remains one of the most vivid tales I have recorded in these chronicles.

As she pondered the mystery of the strange camp from the relative safety of a rocky outcropping, a flash of reflected light caught her eye – moonlight gleaming off what appeared to be metal armour. Squinting into the deepening twilight, she discerned a man, tall and commanding in bearing, dismounting from an impressive steed at the camp's perimeter.

"Even at that distance," she later told me, her voice taking on an unusual softness, "I could see he was powerfully built, his proportions those of a warrior in his prime. When he removed his helmet, revealing hair the colour of burnished copper in the firelight..." Here she had paused, a faint blush colouring her cheeks. "My heart inexplicably quickened its pace."

Curiosity, that most dominant trait in Scota's character, overwhelmed caution. She moved closer to the mysterious encampment, employing all the stealth she had perfected during years of slipping away from her minders in the palace gardens. She darted from shadow to shadow, using the scattered vegetation and rock formations for cover, until she reached what she deemed a safe viewing distance.

"I was impressed despite my predicament," she recounted. "The camp was arranged with military precision—tents pitched in exact formation; sentries posted at regular intervals—everything speaking of discipline and organization. 'This is certainly no bandit camp,' I remember thinking. 'A professional military force, and well-supplied at that.'"

Her attention was drawn irresistibly back to the copper-haired warrior. He had moved to a more central location in the camp and was now removing his heavy battle armour. The firelight played across his form, revealing a physique that might have been carved by a master sculptor – broad shoulders, a chest defined by years of martial training, arms corded with muscle.

"I remember gasping softly," she admitted, that blush deepening as she told this part. "'A cuirass of unfamiliar design, and that strange emblem again...' I told myself I was merely making scholarly observations, but..." She laughed at her own youthful folly. "I was rather more focused on those muscled arms and that rock-hard chest than on any scholarly pursuits."

So absorbed was she in her study of the foreign warrior that she failed to notice the subtle shift in the shadows behind her, the silent approach of a seasoned scout who had marked her presence minutes earlier. It was only when his voice, low and amused, sounded mere inches from her ear that she realized her peril.

"Well, what have we got here? A shadow fiend?"

Before Scota could react, she found herself unceremoniously hoisted into the air, suspended by the back of her borrowed cloak as if she weighed no more than a kitten. Her feet dangled uselessly above the ground as her captor laughed, a deep, resonant sound.

"My humiliation was complete," she related, her eyes flashing at the memory. "There I was, a daughter of David's line, dangling like a rabbit caught by a hunter!"

Her captor raised his voice, calling out to the very warrior she had been watching. "Ey, Captain!" he shouted cheerfully, his grip on her cloak unwavering despite her struggles. "We have a dirty, spying desert fox in our midst!"

Outraged by this indignity, Scota thrashed about with all the ferocity her slender form could muster, trying in vain to reach her captor's face with clawed fingers. "Who art thou calling a dirty fox, thou... thou ugly oaf!" she sputtered indignantly. "Unhand me, thou ignorant brute!"

The soldier, a burly man with a thick beard and mischievous eyes, only laughed harder at her fierce resistance. "I was mistaken, Captain!" he called out, his voice carrying across the now-silent camp. "We have a dirty, spying desert vixen!"

To Scota's mortification, the very warrior she had been so intently "observing" (she would later insist to us that she had merely been conducting a strategic reconnaissance of potential threats) now approached with long, purposeful strides. Up close, he was even more imposing – and, she had to admit, even more handsome. His shirtless torso gleamed in the firelight; each muscle defined with the precision of a Greek statue. His face, framed by that unusual copper-gold hair, was arresting: strong jaw, aquiline nose, and most striking of all, piercing green eyes that seemed to look straight through her flimsy disguise to the princess beneath.

Her struggles ceased as those eyes locked with hers, not out of submission but out of a strange, sudden breathlessness that she couldn't explain. Gathering her scattered dignity, she pulled back her hood with her free hand, revealing her face – lovely despite the smudges of dust that marred her fair complexion – and addressed the warrior in the most imperious tone she could muster.

"Art thou the potentate in charge of this rabble?" she demanded, hiding her nervousness and trying her best not to blush from embarrassment as she uncovered her hood, revealing her lovely, albeit dirt-ridden, face. "Do not just stand there like a mute statue! Tell thy oafish minion here to put me down and release me at once!" her voice sharp as a Damascus blade.

Gathelus – for that was the warrior's name, though Scota did not yet know it – observed the dangling intruder with an expression that hovered somewhere between stoic amusement and genuine surprise. There was something about this girl, this desert fox as his scout had aptly named her, that caught him off guard. Despite her dishevelled state and the absurdity of her position, she possessed a beauty unlike any he had encountered in his travels across the known world. Her eyes, though currently flashing with indignation, held an unmistakable intelligence, and her voice, though pitched with anger, carried a melodic quality that resonated oddly within his battle-hardened heart.

"I was trying so desperately to sound royal and commanding," she confessed with a rueful laugh, "though the effect was somewhat diminished by my dangling feet and dishevelled appearance."

To her surprise, the copper-haired captain seemed more amused than offended. He gestured to the sentry, who lowered her to the ground, though he kept a firm grip on her cloak to prevent escape.

"Potentate, thou sayest?" he replied at length, a hint of dry humour softening his otherwise stern countenance. "I am Gathelus, captain of this host thou dost call a rabble." He paused, studying her with those penetrating green eyes. "And who might thou be, little spying fox?"

Then, as if struck by a sudden thought, he added with surprising urgency, "Oh, but ere thou dost answer, thou might want to step a smidgen closer to me."

Scota, finally released from her undignified suspension and now busily dusting off her borrowed cloak with exaggerated movements, gave him a look of pure scepticism. "I remember saying to him," she told me, her voice taking on the haughty tone she had used, "'And wherefore may I ask should I stand close to a barbarian such as thyself? Are thine ears so battle-damaged that thou canst not hear me from this perfectly reasonable distance?'"

According to Scota's account, which she related with a mixture of embarrassment and laughter, Gathelus leaned in slightly, his voice dropping to a conspiratorial murmur. "Because," he said, with a gleam in his eye, "thou art one step away from falling into the pit full of snakes we cunningly hid to deter spies like thyself!"

"I have never been more mortified in my life," Scota confessed to me, covering her face with her hands as she recounted what happened next. "At the mere mention of snakes—you know my terrible fear of them—all my royal dignity evaporated in an instant. I shrieked like a startled peacock and launched myself forward, directly into the captain's arms, burying my face against his bare chest!"

Gathelus, clearly caught off guard by this unexpected manoeuvre, stumbled back a step, momentarily losing his commanding composure. Even his stoic features registered surprise before settling back into controlled impassivity. The sentry who had captured Scota snickered audibly, earning a sharp glance from his captain.

"I could feel his heart beating against my cheek," she admitted softly, "and the warmth of his skin. For a moment, I forgot everything—my fear, my predicament, even who I was—aware only of the strange sensation of being held in those strong arms."

Just when it seemed the situation could grow no more complicated, a shout rang out from elsewhere in the camp. "My lord, we are under attack! One of our traps was sprung!"

The atmosphere shifted instantly from comedic to deadly serious. Gathelus gently but firmly disentangled himself from Scota's clinging embrace. "Stay here," he ordered, his voice deepening with authority. Then, turning to his men who were already scrambling to defensive positions: "To arms! Form ranks! Likely those desert jackals again!"

Scota, escorted to safety within the central area of the camp, experienced the battle from a different perspective. As she later described to me:

"I had never witnessed actual combat before—only training exercises of my father's guard. The sound alone was terrifying—the clash of metal, the shouted commands, the cries of the wounded. Yet I found myself moving to a position where I could observe the battle, drawn by a curious mixture of fear and fascination."

She watched as Gathelus moved through the combat like a man born to warfare, his sword a blur of deadly precision, his commands crisp and instantly obeyed. Within minutes, the raiders were in full retreat, melting back into the desert darkness from which they had emerged.

"There was something mesmerizing about watching him fight," Scota admitted, her eyes distant with the memory. "He moved with such grace and purpose, as if the battlefield was a place where he truly belonged. It was... unsettling to realize I found it beautiful, in its terrible way."

When relative calm had been restored, Gathelus returned to where Scota waited, within the confines of the captain commanders’ tent where the sentry left her. He retrieved his discarded cuirass, donning it with practiced ease, seemingly unaware of the effect his presence was having on the princess.

"I tried so hard to maintain my royal dignity," she confessed with a small laugh. "I tilted my chin in that way Tea always scolded me for—'haughty as a Persian queen,' she would say. But inside, something had shifted. He was no longer merely my captor or an enemy soldier. He was... well, I wasn't quite sure what he was, but it was certainly something more."

"Now," Gathelus said, his voice calm as if discussing the weather rather than having just repelled an armed attack, "about thy identity, little desert fox. Mine sentry reports that thou dost seem to know much about banners. Perhaps thou art indeed a spy after all."

Scota recounted this exchange to me with particular relish, adopting different voices for herself and the captain:

"I seized the opportunity to reclaim my dignity," she explained. "Drawing myself up to my full—admittedly rather unimpressive—height, I announced with all the royal authority I could muster: 'I am Princess Scota, daughter of King Zedekiah of Judah! And I demand to be returned to my camp at once!'"

She paused, a thoughtful look crossing her face. "Though I spoke with great conviction, part of me—a small, rebellious part that I scarcely acknowledged even to myself—secretly wished to remain longer in the presence of this enigmatic captain. There was something about him that both frightened and fascinated me."

Gathelus’ response, she told me, was measured, a single eyebrow rising sceptically. "A princess, lost and wandering alone in the desert? A curious tale." His gesture encompassed the vast, empty expanse surrounding them. "And thy camp, Princess, where might that be?"

"That was when I realized the depth of my predicament," Scota admitted. "I had no knowledge of where our camp lay in relation to theirs. I faltered visibly and then merely pointed vaguely, saying, 'It lieth... it lieth just yonder!' hoping he would not mark my uncertainty."

A faint, almost imperceptible smile played across Gathelus’ lips. "Very well, Princess. My duty is to protect this frontier. It seemeth it now extendeth to wayward royalty. We shall return thee to thy 'rabble' camp."

Just as Gathelus finished speaking, the sound of approaching footsteps reached them from the desert darkness. According to Scota's later account, Jeremiah had tracked her path by following the faint impressions of her sandals in the sand, guided by both his knowledge of her impulsive nature and what he would later describe as "divine prompting." The prophet had enlisted Ebed-melech's keen eyes and desert-craft in the search, and the distant glow of the foreign camp's fires had drawn them like a beacon across the darkened landscape.

"Who approaches?" the captain called out, placing himself instinctively between the newcomer and Scota—a protective gesture that spoke volumes about his character.

“One who claims to be a prophet milord, seeking an audience.” Remarked a sentry at full attention.

"One who hath been seeking that which thou hast found," Jeremiah replied, his voice carrying that distinctive resonance that had swayed kings and commoners alike throughout his long prophetic career. "I am Jeremiah, prophet of the Lord God of Israel, and the guardian of this impetuous young woman who hath caused thee such trouble."

Recognition flickered across Gathelus’ face—not of Jeremiah personally, but of his office and authority. The captain inclined his head in a gesture of respect that fell just short of a bow. "The prophet of whom many speak," he acknowledged. "Thy reputation extendeth e'en to those of us who serve foreign gods."

"There is but one God," Jeremiah corrected gently, "though He be known by many names among the nations. And it is He who hath guided our paths to cross upon this night, for purposes not yet fully revealed."

Scota, bewildered but glad the prophet managed to find her, seized the opportunity to reassert her presence. "Jeremiah!" she exclaimed, relief and chagrin mingling in her voice as she darted to his side. "Tell this... this barbarian who I am! Make him release me at once!"

Jeremiah's response, as Scota later recounted with a rueful smile, was not what she had hoped for. "Princess Scota," he said, with that particular mixture of exasperation and fondness that I had often observed in his interactions with the younger princess, "thou hast already proclaimed thy identity quite forcefully, I believe. As for thy release, thou art not being held captive, but protected from thine own recklessness. Captain Gathelus hath shown thee courtesy that many would not extend to an uninvited visitor who crept into their camp like a—" he paused, his lips twitching slightly, "—like a desert fox."

Scota blushed deeply at Jeremiah's gentle rebuke, but her natural resilience quickly reasserted itself. "I was merely... investigating," she later admitted telling him, though with less conviction than before. "The banners were unfamiliar. I was curious."

"And then Jeremiah said something that has stayed with me ever since," she told me during our long conversation about that night. "'Curiosity is a virtue when tempered with wisdom. When unleashed without restraint, it becomes folly.' I knew he was right, of course, though I wasn't about to admit it at the time."

Jeremiah turned back to Gathelus, who had been observing this exchange with evident interest. According to the prophet's later account to me, he said, "I must thank thee, Captain, for thy forbearance. The princess is young and hath not yet learned that royal blood offereth no protection against the natural consequences of imprudent actions."

Gathelus inclined his head, this time with a hint of genuine amusement. "She hath spirit," he conceded. "A quality I admire, even when it appeareth in the form of trespassing royalty."

"Spirit she hath in abundance," Jeremiah agreed dryly. "Judgment, however, remaineth a work in progress."

Scota opened her mouth to protest this assessment, then closed it again, apparently deciding that discretion might, for once, be the better part of valour. Instead, she glanced between the two men with a calculating expression that suggested she was reassessing her situation.

What followed has been pieced together from what Jeremiah and Scota later shared with me.
Gathelus, it seemed, was studying Jeremiah with growing curiosity. "Thou hast mentioned the Lord God of Israel," he said slowly. "I have heard tales of His power, how He parted seas and toppled walls. Thy people claim He is the only true God, do they not?"

"Not a claim, but a revelation," Jeremiah corrected. "From the burning bush of Moses to the still, small voice that spake to Elijah, the Lord hath made Himself known to those who have ears to hear and hearts to understand."

Something in these words seemed to resonate with the copper-haired captain. According to Jeremiah, a shadow crossed Gathelus’ face, quickly suppressed but notable—a glimpse of some inner conflict, some private wrestling with concepts larger than the immediate situation.

"Our camp lies not far from here," Jeremiah continued. "We are refugees from Jerusalem, journeying toward Migdol on the Egyptian frontier. The princess's sister and our companions will be anxious about her absence."

Gathelus nodded decisively. "We are bound for the same destination," he revealed. "Pharaoh Hophra has assigned us to patrol this region against Chaldean incursions and desert raiders. We would be honoured to escort you to Migdol, where your safety would be assured."

Indeed, when the prophet later described this moment to me, he spoke of a sudden clarity—as if the pieces of a divine puzzle had clicked into place: Scota's impetuous wandering, our chance encounter with this particular band of warriors, the strange banners that had caught his attention—all orchestrated by a hand far more subtle and purposeful than mere coincidence could explain.

As if to underscore this sense of divine purpose, a shooting star blazed across the night sky, its brilliant trail momentarily illuminating the scene with an otherworldly radiance. All three—prophet, princess, and captain—looked up simultaneously, their faces bathed in the transient celestial light.

"It was the strangest feeling," Scota confided to me days later, her voice hushed with wonder. "As if the heavens themselves were confirming something important had just happened. I felt small beneath that vast sky, yet somehow part of something much larger than myself."

Gathelus, too, seemed moved by the moment. "Come," he said, breaking the silence with a decisiveness that seemed to acknowledge its import. "Let us return the princess to her people. I will accompany you personally, with a small guard. The desert is treacherous at night."
