The battle against supernatural evil had tested every aspect of our faith, courage, and determination, and we had emerged not merely victorious but fundamentally transformed. We had witnessed the direct intervention of the Almighty in human affairs, received healing that exceeded natural possibility, and been marked as instruments chosen to fulfil purposes that reached beyond our own comprehension.

Most significantly, we had learned to honour sacrifice through service, ensuring that the courage of those who had given their lives for our mission would find its ultimate vindication in the successful completion of the tasks to which we had been called. Brutus, Alexios, Demos, and Nikomedes had not died in vain; their sacrifice had purchased not merely our immediate survival but our transformation into something worthy of the divine calling we had received.

As the Sea Serpent glided into the peaceful waters of Kallistê's harbour, surrounded by evidence of divine blessing and protection, I offered my own prayer of thanksgiving for the privilege of witnessing such extraordinary events and recording them for future generations who would need to understand the ways in which the Almighty works through ordinary people called to extraordinary service.

The great voyage continued, but we were no longer the same company that had fled Egypt in uncertainty and fear. We sailed now as servants of the Most High, carrying His purposes toward their appointed fulfilment in realms that lay beyond the edge of the known world, sustained by faith that had been tested in supernatural fire and proven stronger than the powers of darkness that opposed our sacred mission.

The adventure had become a pilgrimage, and there could be no turning back from whatever destiny awaited us in the mist-shrouded lands of the setting sun.
 
Chapter 10: Miracles and Maledictions

As compiled by Baruch ben Neriah

 In which love blooms amid lessons of war, prophetic dreams trouble the night, and ancient bloodlines reveal their hidden strengths

In the days that followed our miraculous deliverance from the supernatural pirate fleet, as we took refuge at the blessed island of fountains that Lysander had identified as Kallistê, a profound transformation took root in Gathelus that was evident to all who observed him. The serene haven, with its crystal springs and sheltered harbor where our wounded Sea Serpent could undergo necessary repairs and outfitting, provided the perfect sanctuary for contemplation and renewal. Here, surrounded by the gentle sounds of craftsmen restoring our vessel and the musical babbling of the sacred fountains, Gathelus’ warrior instincts, once guided purely by tactical considerations and battle-tested reflexes, began to be directed by a deeper wisdom—a spiritual awareness that transcended mere military competence and touched upon matters of eternal significance.

He spent hours each day with Jeremiah, his keen mind grappling with the ancient histories of Israel, the covenants that bound the Almighty to His chosen people, the intricate laws that governed righteous living, and the prophecies that spoke of future glories and tribulations yet to come. I often observed these sessions from a respectful distance, marvelling at the hunger for understanding that drove this Greek prince to master theological concepts that had taken Hebrew scholars lifetimes to comprehend fully.

The process was not without its struggles. Gathelus sought to reconcile the vastness of the Lord God—who commanded fire and sea, who could call down divine judgment upon entire fleets, who held the very forces of nature as instruments of His will—with the limited pantheon of his former beliefs. The Greek gods, for all their power and complexity in the ancient stories, seemed diminished when compared to the Almighty who had personally intervened to preserve our mission.

"I begin to understand," he said one afternoon as we sat together discussing the theological implications of our recent experiences, "wherefore the philosophers of my people have always struggled with questions of divine nature and cosmic order. They were seeking truth, but they were looking in the wrong direction. They tried to understand the divine through human reasoning alone, rather than through revelation and covenant relationship."

It was during these conversations that he spoke most frequently of the "Red Hand"—the symbol of his Zerahite ancestors that had marked his family line for generations. Now, with his growing understanding of Hebrew history and genealogy, he saw this ancient emblem not merely as a tribal identifier but as a sacred sign of his connection to the very bloodline from which the Messiah would eventually spring.

"Zerah was the twin brother of Perez," he explained to me with wonder evident in his voice, "and both were sons of Judah by Tamar. The red thread that marked Zerah's hand during their birth was a sign from the Almighty Himself. Mine ancestors were not simply Greeks who had migrated to foreign lands—they were descendants of the royal tribe of Judah, carriers of the same blood that doth flow in the veins of these royal daughters we protect."

This revelation had profound implications for understanding the divine purposes that had brought our diverse company together. What had appeared to be chance encounters and coincidental meetings were revealed as carefully orchestrated elements of a plan that had been in motion for centuries. Gathelus was not merely a hired protector or a convenient ally—he was family, bound by blood to the Davidic line he now served, and destined by divine providence to play a crucial role in the preservation and transplantation of the sacred royal heritage.

Princess Scota, ever observant and possessed of remarkable insight into human nature, watched these developments with a tenderness that had deepened far beyond mere affection or political alliance. She saw in Gathelus not just a formidable warrior whose skill had proven decisive in battle, but a man reborn, a soul grappling with the immensity of divine truth and finding his place within the eternal purposes of the Almighty.

Their bond, which had been forged in the shared perils of their escape from Egypt and sealed by the revelation of their intertwined lineages, grew stronger with each passing day. Yet it was a relationship that developed in ways that provided considerable entertainment for the rest of our company, as both parties seemed determined to approach their obvious mutual attraction with a combination of royal dignity and warrior stoicism that resulted in some of the most charmingly awkward courtship rituals I have ever witnessed.

The source of much of this entertainment arose from Princess Scota's determination to become more actively involved in her own defence. The terror she had experienced during the pirate attack, when she had been forced to remain below deck while others fought for their lives above, had convinced her that royal birth and feminine delicacy were insufficient protections in a world where supernatural enemies might attack without warning.

"I refuse to spend the remainder of this voyage cowering in the hold while braver souls risk their lives on my behalf," she announced one morning with characteristic determination. "If we are to face further dangers—and Jeremiah's prophecies suggest we certainly shall—then I intend to be prepared to defend myself and assist in the defence of our sacred cargo."

Her request was reasonable and even admirable, demonstrating the courage and practical wisdom that had marked her character throughout our journey. The challenge lay in determining how best to provide her with useful martial training while accommodating the realities of shipboard life and her complete lack of previous combat experience.

Gathelus, despite his growing spiritual awareness, remained fundamentally a professional warrior whose approach to combat training was shaped by years of preparing men for life-or-death struggles. When Scota approached him with her request for instruction in swordplay, he agreed with the serious demeanour of a master accepting a new student, apparently failing to anticipate the unique challenges that would arise from attempting to teach weapon craft to a strong-willed princess who had never held anything more dangerous than a ceremonial dagger.

"We shall begin with basic sword and shield work," he announced with military precision, producing a practice sword whose weight and balance had been adjusted for training purposes. "The foundation of all combat is proper stance, correct grip, and understanding of distance and timing. Master these fundamentals, and more advanced techniques shall follow naturally."

What followed was a series of training sessions that provided endless amusement for the twins, Korax, and the other members of our warrior band, though they were careful to observe from sufficient distance to avoid being recruited as additional instructors or, worse yet, as practice dummies.

Princess Scota approached her martial education with the same determined intensity that had marked her mastery of languages, statecraft, and royal protocol. Unfortunately, intellectual brilliance and political acumen proved to be poor substitutes for the physical coordination, muscle memory, and spatial awareness that effective swordplay required.
"Nay, nay," Gathelus said with determined patience as Scota attempted for the tenth time to execute a basic parry. "Thou must move the shield to intercept the blow, not simply wave it in the general direction of the attack. The shield is not a ceremonial ornament—'tis a tool for preserving thy life."

"I am moving it to intercept," Scota replied with growing frustration, sweat beading on her forehead despite the cool sea breeze. "The fault lieth with this clumsy piece of equipment, not with my technique."

"The shield doth respond to proper manipulation," Gathelus observed with the diplomatic neutrality he might have employed when correcting the errors of a particularly dense recruit. "Mayhap if thou didst adjust thy grip..."

"My grip is perfectly adequate!" Scota snapped, though the shield continued to wobble unsteadily on her arm. "The problem is that this weapon was obviously designed by men for men, with no consideration for different physical proportions or fighting styles."

I watched from the sidelines as Gathelus struggled to maintain his professional composure while dealing with a student whose royal upbringing had not prepared her for the humbling experience of being utterly incompetent at something she had assumed would be relatively straightforward. His face remained carefully neutral, but I could see the effort it required to avoid smiling at her increasingly creative explanations for her difficulties.
The situation reached its comedic peak during a lesson on basic sword forms, when Scota became so frustrated with her inability to execute a simple thrust-and-recover sequence that she began lecturing her instructor on the theoretical principles that should govern combat techniques.

"Obviously," she said with the authority of someone who had read extensively about military tactics, "the key to effective swordplay is understanding the geometry of attack and defence. If one doth simply apply the proper mathematical principles to the positioning of blade and body, success should follow logically."

"Combat is not mathematics, Princess," Gathelus replied with strained patience. "'Tis about instinct, timing, and the ability to react faster than conscious thought. No amount of theoretical knowledge can substitute for practical experience and physical conditioning."

"That is precisely the sort of limited thinking that doth prevent innovation in military science," Scota declared, warming to her subject despite her obvious fatigue. "A truly enlightened approach would integrate intellectual analysis with physical training to achieve superior results."

She attempted to demonstrate her point by executing what she evidently considered a perfect example of geometrically optimized sword work. The result was a series of movements so divorced from any recognizable combat technique that even the ship's rigging seemed to recoil in self-defence.

"Dost thou see?" she said triumphantly, though slightly out of breath. "By calculating the optimal angles and applying proper leverage principles, one can achieve devastating effectiveness with minimal effort."

Gathelus stared at her in fascination, as if observing some previously unknown species of warrior. "Princess," he said slowly, "that was certainly... unique. However, I believe thy theoretical approach may benefit from certain practical adjustments."

"Such as what?"

"Such as actually hitting the target thou didst intend to strike, rather than endangering the mainmast, three innocent bystanders, and a passing seabird."

The breaking point came when Scota, frustrated beyond endurance by her instructor's failure to appreciate her innovative techniques, finally threw down her practice sword and shield in disgust.

"This is impossible!" she declared, her royal composure finally cracking under the strain of repeated failure. "Thou art obviously a terrible teacher, the equipment is defective, and the entire concept of sword fighting is fundamentally flawed!"

To emphasize her point, she began hurling small stones from the ship's ballast at Gathelus, who dodged them with the reflexes of an experienced warrior while maintaining his carefully neutral expression.

"Princess," he said diplomatically as a particularly well-aimed pebble whistled past his ear, "mayhap we should take a brief respite from today's lesson."

"We should take a permanent respite!" Scota replied, launching another barrage of projectiles with increasing accuracy. "I have no intention of subjecting myself to further humiliation at the hands of an instructor who clearly doth lack the pedagogical skills necessary for proper education!"

It was at this moment that Gathelus made an observation that would transform both Scota's martial education and their evolving relationship.

"Thy aim," he said thoughtfully, ducking a stone that would have struck him squarely in the forehead if he had not moved, "is remarkably impressive."

Scota paused in mid-throw, a handful of ballast stones poised for launch. "What didst thou say?"

"Thy accuracy with those projectiles," Gathelus repeated, genuine admiration replacing his previous diplomatic neutrality. "Thou hast natural talent for ranged weapons that far exceedeth anything thou hast shown with sword and shield."

The princess looked down at the stones in her hand, then at her instructor, then at the pile of ballast from which she had been drawing her ammunition. "Art thou suggesting," she said slowly, "that my complete incompetence with traditional weapons is somehow a positive development?"

"I am suggesting," Gathelus replied with the first genuine smile he had displayed during their entire training relationship, "that we have been approaching thy martial education from entirely the wrong direction. Not every warrior is meant to be a master of sword and shield. Some are born to excel with weapons that require different skills entirely."

What followed was a revolutionary change in Scota's combat training that proved far more successful than their initial attempts at conventional weapons instruction. Gathelus, demonstrating the tactical flexibility that had made him an effective commander, completely abandoned their focus on close-quarters combat in favour of developing her obvious aptitude for precision ranged attacks.

The sling proved to be her ideal weapon—a device that required the same combination of mathematical calculation, precise timing, and steady nerves that had made her frustrated lectures on combat geometry so amusing. Under Gathelus’ guidance, she mastered the basic techniques with startling rapidity, progressing from simple accuracy exercises to complex scenarios that would have challenged experienced warriors.

"The sling," Gathelus explained as he watched her consistently strike targets at ranges that impressed even veteran soldiers, "is far from being a primitive or inferior weapon. In skilled hands, it can be more devastating than sword or spear, and it doth allow the user to engage enemies from distances that provide significant tactical advantages."

Scota's progress was remarkable not only for its speed but for the way it seemed to restore her confidence and enthusiasm for martial training. The princess who had been reduced to frustrated tears by her inability to execute basic sword forms was transformed into an eager student whose questions demonstrated sophisticated understanding of ballistics, target selection, and battlefield tactics.

"The historical precedents are quite encouraging," she observed one afternoon as she practiced with increasingly challenging targets. "David's victory over Goliath, the Egyptian slingers who serve in Pharaoh's armies, the Assyrian light troops whose accuracy hath proven decisive in many campaigns—all do demonstrate that ranged weapons can be decisive in conflicts where close combat would be impossible or inadvisable."

Her enthusiasm was infectious, and soon other members of our company were requesting instruction in sling techniques. Tea Tephi proved to have considerable natural ability, while several of the Egyptian guards discovered that their experience with hunting fowl had provided them with skills that transferred well to combat applications.

But it was when the twins, Nikias and Philippos, began secretly supplementing Scota's training with throwing knife instruction that her martial education truly blossomed into something extraordinary. The brothers, whose reputation for unconventional tactics had been well-established during our battle with the pirates, recognized in the princess a kindred spirit whose tactical thinking transcended traditional approaches to combat.

"Throwing knives," Nikias explained during one of their clandestine training sessions, "require the same precision and timing as sling work, but they offer advantages in close-quarters situations where a sling would be unwieldy."

"Additionally," Philippos added with the grin that invariably preceded his most innovative tactical suggestions, "they can be concealed more easily than conventional weapons, allowing a seemingly unarmed opponent to maintain the element of surprise."

Scota's progress with these weapons was even more dramatic than her success with the sling. Her natural coordination, combined with her analytical approach to understanding the physics of projectile motion, made her a formidable student whose accuracy improved with each practice session.

The secrecy surrounding these lessons was motivated by Scota's desire to surprise Gathelus with evidence of her expanded capabilities, though it provided an additional source of entertainment for those of us who were aware of the conspiracy. The sight of the twins sneaking about the ship with concealed knives, followed by the princess attempting to practice her techniques without arousing her primary instructor's suspicion, created a comedy of errors that brightened even the most monotonous days of our voyage.

The revelation of her expanded arsenal came during a practice session when Gathelus challenged her to demonstrate her sling skills against multiple targets in rapid succession. Scota not only met his challenge but exceeded it by smoothly transitioning from sling to throwing knives, striking a series of targets that would have been impossible to reach with her primary weapon.

"Where," Gathelus asked with carefully controlled surprise, "didst thou learn to do that?"

"Oh," Scota replied with studied casualness, "I have been supplementing my education through independent study. I believe in taking a comprehensive approach to learning."

The expression on Gathelus’ face as he realized that his student had been receiving secret instruction from his own subordinates was one of the most entertaining sights of our entire voyage. Professional pride warred with genuine admiration, while his growing personal feelings for the princess complicated his response to what could have been interpreted as either commendable initiative or inexcusable insubordination.

"I see," he said slowly, watching as Scota demonstrated a knife-throwing technique that bore the unmistakable hallmarks of the twins' unconventional teaching methods. "And how long has this... independent study... been proceeding?"

"Oh, several weeks now," Scota replied airily, as if advanced weapons training were a perfectly normal hobby for royal princesses to pursue in their spare time. "Nikias and Philippos have been most helpful in expanding my tactical education."

The twins, who had been observing from what they had assumed was a safe distance, suddenly found themselves the focus of their commander's attention in a way that suggested their next training session might be significantly more challenging than they had anticipated.

"Have they indeed?" Gathelus said with the tone of a man making mental notes for future discussions with his subordinates. "How... thoughtful of them to take such initiative in matters of education."
Despite his mild disapproval of their secretive methods, Gathelus could not deny that the results of Scota's expanded training regimen were impressive. The princess who had been unable to execute basic sword forms was now capable of engaging multiple targets with different weapons in rapid succession, demonstrating tactical flexibility that would have been valuable in actual combat situations.

More importantly, from the perspective of our shipboard observers, the success of her martial education seemed to have opened new dimensions in the evolving relationship between student and instructor. Scota's confidence in her combat abilities had restored her natural wit and charm, while Gathelus’ genuine admiration for her progress had awakened aspects of his personality that had been dormant during his years of purely military service.

The resulting courtship, if it could be dignified with such a formal term, provided endless entertainment for those of us privileged to witness it. Both parties seemed determined to maintain appropriate dignity and professional decorum, despite the obvious mutual attraction that was apparent to everyone except, apparently, themselves.

Gathelus’ attempts at romantic gestures were masterpieces of awkward sincerity. He would appear at Scota's side during her morning observations of the horizon, offering profound comments about the beauty of sunrise over water that somehow managed to sound like tactical assessments of visibility conditions. His gifts of interesting stones for her sling practice were presented with the formal solemnity of diplomatic negotiations, accompanied by detailed explanations of their aerodynamic properties that completely obscured any romantic intentions.

"I have observed," he announced one afternoon with the gravity of a military briefing, "that certain types of limestone possess optimal weight-to-density ratios for long-range accuracy. I thought thou mightest find these specimens useful for advanced training exercises."

Scota accepted his geological offerings with gracious formality, though I noticed that she treasured them far beyond their practical value. Her attempts to encourage his romantic advances were equally indirect, consisting primarily of finding excuses to request his assistance with tasks that required close physical proximity or extended private conversation.

"Gathelus," she said one evening with studied casualness, "I have been reviewing some of the navigational techniques that Lysander has been teaching me, and I find myself uncertain about certain calculations. Couldst thou perhaps review my work and offer suggestions for improvement?"

The request was transparently unnecessary—Scota's mathematical abilities far exceeded what was required for basic navigation—but Gathelus accepted it with the serious attention he would have devoted to genuine technical consultation. The resulting tutorials, conducted in the privacy of her cabin with charts spread across her table, created opportunities for the sort of intimate conversation that would have been impossible in the presence of other members of our company.

Yet both parties seemed determined to treat these encounters as purely educational, despite the obvious tension that arose from their proximity. Gathelus would explain astronomical navigation with the focused intensity of a scholar, while Scota asked thoughtful questions that demonstrated both genuine interest and a remarkable ability to prolong discussions that could have been concluded in a fraction of the time they actually required.

The comedy reached its peak during an incident involving a navigation exercise that had been designed to teach Scota how to calculate our position using celestial observations. The lesson required them to stand together at the ship's rail during the hour before dawn, sharing Gathelus’ navigation instruments while he guided her through the process of measuring stellar positions.

The combination of pre-dawn darkness, close physical proximity, and the romantic atmosphere created by starlight reflecting off calm water should have provided the perfect opportunity for the declaration of feelings that everyone aboard was anticipating. Instead, both parties became so focused on the technical aspects of celestial navigation that they managed to conduct an entire romantic interlude as if it were an advanced mathematics tutorial.

"The astrolabe must be held precisely level," Gathelus explained, his hands covering hers as he helped her position the instrument, "and the sight must be aligned exactly with the star's apparent position."

"I understand the principle," Scota replied, though her voice carried a breathless quality that had nothing to do with intellectual excitement, "but I find the practical application more challenging than I had anticipated."

They stood together in the starlight, their faces inches apart as they peered through the astrolabe's sighting mechanism, apparently oblivious to the romantic potential of their situation. The navigation lesson continued for nearly an hour, with both participants demonstrating remarkable dedication to astronomical education despite the obvious distractions.

The frustration of observing such elaborate romantic evasion was eventually too much for Princess Tea Tephi, who finally decided to take direct action.

"Sister," she announced the following morning with characteristic directness, "thy navigation skills have improved remarkably, but I believe thou mayest be overcomplicating certain aspects of thy education."

"Whatever dost thou mean?" Scota replied with studied innocence, though her heightened colour suggested she understood her sister's implications perfectly well.
"I mean," Tea Tephi said with the patient tone of someone explaining obvious truths to the deliberately obtuse, "that celestial navigation requires neither the extended private consultations nor the detailed hand-positioning instruction that thou dost seem to find necessary."
"Technical education demands thorough attention to detail," Scota replied with dignity that was somewhat undermined by her inability to meet her sister's eyes. "Gathelus is merely ensuring that I receive proper instruction."

"I see," Tea Tephi observed dryly. "And does this proper instruction typically require quite so much... physical guidance?"

"Navigation instruments are complex and potentially dangerous if handled incorrectly," Scota said with the air of someone defending an unassailable position. "Safety considerations make close supervision essential."

Tea Tephi's expression suggested that she was not entirely convinced by these technical arguments, but she refrained from further direct commentary on her sister's romantic strategy. Instead, she began offering her own subtle assistance to the courtship process, creating opportunities for private conversation and ensuring that various shipboard duties required Gathelus and Scota to work together on projects that had nothing to do with navigation or combat training.

Meanwhile, Tea Tephi herself was undergoing her own intensive education, though hers focused on the practical skills that would be required for royal governance in whatever realm they might eventually establish. Under Jeremiah's guidance, she studied the economic principles that governed trade relationships, the military tactics necessary for defending territory, and the diplomatic protocols required for establishing legitimate authority in foreign lands.

Her natural intelligence and studious temperament made her an ideal student for these complex subjects. Unlike her sister's dramatic struggles with combat training, Tea Tephi approached each new area of knowledge with methodical thoroughness that reminded me of Hebrew scholars studying sacred texts.

"The establishment of legitimate royal authority," Jeremiah explained during one of their tutorials, "requires more than simple conquest or political manoeuvring. A throne that would endure must be founded upon principles of justice, supported by economic prosperity, and defended by military competence that inspires respect rather than fear."

"The historical examples suggest considerable variation in approach," Tea Tephi observed thoughtfully. "David's kingship was established through divine anointing and popular support, while Solomon's authority rested upon inherited legitimacy and demonstrated wisdom. Each required different strategies for maintaining power and ensuring succession."

Her questions demonstrated sophisticated understanding of the challenges they would face in establishing a new royal house in unfamiliar territory. She grasped instinctively that their success would depend upon their ability to adapt traditional Hebrew concepts of kingship to whatever cultural and political realities they encountered in their destination.

"The sacred relics we carry," she said during one particularly intensive discussion, "will provide evidence of our legitimate authority, but they will mean nothing to people who do not understand their significance. We must be prepared to establish our credibility through practical demonstration of our ability to provide effective governance."

These educational sessions served multiple purposes beyond their obvious practical value. They reinforced Tea Tephi's understanding of her future responsibilities while providing her with the intellectual framework necessary for making the complex decisions that royal governance would require. More importantly, they created opportunities for private conversation with Jeremiah that allowed her to discuss concerns that she preferred not to share with other members of our company.

It was during one of these confidential consultations that she revealed the disturbing dreams that had been troubling her sleep for several weeks.

"I have been experiencing visions," she confided to the old prophet, her voice carrying the weight of genuine distress. "Not prophecies like yours, but dark dreams that seem too vivid and consistent to be dismissed as mere products of an overactive imagination."

Jeremiah's expression grew serious as he recognized the potential significance of her revelation. Throughout Hebrew history, divine communication had often come through dreams and visions, but so had warnings of coming dangers and glimpses of spiritual warfare that remained invisible to ordinary perception.

"Tell me about these dreams," he said gently. "Describe what you see, and do not omit any details that might seem insignificant. The Lord often speaks through visions that appear confused or symbolic to human understanding."

Tea Tephi took a deep breath before beginning her account, as if the very act of describing her dreams required considerable courage.

"I see a great army being overrun by creatures that are not entirely human," she said quietly. "The warriors fight bravely, but they are overwhelmed by enemies whose strength and ferocity exceed anything that mortal men should be able to withstand. The battle takes place in a land I do not recognize—green hills and forests unlike anything we have seen during our travels."

Her description sent a chill through me as I listened from my position as recorder of these important conversations. The supernatural enemies she described sounded remarkably similar to the Rephaim we had recently encountered, suggesting that our battle with Captain Moloch might have been only the first of many confrontations with beings whose existence challenged the natural order.

"The most disturbing aspect of these visions," Tea Tephi continued, "involves a king who possesses the strength of ten men but seems determined to conceal this gift rather than use it openly. He fights alongside his warriors, but he restrains his true capabilities as if afraid that others might discover the full extent of his power."

Jeremiah nodded thoughtfully as he processed the implications of her account. "Such strength would be a tremendous blessing in battle, yet concealment suggests either humility or fear of how others might react to such supernatural capabilities."

"Why would a king hide strength that could save his people?" Tea Tephi asked, voicing the question that had troubled her most deeply. "If divine blessing had granted him such power, would not duty require him to use it fully in defence of his realm?"

The prophet's response drew upon the deepest traditions of Hebrew understanding about the nature of divine gifts and the responsibilities they carried.
"The sacred texts speak of such strength in our own lineage," he said slowly, his voice carrying the weight of ancient memory. "Jacob himself possessed the strength of ten men—strength sufficient to wrestle with an angel and prevail. This account describes how this unnatural strength and power was both blessing and burden, marking him as chosen by the Almighty but also setting him apart from ordinary humanity in ways that created both awe and fear among those who witnessed it."

He paused to consult his memory of other scriptural references before continuing his exposition.

"The testaments of the patriarchs record that Judah, too, was blessed with unusual strength—power that enabled him to tear apart wild beasts with his bare hands and to lift stones that required teams of ordinary men to move. Yet the texts also suggest that such gifts carried responsibilities that were not always welcome to those who possessed them."

Tea Tephi listened intently as Jeremiah explained the theological implications of supernatural strength granted to chosen individuals. The pattern that emerged from his teaching suggested that divine gifts of this magnitude were rarely given for personal advantage but rather as tools for accomplishing specific purposes within the Almighty's greater plan.

"Those who receive such blessings," the prophet explained, "often find themselves isolated by their very capabilities. Ordinary men fear what they cannot understand, and supernatural strength can create barriers between the gifted individual and the people he is meant to serve. The king in your vision may be hiding his true capabilities to maintain the trust and fellowship of his warriors."

"But surely," Tea Tephi protested, "the preservation of his kingdom and the lives of his people would justify revealing whatever strength was necessary to achieve victory?"

"Perhaps," Jeremiah replied thoughtfully, "but the dynamics of leadership are more complex than simple military calculations might suggest. A king who appears to possess powers beyond human ken risks being seen as something other than human himself. Such perceptions can undermine the very authority that supernatural strength is meant to support."

This explanation provided a framework for understanding the troubling aspects of Tea Tephi's visions, but it also raised disturbing questions about the challenges that might await our own company in the unknown lands toward which we sailed. If the royal line we carried was destined to establish a new kingdom in territories where supernatural enemies posed ongoing threats, then the hidden strengths that Jeremiah described might prove essential for survival.

"Do you believe," Tea Tephi asked quietly, "that these dreams are warnings of trials we ourselves might face? Are we sailing toward a land where such battles will be necessary?"

"The Lord's purposes are not always revealed clearly to human understanding," Jeremiah replied with the caution appropriate to prophetic matters. "Your visions may indeed be warnings of challenges that await us, or they may be glimpses of events in distant times and places that are connected to our mission in ways we cannot yet comprehend."

"But the strength you describe," she pressed, "the supernatural power granted to Jacob and Judah—could such gifts appear again in their descendants?"

The prophet's answer carried implications that would influence our understanding of the divine purposes we served for the remainder of our voyage.

"The bloodline you carry," he said solemnly, "is marked by the Almighty for purposes that extend far beyond simple political authority. The promises made to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob include blessings that will manifest in their descendants according to the Lord's timing and purposes. Supernatural strength, divine wisdom, prophetic insight—all these gifts remain within the heritage you bear, waiting to be revealed when the need arises and the time is right."

As I recorded this conversation for future reference, I found myself wondering whether the transformation that had already taken place in our company might be only the beginning of changes that would be required for the fulfilment of our mission. Gathelus had already demonstrated capabilities that exceeded normal human limits during his duel with the Rephaim captain, though the sword of Goliath had provided the immediate source of his enhanced abilities.

But if the royal daughters carried within their bloodline the potential for supernatural gifts that had marked their ancestors, then our destination might require far more than conventional royal governance and military competence. We might be sailing toward trials that would call forth hidden strengths and divine blessings that had lain dormant for generations, waiting for the proper time and circumstances to be revealed.

The implications of such possibilities both thrilled and sobered me as I contemplated the magnitude of the purposes we served. Our small company had already witnessed divine intervention that exceeded anything recorded in the chronicles of ordinary kingdoms, but we might be approaching challenges that would require even greater manifestations of supernatural power and blessing.

As the days passed on the blessed island of Kallistê, the patterns of our temporary settlement developed rhythms that reflected both our practical needs and our growing understanding of the sacred nature of our mission. Scota's combat training progressed from source of entertainment to genuine martial competence, while her relationship with Gathelus evolved from awkward mutual attraction to something approaching genuine courtship, though both parties maintained their determination to approach romance with the same methodical thoroughness they applied to all other aspects of their education.
Tea Tephi's studies continued to expand her understanding of royal responsibilities, while her troubling dreams provided ongoing reminders that our destination might present challenges requiring wisdom, courage, and perhaps supernatural intervention on a scale that would test every aspect of our faith and determination.

The Sea Serpent rested in Kallistê's crystal harbor, her wounds from the supernatural battle healing under the skilled hands of Lysander's craftsmen. Soon she would be ready to carry us toward whatever destiny awaited in the green lands that lay beyond the western horizon. Behind us lay the miraculous deliverance from supernatural enemies that had proven the divine protection surrounding our mission. Ahead lay trials and triumphs that would require all the courage, wisdom, and faith we could muster.

We were no longer simply refugees fleeing the collapse of earthly kingdoms, but servants of the Highest preparing for the final stage of our sacred journey toward realms that existed more in prophecy than in the maps of mortal geographers. The adventure had become a pilgrimage, the pilgrimage had become a sacred mission, and there could be no turning back from whatever extraordinary challenges awaited us in the mist-shrouded lands of the setting sun.
 
Chapter 11: Shores of Iberia (Spain)

 As compiled by Baruch ben Neriah

 In which ancient kinship is revealed, lost tribes are rediscovered, and the Red Hand banner finds its ancestral home among the Celto-Iberians.

The morning mist clung to the crystal waters of Kallistê's harbor as the Sea Serpent prepared for departure, her hull gleaming with fresh pitch and her rigging singing with new cordage. The blessed island of fountains had restored both our vessel and our spirits, but the divine purposes that drove our mission would not permit extended rest. As Lysander called orders to his crew and the great sail unfurled to catch the western wind, I felt the familiar mixture of anticipation and trepidation that had marked each stage of our extraordinary journey.

Princess Tea Tephi stood at the stern; her eyes fixed upon the sacred springs that had provided such sweet refreshment during our sojourn. "Will we see this place again?" she asked quietly, though I sensed her question touched upon deeper concerns about the permanence of any earthly sanctuary in lives dedicated to divine service.

"All places blessed by the Almighty remain in His keeping," Jeremiah replied with the certainty of one who had witnessed the destruction of earthly temples yet knew that heavenly purposes endured beyond the rise and fall of kingdoms. "Whether our feet do tread these shores again mattereth less than whether our hearts carry forward the peace and renewal we have found here."

Gathelus supervised the loading of our expanded provisions with the methodical attention to detail that had marked his approach to every aspect of our expedition. The island's generous inhabitants had replenished our stores with fresh water, preserved foods, and medicinal herbs that would prove invaluable during the final stages of our westward voyage. More importantly, the time of rest and preparation had allowed our company to develop into a cohesive fellowship bound by shared purpose and mutual trust.

Princess Scota, now armed with her sling and throwing knives, moved with a confidence that would have been unthinkable during our desperate flight from Egypt. Her transformation from pampered royal to capable warrior-princess had been perhaps the most dramatic change in our entire company, though the romantic tension between her and Gathelus continued to provide entertainment for those of us privileged to observe their continuing courtship.

As the Sea Serpent cleared the harbor and set her course toward the western horizon, I reflected upon the remarkable providence that had brought us safely through trials that would have overwhelmed any expedition relying purely upon human wisdom and strength. The supernatural enemies we had faced, the divine interventions we had witnessed, and the spiritual transformations we had experienced had prepared us for whatever challenges awaited in the unknown lands toward which we sailed.

The voyage to Iberia proved mercifully uneventful, blessed with favourable winds and calm seas that seemed to confirm divine approval for this stage of our journey. Unlike our previous travels, which had been marked by constant dangers and supernatural opposition, these days of peaceful sailing allowed our company to consolidate the lessons learned during our time at Kallistê and prepare mentally for whatever reception awaited us in the Celtic lands.

Lysander's navigation proved as skilful as ever, though even his experienced eyes showed wonder as the distant coastline began to resolve into features that matched ancient charts and legendary descriptions. "There," he announced one morning, pointing toward a distinctive landmark that crowned the approaching cliffs, "Breogan's Tower. We have reached the western shores of Iberia, the realm of the Celto-Iberians whose fame hath spread throughout the Mediterranean world."

The sight that greeted us as we approached the coast exceeded even the most elaborate descriptions we had heard from traders and travellers. Towering cliffs of grey stone rose directly from the sea, crowned with pine forests whose dark green contrasted dramatically with the white-capped waves below. And dominating the entire landscape, perched upon the highest promontory like a sentinel watching over both land and sea, stood the ancient structure known throughout the western world as Breogan's Tower.

The tower itself was a marvel of engineering that spoke of builders possessed of both ambition and skill far exceeding what one might expect from supposedly primitive peoples. Rising more than a hundred cubits into the sky, its massive stone blocks were fitted together with such precision that no mortar was visible in the joints, while the spiral stairway that wound around its exterior suggested both defensive capability and ceremonial significance.

"I have heard tales of this tower since my youth," Gathelus murmured as he studied the imposing structure with a warrior's eye for strategic advantages and architectural strength. "The Celtic bards claim it was built by a hero-king whose name hath been preserved in the very stone of this coast. But seeing it with mine own eyes, I understand wherefore it hath become a landmark for sailors throughout the western seas."

We made landfall in a sheltered cove where a small stream provided fresh water and the beach offered suitable protection for our vessel. The local inhabitants, who had obviously observed our approach from elevated positions, appeared on the heights above with the cautious curiosity characteristic of peoples accustomed to both trading opportunities and potential threats from the sea.

These Celto-Iberians proved to be a remarkable blend of warrior courage and sophisticated culture that impressed even our travel-hardened company. Their physical appearance combined the dark colouring and compact build typical of Mediterranean peoples with the fair complexions and proud bearing that marked the Celtic tribes, while their clothing and weapons demonstrated both practical functionality and artistic sensibility that spoke of ancient traditions refined over generations.

The chieftain who first approached our landing party was a man of middle years whose bearing and equipment marked him as both warrior and leader. His bronze-fitted sword and intricately decorated shield were of quality that would have impressed Mediterranean craftsmen, while the torque of twisted gold around his neck and the elaborate brooches that secured his cloak spoke of wealth and authority that transcended mere military command.

"I am Viriatus of the Red Lion," he announced in accented but understandable Greek, apparently recognizing our origins from our dress and equipment. "Chief of the coastal settlements that do acknowledge the protection of Breogan's Tower. What doth bring strangers from the eastern seas to our shores?"

It was at this moment that Gathelus experienced the first of many shocks that would transform his understanding of his own heritage and destiny. The banner that flew from Viriatus's spear bore a device that sent a jolt of recognition through his entire being—a red lion rampant on a field of gold, identical in every detail to the heraldic symbol that had marked his own family's standards for generations beyond counting.

"Thy banner," Gathelus said slowly, his voice carrying a wonder that was apparent to all who heard him. "The Red Lion. Where didst thou acquire this device?"

Viriatus studied our Greek prince with increased interest, his experienced warrior's eye noting details of bearing and equipment that suggested both noble birth and combat experience. "The Red Lion hath been the symbol of my family since the days when the first settlers came to these shores from the eastern lands," he replied carefully. "It was brought by those who claimed descent from the tribe marked by the scarlet thread, the people of the Red Hand who carried the blessing of the Most High."

The implications of this revelation sent a thrill of excitement through our entire company, though it was Jeremiah who first grasped the full significance of what we were witnessing. The prophet's eyes blazed with the light of divine understanding as he stepped forward to address the Celtic chieftain with the authority of one who spoke for the Almighty Himself.

"Son of the western tribes," he said with solemn dignity, "thou speakest truly when thou dost claim descent from the people of the Red Hand. The scarlet thread that marked Zerah's birth hath been preserved in bloodlines that have wandered far from their original heritage, yet the promises of the Most High do endure beyond the rise and fall of earthly kingdoms."

What followed was one of the most extraordinary conversations I have ever been privileged to record, as Jeremiah began to reveal to these lost descendants of Israel their true identity and the purposes for which divine providence had preserved them in this distant land. The prophet's words, spoken with the authority of one who had witnessed the rise and fall of kingdoms and the fulfilment of ancient prophecies, carried a power that transcended mere human eloquence.

"Long ago," Jeremiah explained to the growing crowd of Celtic warriors and their families, "when the northern kingdom of Israel fell to Assyrian conquest, many of the scattered tribes sought refuge in distant lands rather than submit to foreign rule. The tribes of Reuben and Gad, whose territories lay east of the Jordan, were among the first to be displaced, and their descendants carried the ancient faith and the sacred symbols westward through lands that knew not the name of the true God."

The recognition that dawned in the eyes of our Celtic listeners as the prophet spoke was like watching the sunrise break through morning mist. Ancient traditions that had been preserved in songs and stories suddenly took on new meaning, while symbols and customs that had seemed mere cultural inheritance revealed their sacred origins and divine purposes.

Viriatus himself was visibly moved as he began to understand the full implications of Jeremiah's revelation. "The old songs speak of the Great Exile," he said slowly, "when our fathers were driven from the lands of promise by enemies whose number was like the sand of the sea. They tell of sacred relics carried westward and bloodlines preserved for purposes that would be revealed in the fullness of time."

"Those purposes," Jeremiah declared with prophetic authority, "are being revealed even now. The bloodlines of Zerah and the house of David have been brought together by divine providence for the establishment of a new kingdom that will extend the blessings of the Most High to the ends of the earth."

It was at this moment that other Celtic chieftains began to arrive, drawn by reports of strangers whose presence seemed to fulfil ancient prophecies and long-held expectations. Each brought additional evidence of the remarkable connections between our mission and the heritage of these western tribes.

Brennus of the Golden Boar, whose territory encompassed the ancient settlement called Soria—a name that immediately caught Gathelus’ attention as a possible corruption of "Zerah's town"—brought with him a bronze tablet inscribed with genealogical records that traced his lineage to the eastern lands and the tribe marked by the scarlet thread.

Ambiorix of the Silver Wolf ruled over the coastal region near the great trading port that the locals called Gades, though Jeremiah recognized the name as a clear descendant of "Gad's place," confirming his understanding of the tribal movements that had brought the scattered children of Israel to these western shores.

Most remarkable of all was the arrival of Vercingetorix of the Iron Eagle, whose domain included the inland fortress town known as Saragossa—a name that caused Gathelus to gasp with recognition as he realized he was looking upon "Zerah's fortress," a settlement established by his own ancestors’ generations before his birth.

"The very stones cry out in witness," Gathelus murmured as he contemplated the evidence that surrounded him. "My family's wanderings were not aimless exile but guided pilgrimage, bringing us full circle to lands where our blood had already established roots and our symbols had been preserved by kinsmen we never knew existed."

The warmth of kinship that immediately developed between Gathelus and these Celtic chieftains transcended ordinary diplomatic courtesy or strategic alliance. These were men who recognized in each other the marks of common ancestry and shared destiny, warriors whose ancestors had carried the same promises and preserved the same sacred traditions despite centuries of separation and geographic distance.

But it was when the local bards began to recite their ancient prophecies that the true magnitude of divine providence became apparent to all who witnessed these remarkable events. The Celtic tradition spoke of a coming hero-king who would bear the banner of the Red Hand and bring with him a "Tender Twig" whose planting would grow into a vine whose branches would extend throughout the earth.

"For generations beyond counting," declared Cathbad, who wore the white robes and golden torque that marked him as chief among the local priests, "our people have preserved legends of a Red Hand King who would come from across the eastern sea. He would unite the scattered tribes, drive out the oppressors who terrorize the innocent, and establish a kingdom whose influence would spread to the uttermost parts of the world."

Jeremiah listened to these words with the keen attention he always gave to matters that might touch upon divine purposes, yet his response was measured and clear. "If the Most High hath allowed echoes of His eternal purposes to survive even among those who have served false gods, it is but proof of His mercy and sovereignty," he declared to our company. "Yet we must distinguish between the corrupted shadows preserved in pagan traditions and the pure light of divine revelation. These people speak of kings and kingdoms because the Creator hath written such hopes upon every human heart, but they know not the true King nor the righteous kingdom He will establish."

The parallels between certain Celtic legends and the Hebrew prophecies that had shaped our mission were notable, yet Jeremiah was careful to explain that such similarities represented not divine approval of druidic practices, but rather the universal human longing for redemption and righteous rule that God had planted in every nation—longings that could only be truly fulfilled through the true Messiah and His kingdom.

As word of our arrival spread throughout the region, representatives from tribes throughout Iberia began to arrive at Breogan's Tower, each bringing additional confirmation of the remarkable connections between our mission and their ancient expectations. The evidence mounted daily that this land had been prepared by divine providence to serve as a staging ground for the next phase of our journey, while the people had been preserved and prepared to play crucial roles in the fulfilment of purposes that transcended their individual tribal concerns.

Yet even as these revelations brought joy and wonder to our company, we began to understand why divine providence had brought us to this particular time and place. The Celtic tribes, for all their courage and nobility, were facing challenges that threatened their very survival as free peoples. Giant clans—descendants of the Nephilim who had once terrorized the ancient world—had taken root in the mountainous regions of Iberia and were systematically oppressing the human settlements with a cruelty that exceeded even the worst excesses of ordinary warfare.

"These are not merely large men or particularly fierce warriors," explained Brennus during one of our evening councils around the fires that burned before Breogan's Tower. "The giants who plague our people possess strength and ferocity that exceed human limits, while their leaders demonstrate cunning and malice that suggest supernatural intelligence directed toward specifically evil purposes."

The descriptions we heard of these giant clans matched disturbing accounts from Hebrew tradition regarding the offspring of the fallen angels who had corrupted the earth in the days before the Great Flood. Like their ancient predecessors, these beings seemed driven by hatred for human civilization and took particular pleasure in destroying anything that reflected divine blessing or creative order.

"They do not simply raid for plunder or territory," Ambiorix added with the grim authority of one who had witnessed their depredations firsthand. "Their attacks are designed to spread terror, to break the spirit of free peoples, and to establish dominion through fear and cruelty that goes beyond any rational military objective."

It was in response to these revelations that Jeremiah made a proposal that would shape the remainder of our time in Iberia and establish the foundation for future cooperation between our mission and the Celtic peoples.

"The strength that the Most High has granted to Gathelus," the prophet declared with the authority of divine inspiration, "was not given merely for his personal protection or the defence of our immediate company. Like the gifts bestowed upon the heroes of ancient Israel, supernatural blessing carries responsibility for the protection of the innocent and the establishment of righteous order in the earth."

The wisdom of this observation was immediately apparent to all who heard it, though the practical implications required careful consideration of both spiritual principles and tactical realities. Gathelus possessed capabilities that could prove decisive against enemies whose supernatural nature gave them overwhelming advantages over ordinary human warriors, yet the scope of the giant problem in Iberia far exceeded what could be addressed through individual heroism alone.

"What is needed," Jeremiah continued, his voice carrying the weight of prophetic insight, "is not merely military intervention but the unification of the scattered tribes under leadership that combines divine blessing with practical wisdom. The Celtic peoples possess courage and skill in abundance, but they lack the unified command necessary to coordinate effective resistance against enemies whose strength exceeds human limits."

The proposal that emerged from these discussions was as ambitious as it was necessary—Gathelus would assume leadership of a unified Celtic confederation dedicated to driving the giant clans from Iberia and establishing a realm where the ancient faith could take root and flourish. The task would require not only military genius but the sort of spiritual authority that could unite traditional enemies in common cause against supernatural opposition.

Gathelus received this proposal with the mixture of humility and determination that had marked his response to every divine calling since his conversion aboard the Sea Serpent. "If the Most High has prepared me for such a purpose," he declared with quiet confidence, "then I will not shrink from whatever responsibilities He places upon me. These people are my kinsmen by blood and brothers in faith, and their suffering demands response from any who possess the power to provide relief."

The Celtic response to this proposal exceeded even Jeremiah's optimistic expectations. Warriors who had been feuding for generations set aside ancient grievances to unite under the banner of the Red Hand, while tribal leaders who had never acknowledged any authority beyond their own bloodlines pledged allegiance to the Greek prince whose ancestry and divine blessing marked him as the fulfilment of their most sacred prophecies.

Yet it was Princess Scota's reaction to these developments that provided the most immediate practical challenge to our mission planning. The prospect of dangerous expeditions into giant-infested territory was difficult enough without the additional complications introduced by royal romance and feminine jealousy.

The source of Scota's distress became apparent when Viriatus introduced his daughter Brigantia, a young woman whose reputation as a warrior and scout had spread throughout the Celtic territories. Tall and fair-haired, with the green eyes and proud bearing characteristic of her people, she possessed both the beauty and capability that would have attracted masculine attention in any culture, while her knowledge of local geography and giant clan movements made her participation in reconnaissance missions virtually essential.

"Brigantia knows the mountain paths better than any warrior in our tribe," Viriatus explained with paternal pride, apparently oblivious to the effect his daughter's presence was having upon the romantic dynamics within our company. "She has tracked the giant movements for months, mapping their strongholds and learning their patterns in ways that will prove invaluable for planning effective campaigns against them."

The Celtic maiden's initial greeting to Gathelus included a series of subtle gestures and meaningful glances that required no translation, regardless of linguistic barriers. Her obvious appreciation for his warrior bearing and noble features was accompanied by the sort of flirtatious attention that would have been flattering under different circumstances but created considerable tension given the existing romantic arrangements within our party.

Princess Scota's response to this development demonstrated that royal training in diplomacy had definite limitations when confronted with direct threats to personal happiness. Her attempts to maintain appropriate dignity while simultaneously asserting her prior claim upon Gathelus’ affections resulted in a series of increasingly creative interventions that provided entertainment for everyone except the principals involved.

When Gathelus announced his intention to lead a reconnaissance expedition into giant territory, accompanied by myself as cartographer and Brigantia as guide, Scota's diplomatic restraint finally cracked under the strain of imagined romantic competition.

"Absolutely not!" she declared with royal authority that brooked no contradiction. "If there are dangerous expeditions to be undertaken, then I shall certainly accompany them. My combat training was not undertaken merely for decorative purposes, and I refuse to remain safely behind while others face perils on our behalf."

Gathelus’ attempts to dissuade her participation demonstrated his continuing inability to recognize the romantic undercurrents that were obvious to every other member of our company. His arguments focused exclusively upon practical considerations of danger and tactical necessity, completely missing the emotional dynamics that were driving Scota's determination to insert herself into situations where she could monitor potential romantic rivals.

"Princess," he said with the patient tone of a military commander explaining obvious tactical principles to an inexperienced recruit, "the giant clans represent threats that exceed anything we have previously encountered. These will not be conventional battles where courage and skill can compensate for differences in size and strength. We will be facing enemies whose supernatural capabilities require specialized tactics and rapid movement through terrain that will challenge even experienced scouts."

"Then obviously I need the experience that such expeditions would provide," Scota replied with logic that was unassailable even if her motivations were transparently personal. "How can I expect to rule effectively in lands where such creatures pose ongoing threats if I remain ignorant of their capabilities and the tactics necessary to combat them?"

The Celtic warriors who observed these exchanges were clearly entertained by the romantic drama unfolding before them, though their cultural traditions provided different perspectives on feminine participation in military operations. Celtic women had long enjoyed greater freedom and authority than their Mediterranean counterparts, and the idea of a warrior-princess accompanying dangerous missions seemed less unusual to them than it did to our own company.

Brigantia herself added to the complications by expressing genuine enthusiasm for Scota's participation, apparently viewing the Egyptian princess as an interesting cultural novelty rather than a romantic rival. Her friendly overtures and helpful suggestions regarding equipment and tactics only increased Scota's suspicions, since such behavior could be interpreted either as innocent Celtic hospitality or as sophisticated misdirection designed to lower defences before striking.

The situation reached its peak of absurdity when Scota began insisting that every aspect of mission planning required her personal oversight and approval. Equipment selection, route planning, provisions procurement—every detail became an opportunity for the princess to demonstrate her indispensability while simultaneously ensuring that Gathelus and Brigantia had minimal opportunities for private conversation.

"I believe," Scota announced with the authority of someone stating obvious truths, "that the success of any expedition depends upon careful coordination between all participants. Therefore, I shall personally review every aspect of our preparations to ensure that nothing has been overlooked and that all contingencies have been properly addressed."

The result was a level of micromanagement that would have been impressive in a royal bureaucracy but proved somewhat overwhelming when applied to what should have been straightforward military planning. Every decision required extended consultation, every suggestion prompted detailed analysis, and every interaction between Gathelus and Brigantia received the sort of scrutiny typically reserved for diplomatic negotiations between rival kingdoms.

Finally, recognizing that further resistance would only prolong the inevitable, Gathelus accepted Scota's participation with the resigned grace of a commander accommodating political realities beyond his control. "Very well," he said with diplomatic neutrality that concealed his awareness of the emotional dynamics involved. "Your participation will certainly contribute additional perspectives and capabilities to our mission."

Thus, it was that our reconnaissance expedition into giant territory acquired a complexity that far exceeded what would have been necessary for purely military purposes. What should have been a small, swift scouting party became an elaborate diplomatic and romantic adventure involving a lovestruck princess, an oblivious warrior-prince, a friendly Celtic guide whose very existence constituted a perceived threat, and a scribe whose primary qualifications were cartographic skill and the dubious privilege of recording whatever extraordinary events might ensue.

Our preparations for departure from Breogan's Tower provided additional opportunities for comedy as Scota continued her campaign to ensure proper supervision of all interactions between Gathelus and Brigantia. The princess's sudden interest in Celtic geography, mountain warfare tactics, and local customs reached levels of enthusiasm that would have been admirable if they had been motivated by purely intellectual curiosity.

"I find the Celtic approach to wilderness navigation absolutely fascinating," she announced while reviewing maps with exaggerated interest. "The way Brigantia describes the use of natural landmarks and seasonal patterns is so much more sophisticated than anything we learned in Egyptian court education."

Brigantia, whose straightforward Celtic nature had not prepared her for the subtle complexities of Mediterranean romantic rivalry, responded to these overtures with the friendly enthusiasm she would have shown to any genuine student of her people's traditions. Her detailed explanations and helpful demonstrations only provided additional opportunities for misunderstanding, as Scota interpreted every smile and gesture through the lens of perceived romantic competition.

The final preparations were completed during the week following our initial contact with the Celtic tribes, allowing time for both practical arrangements and the diplomatic discussions necessary to establish formal cooperation between our mission and the local chieftains. The sacred relics we carried—particularly the stone of destiny and the sword of Goliath—drew intense interest from the Celtic people, though their traditional priests viewed them with mixed emotions of awe and resentment. While many common folk saw these artifacts as confirmation of divine power, the established religious authorities were troubled by implications that their own practices might be called into question by the arrival of Hebrew revelation.

It was during this period that we made perhaps the most remarkable discovery of our entire sojourn in Iberia. While exploring the ancient chambers beneath Breogan's Tower, Gathelus uncovered a sealed vault containing artifacts and documents that would transform his understanding of his own heritage in ways that exceeded even the previous revelations about Zerahite bloodlines and Celtic connections.

The chamber itself was a marvel of ancient engineering, carved from the living rock and sealed with stones that bore inscriptions in scripts that predated any known Celtic writing. The air within was dry and still, preserving contents that might otherwise have crumbled to dust over the centuries since their placement.

Among the treasures we discovered were weapons and ornaments of obvious Trojan manufacture, bronze tablets inscribed with genealogical records, and most remarkably, a scroll case containing documents that traced the royal lineage of Troy through various migrations and settlements to the very bloodlines that had produced Gathelus himself.

"By the Name," Gathelus whispered as he studied the ancient genealogy by torchlight, his voice carrying a wonder that transcended mere historical curiosity. "Here is recorded the lineage of Brutus the Trojan, first king of Britain, descendant of Aeneas and heir to the royal blood of Troy itself."

The implications of this discovery were staggering, adding yet another layer to the already complex web of divine purposes that had brought our company to this place at this time. Not only was Gathelus descended from the Zerahite branch of Judah's tribe, but his ancestry also included the royal house of Troy, making him heir to both Hebrew promises and classical heroic traditions.

"The prophecies speak truly," Jeremiah observed as he examined the ancient records with the keen eye of one well-versed in genealogical verification. "The bloodlines of east and west have been preserved and brought together for purposes that extend beyond any single nation or kingdom. The Most High has prepared vessels worthy of the blessings He intends to pour out upon the earth."

This revelation provided the perfect conclusion to our initial sojourn at Breogan's Tower, confirming that divine providence had guided every step of our journey and prepared every element necessary for the fulfilment of sacred purposes. The Celtic peoples had been preserved as descendants of scattered Israel, the land had been prepared as a staging ground for future expansion, and Gathelus had been revealed as the chosen instrument for uniting ancient bloodlines in service of eternal purposes.

Yet even as we celebrated these remarkable discoveries, the romantic complications that had plagued our mission planning continued to provide entertainment for that observant enough to recognize the subtle warfare being waged between the principals involved. Gathelus, for all his tactical brilliance in conventional military matters, remained utterly mystified by Princess Scota's increasingly possessive behaviour and her apparent obsession with monitoring his every interaction with other members of our expanded company.

"I cannot understand what has gotten into the princess," he confided to me one evening as we reviewed the maps that would guide our upcoming expedition. "She seemeth determined to involve herself in every aspect of our mission planning, yet her questions suggest concerns that go far beyond legitimate tactical considerations."

The example he cited was particularly telling. During a routine discussion of supply requirements, Scota had launched into an extended interrogation about why Brigantia's expertise was supposedly essential for our success, whether her participation was truly necessary for mission completion, and whether there might not be other, equally qualified guides available who could provide the same services without requiring such... close collaboration.

"She asked me seventeen separate questions about Brigantia's qualifications," Gathelus continued with the bewildered tone of a man attempting to solve an incomprehensible puzzle. "Seventeen! As if I were planning to appoint her to some permanent position of authority rather than simply utilizing her knowledge of local geography for a single reconnaissance mission."

I found myself in the uncomfortable position of observing romantic drama unfold while being unable to offer enlightenment to the principal male participant, whose obliviousness to feminine psychology was so complete that direct explanation would have been both inappropriate and probably ineffective. Instead, I offered the sort of diplomatically neutral responses that allowed him to continue his analysis without receiving guidance that might have resolved his confusion.

"Perhaps," I suggested carefully, "the princess is simply demonstrating the thoroughness that royal training instils in matters of planning and preparation."

"But that does not explain her sudden interest in Celtic archery techniques," Gathelus replied, clearly struggling to find logical explanations for behaviour that defied rational analysis. "Yesterday she spent two hours questioning Brigantia about bow construction and arrow fletching, subjects that have never previously captured her attention despite months of weapons training aboard the Sea Serpent."

What he failed to recognize, but which was obvious to every other observer, was that Scota's sudden expertise in Celtic martial arts was motivated less by genuine intellectual curiosity than by determination to remain present during any conversation that might develop into something approaching personal intimacy between her chosen romantic target and her perceived rival.

The situation reached its peak of absurdity when Scota began offering to assist with tasks that clearly required no assistance, simply to ensure her presence during any activity that might otherwise allow Gathelus and Brigantia to interact without supervision. Her volunteer services included helping with map annotation (despite having no cartographic training), participating in equipment inspections (despite lacking familiarity with Celtic gear), and providing additional perspective on route selection (despite never having visited the territories under discussion).

"The princess has become remarkably helpful," Gathelus observed with the innocent appreciation of someone who had not grasped the true motivations behind such dedicated assistance. "Her willingness to contribute to every aspect of our preparation demonstrates the sort of commitment to shared responsibility that will serve her well in future governance."

It was at this point that Brigantia herself, whose straightforward Celtic nature had not initially prepared her for the subtleties of Mediterranean romantic rivalry, finally recognized the true source of Scota's intensified involvement in mission planning. Rather than taking offense at what could have been interpreted as questioning of her competence or integrity, the Celtic maiden responded with a combination of amusement and sympathy that demonstrated both wisdom and genuine kindness.

"Methinks," she said to me privately one afternoon, "that your princess may be labouring under certain... misapprehensions... regarding the nature of my interest in your Greek warrior."

When I encouraged her to elaborate, Brigantia's explanation revealed that she had been observing the romantic dynamics within our company with the same keen attention she devoted to tracking giant movements through mountain terrain.

"She seems to believe that I pose some sort of threat to her obvious claim upon Gathelus’ affections," Brigantia continued with a smile that suggested both understanding and gentle amusement. "The poor dear has been working herself into a state of exhaustion trying to prevent interactions that were never intended to be anything more than professional cooperation."

It was then that she proposed a solution that would not only resolve the immediate romantic complications but also provide our company with additional military capabilities that would prove invaluable during our upcoming campaigns against the giant clans.

"Perhaps," she suggested with the diplomatic skill that had made her one of the most respected scouts in her father's territory, "it would be appropriate for me to introduce the company to my betrothed, who has been training with your warrior Korax for several weeks now and has developed quite a reputation among our people for his prowess with both sword and tactical planning."

The introduction of Cador ap Geraint proved to be exactly the sort of dramatic revelation that would have been impossible to orchestrate through conventional planning but which divine providence seemed to delight in providing at precisely the right moment. The young Celtic warrior was everything that romantic literature might have described as the ideal heroic figure—tall and golden-haired, with the sort of classical features that would have impressed Greek sculptors, combined with the bearing and confidence that marked him as both natural leader and formidable fighter.

"I am honoured to meet the legendary warrior whose reputation has spread throughout the Celtic territories," Cador announced as he was formally presented to Gathelus, his manner combining appropriate respect with the sort of confident bearing that spoke of extensive combat experience. "Korax has told me much about your capabilities, and I confess that I have been hoping for an opportunity to test my skills against a master whose fame has reached even these distant shores."
The transformation in Gathelus’ demeanour upon meeting a fellow warrior whose interests aligned perfectly with his own was remarkable to observe. Where he had been mystified by feminine psychology and romantic complications, he immediately grasped the straightforward dynamics of martial challenge and professional respect.
