"The honour is mine," he replied with the genuine warmth that always marked his interactions with serious fighting men. "Korax speaks highly of your abilities, and I would welcome the opportunity to measure my skills against a warrior trained in the Celtic traditions."

"Then perhaps," Cador suggested with the sort of smile that indicated he had been planning this moment for some time, "we might arrange a formal contest that would allow both of us to demonstrate our capabilities while providing entertainment for our respective companies. I would be honoured to offer my services to your cause, with the understanding that such an arrangement might include opportunities for the sort of professional development that can only come through combat with skilled opponents."
The immediate effect of this proposal upon Princess Scota was both dramatic and amusing. The romantic anxiety that had been driving her increasingly erratic behaviour dissolved instantly as she realized that Brigantia's attentions had been focused not upon romantic conquest but upon securing military alliance for her betrothed. The relief that flooded her features was so obvious that even Gathelus, despite his previous obliviousness, could not fail to recognize that something significant had changed in the emotional dynamics of the situation.

"A formal combat," Scota said slowly, her voice carrying the tone of someone whose worldview had just been fundamentally restructured. "Between thine and Gathelus. To demonstrate... professional capabilities."

"Unfortunately, yea," Brigantia replied with a resigned sigh that immediately revealed her true feelings about her betrothed's proposal. "Cador has been obsessing over such an opportunity since he first heard of Gathelus’ arrival in our territory. He believeth that combat between masters of different traditions provides the best possible training for both participants, though I confess I find the entire notion rather... theatrical."

The princess's initial reaction to this revelation demonstrated that her relief at discovering the true nature of Brigantia's interests was tempered by growing concerns about the wisdom of formal duelling as a method of military recruitment.

"I find the entire concept rather primitive," she declared with the sort of royal disapproval that suggested she had strong opinions about civilized approaches to personnel management. "Surely there are more sophisticated methods for evaluating martial competence than requiring grown men to engage in elaborate displays of violence for the entertainment of spectators."

Her criticism found an immediate and enthusiastic supporter in Brigantia, whose own reservations about her betrothed's enthusiasm for formal combat had been a source of ongoing frustration during the weeks of preparation leading up to our arrival.

"I must confess that I share your concerns," the Celtic maiden replied with the sort of diplomatic honesty that immediately established common ground between the two women. "Cador's dedication to martial excellence is admirable, but his boyish enthusiasm for dramatic gestures sometimes exceeds what practical necessity might require."

This shared perspective on masculine priorities and theatrical inclinations provided the foundation for what would develop into a genuine friendship between the two women, though their initial alliance was forged through mutual disapproval of what they considered unnecessarily elaborate approaches to straightforward military cooperation.

"Men," Scota observed with the weary wisdom of someone who had spent considerable time attempting to understand masculine psychology, "seem constitutionally incapable of accomplishing anything important without first engaging in some form of competitive display designed to establish relative status within their group."

"Indeed," Brigantia agreed with feeling. "One might think that reputation, recommendation, and demonstrated competence would suffice for rational evaluation of military capabilities, but apparently such sensible approaches lack the dramatic appeal that makes male decision-making so unnecessarily complicated."

Despite their philosophical objections to the proposed combat, both women found themselves caught up in the excitement that began to build throughout the Celtic territories as word spread of the impending duel between the legendary Greek warrior and the greatest swordsman of their own people. What had begun as a simple proposal for professional development was rapidly evolving into a major cultural event that would draw spectators from tribes throughout Iberia.

The twins, Nikias and Philippos, recognized immediately that such a gathering provided unprecedented opportunities for the sort of entrepreneurial activities that had supplemented their income throughout our journey. Their experience with organizing entertainment and managing complex logistics made them natural coordinators for the expanded festivities that began to develop around the central combat.

"Brother," Nikias observed to Philippos as they surveyed the growing crowd of Celtic warriors and their families, "I believe we have discovered the sort of business opportunity that occurs perhaps once in a lifetime."

"Indeed," Philippos replied with the sort of calculating expression that had preceded their most successful ventures. "A gathering of this magnitude, with participants from tribes throughout the region, provides ideal conditions for multiple forms of entertainment and commerce."
Their proposal for a comprehensive festival of martial arts and athletic competition transformed what might have been a simple duel into a celebration that would be remembered throughout Celtic history. The program they developed included archery competitions featuring Theron against the finest Celtic bowmen, javelin throwing contests that would test the skills of warriors from multiple tribes, and tests of strength that would provide opportunities for dramatic displays of physical prowess.

Korax, whose experience as a military trainer had included considerable expertise in organizing competitive events, accepted the role of official commentator and analyst for the various contests. His knowledge of both Greek and Celtic fighting traditions made him uniquely qualified to explain the significance of various techniques and tactical approaches to spectators who might lack the specialized knowledge necessary to appreciate the finer points of combat.

"Ladies and gentlemen," he announced to the assembled crowd with the sort of theatrical flair that had made him popular among the soldiers he had trained, "what you are about to witness represents the meeting of two great martial traditions—the disciplined techniques of Mediterranean warfare and the fierce individualism of Celtic combat. Each approach has its own strengths and characteristics, and the contest between these masters will demonstrate the highest levels of skill that either tradition can produce."

The preliminary contests provided entertainment that exceeded even the most optimistic expectations. Theron's archery demonstration against the best Celtic bowmen resulted in a display of precision and consistency that earned genuine admiration from competitors who had been skeptical of foreign techniques. The javelin competition showcased the remarkable throwing abilities of warriors trained in terrain where long-range accuracy could mean the difference between successful hunting and starvation.

The tests of strength proved particularly popular with spectators, as they provided opportunities for dramatic displays of physical capability that required no specialized knowledge to appreciate. Celtic warriors whose reputations had been built upon their ability to lift massive stones or bend iron bars found themselves competing against Greeks whose training had emphasized different forms of physical development.

Throughout these preliminary events, the betting organized by the twins created an atmosphere of excitement and anticipation that drew participants from social levels that might not normally have interacted. Celtic chieftains found themselves wagering alongside common warriors, while the women of various tribes compared notes on the relative merits of different fighting styles and tactical approaches.

"The Greek archer demonstrates remarkable consistency," observed one Celtic matron as she watched Theron's performance, "but I note that his technique lacks the sort of instinctive adaptability that our own bowmen develop through hunting in varied terrain."

"True," replied her companion, "but such consistency might prove valuable in battle conditions where psychological pressure could disrupt the concentration required for more intuitive approaches."

These discussions, multiplied throughout the gathering crowd, created an intellectual atmosphere that elevated the event beyond mere entertainment into something approaching a cultural symposium on the nature of warfare and martial excellence.

But it was the main event—the formal duel between Gathelus and Cador—that provided the climax toward which all other activities had been building. The combat circle that had been prepared for their contest was large enough to accommodate the sort of mobile fighting that both warriors preferred, while the elevated seating that had been constructed allowed spectators to observe every detail of the encounter.

The two fighters presented a striking contrast as they entered the circle and saluted each other with the formal courtesy that marked civilized combat. Gathelus, with his Mediterranean colouring and compact, muscular build, carried himself with the sort of disciplined precision that spoke of extensive training in multiple weapon systems. Cador, taller and more lightly built, moved with the fluid grace characteristic of Celtic warriors whose fighting style emphasized speed and adaptability over raw power.

"Gentlemen," announced Cathbad the Chief Druid, who had been selected to serve as formal judge for the contest, "you are about to engage in combat that will be remembered in the songs of our people for generations to come. Let your skills honour the traditions you represent, and may the best warrior claim victory through merit rather than chance."

The opening exchanges established immediately that both combatants possessed the sort of technical mastery that separated true masters from merely competent fighters. Gathelus’ initial attacks demonstrated the sort of systematic approach characteristic of Greek military training, with each movement flowing logically from the previous one in patterns designed to create openings while maintaining defensive capability.

Cador's responses revealed the more intuitive approach typical of Celtic combat, where individual creativity and adaptability were valued over adherence to predetermined sequences. His counters seemed to emerge spontaneously from the immediate tactical situation, creating combinations that were both unexpected and remarkably effective.

"Note," Korax explained to the fascinated spectators, "how the Greek fighter maintains perfect distance control while executing his attacks. Each movement is calculated to achieve maximum effect while minimizing exposure to counterattack. This represents the disciplined approach that has made Mediterranean armies so effective in large-scale warfare."

"But observe," he continued as Cador launched a series of rapid combinations that forced Gathelus to rely upon his defensive skills, "how the Celtic warrior adapts his technique to exploit the specific characteristics of his opponent's style. Rather than adhering to fixed patterns, he creates new approaches based upon the immediate requirements of the combat situation."

The duel continued for nearly an hour, with both fighters demonstrating levels of skill and endurance that impressed even the most experienced warriors among the spectators. Neither combatant was able to establish a decisive advantage, though both created opportunities that might have proved conclusive against less skilled opponents.

The turning point came when Gathelus, recognizing that conventional approaches would not be sufficient against an opponent of Cador's calibre, began to introduce elements of unpredictability that drew upon his experiences with supernatural enemies and divine intervention. His attacks became less systematic but more creative, incorporating movements and combinations that defied conventional tactical analysis.

"Interesting," Korax observed as the nature of the combat began to change. "The Greek warrior is abandoning the structured approach that has served him well throughout the earlier phases of this contest. He seems to be developing new techniques even as the combat proceeds."

This shift in approach proved decisive. Cador, whose defensive skills had been calibrated to counter the disciplined precision of conventional Greek combat, found himself struggling to adapt to an opponent whose movements had become genuinely unpredictable. A series of attacks that should have been easily parried instead created openings that forced him into increasingly desperate defensive measures.

The final exchange was so rapid and complex that even experienced warriors had difficulty following the sequence of movements that led to Cador's defeat. Gathelus, having created an opening through a combination that seemed to violate basic principles of tactical logic, pressed his advantage with a series of attacks that overwhelmed his opponent's defences and forced him to yield.

"Victory to Gathelus of the Red Hand!" Cathbad announced as the exhausted combatants separated and saluted each other with the mutual respect of masters who had tested each other's skills to the fullest extent.
Cador's response to his defeat demonstrated exactly the sort of character that had made him legendary among his own people. Rather than showing disappointment or making excuses, he immediately acknowledged the superiority of his opponent's performance and offered the sort of formal allegiance that Celtic tradition reserved for the most respected leaders.

"I have been privileged to face a warrior whose skills exceed my own," he declared with genuine admiration. "If you will accept my service, I pledge my sword to your cause and my loyalty to whatever purposes the Most High has prepared for your mission."

The celebration that followed this formal exchange of oaths transformed the entire gathering into a feast that would be remembered throughout Celtic history. The various tribes that had attended the competition found themselves united in appreciation for the martial excellence they had witnessed, while the betting organized by the twins had created a network of social connections that would prove valuable for future diplomatic cooperation.

Princess Scota, her romantic anxieties finally resolved and her friendship with Brigantia firmly established, found herself able to enjoy the festivities with the sort of royal grace that had been absent during her previous state of romantic uncertainty.

"I must confess," she said to Brigantia as they observed the continuing celebrations, "that despite my initial reservations about the wisdom of formal combat, the contest we have witnessed was genuinely impressive. Both warriors demonstrated levels of skill that commanded respect regardless of one's philosophical position regarding the necessity of such displays."

"Indeed," Brigantia agreed with the satisfaction of someone whose betrothed had acquitted himself with honour despite his defeat. "And the broader results of this gathering suggest that your Greek warrior has succeeded in establishing exactly the sort of unified leadership that our people have needed for effective resistance against the giant clans."

Yet even as we celebrated these remarkable achievements, the sounds of distress from a nearby village reminded us that immediate practical challenges demanded our attention before we could focus upon the broader implications of our mission. The giant clans had struck again, terrorizing an impoverished settlement whose only crime had been their refusal to submit to supernatural tyranny.

"We cannot ignore such suffering," Gathelus declared as reports reached us of the latest atrocity. "Whatever broader purposes await us in distant lands, our immediate duty is clear. These people are under our protection now, and their enemies shall learn that the power of the Most High stands guard over the innocent."

Thus began the campaign that would establish Gathelus as the legendary Red Hand King of Celtic prophecy, though that is a tale for future volumes of this chronicle. Our reconnaissance expedition into giant territory marked the beginning of adventures that would test every aspect of our faith, courage, and divine calling, while the romantic complications that accompanied our mission would provide ongoing entertainment for those privileged to witness the unfolding of both sacred history and very human comedy.

As I prepared my maps and gathered my writing materials for the journey ahead, I reflected upon the remarkable providence that had brought us safely through so many perils to this place of revelation and renewed purpose. Behind us lay the smoking ruins of Jerusalem and the collapse of earthly kingdoms, but ahead stretched possibilities that exceeded the wildest dreams of mortal imagination.

We were no longer merely refugees carrying sacred relics toward uncertain sanctuary, but instruments of divine purpose prepared for the establishment of a kingdom whose influence would indeed extend to the uttermost parts of the earth. The adventure was far from over—in many ways, it was just beginning.

The Celtic bards would sing of what followed for generations uncounted, but that is matter for another chapter in this chronicle of the Stone and the Sceptre. For now, it was enough to know that the Most High had brought us safely to the place of His choosing, surrounded us with kinsmen we had never known existed, and prepared us for trials and triumphs that would require every ounce of faith, courage, and divine blessing we could muster.

The Red Hand banner flew proudly from Breogan's Tower as we prepared for departure, while the Tender Twig of David's line stood ready to be planted in whatever soil the Almighty had prepared for its flourishing. The greatest adventures still lay ahead, but the foundation had been laid for wonders that would echo through the ages.
 
Chapter 12: The Parting of the Sisters

 As compiled by Baruch ben Neriah

 In which the sacred bloodline divides to fulfil twin destinies, urgent visions compel westward departure, and ancient prophecies speak of kingdoms yet to come

The weeks that followed the great duel at Breogan's Tower brought both healing and a growing sense that our company stood at a crossroads where divine purpose would require the most difficult of choices. The Sea Serpent rested in the harbor like a patient beast, her battle wounds mended and her timbers strengthened for whatever trials lay ahead, yet the very completeness of her restoration seemed to emphasize the approaching moment when decisions could no longer be postponed.

It was during this period of seeming tranquillity that the true magnitude of our mission's complexity began to reveal itself through dreams and visions that carried the weight of divine urgency. Princess Tea Tephi, whose prophetic gifts had been growing stronger throughout our journey, found herself increasingly troubled by nocturnal revelations that spoke of distant shores and approaching dangers that required immediate response.

"The dreams come every night now," she confided to Jeremiah during one of their private consultations, her voice carrying the strain of someone who had been wrestling with divine communications too powerful for easy comprehension. "I see green islands shrouded in mist, ancient stones arranged in patterns that speak of forgotten wisdom, and voices calling from across the western sea—urgent voices that warn of preparations being made by forces that would oppose our mission."
The prophet listened with the grave attention he always accorded to matters of spiritual significance, his experienced understanding of divine communication allowing him to recognize the authentic character of her visions. "Tell me more about these dreams," he urged gently. "What do the voices say? What preparations do you see?"

Tea Tephi's account of her nightly experiences painted a picture of spiritual warfare that transcended ordinary political or military concerns. In her dreams, she witnessed ancient powers stirring in the western lands, awakening from centuries of slumber to oppose the coming of the sacred bloodline that would establish divine authority in territories they considered their own domain.

"I see standing stones that pulse with malevolent energy," she continued, her voice dropping to a whisper as if speaking too loudly might attract unwanted attention from the powers she described. "Dark figures moving through pagan groves, corrupting ancient rituals and binding the land itself to their will. And beneath it all, a growing awareness that our coming has been detected, that forces are marshalling to prevent the fulfilment of whatever purposes the Most High has planned for the western islands."

The urgency that accompanied these visions was unlike anything Tea Tephi had previously experienced. Where her earlier troubled dreams had provided glimpses of possible futures, these new impressions carried a compelling force that seemed to demand immediate action. The spiritual enemies she perceived were not merely preparing defences—they were actively working to corrupt the very land that was destined to receive the transplanted royal heritage.

"Time is running short," she said with the conviction of one who had been deeply moved by what she had seen. "Every day we delay gives our enemies additional opportunity to strengthen their position and corrupt the soil that should receive the Tender Twig of David's line. The urgency grows stronger each night, as if the very land itself pleads for swift arrival before the spiritual darkness can complete its work."

Jeremiah's response to these revelations confirmed his own growing awareness of the supernatural opposition that awaited them beyond the horizon. "I too have sensed the stirring of ancient powers," he admitted with the gravity appropriate to such matters. "The enemy has not been idle during our journey, and our victories over the Rephaim pirates have apparently alerted darker forces to the threat we represent to their dominion over the western lands."

This divine urgency created a painful dilemma for our company, as it became increasingly clear that the Celtic peoples had developed a desperate need for the leadership that only Gathelus and Princess Scota could provide. The giant clans that had long terrorized the human settlements were showing signs of increased organization and malevolent purpose, while reports from throughout Iberia spoke of a new level of threat that exceeded anything previously encountered.

"The situation grows more dire with each passing day," Viriatus explained during one of our council meetings, his weathered face reflecting the strain of constant vigilance against supernatural enemies. "The giant clans have ceased their random raiding and have begun coordinating their attacks in ways that suggest unified command. Our scouts report gatherings in the mountain fastnesses—gatherings that include creatures whose very presence seems to drain the life from the surrounding landscape."
The intelligence that reached us from throughout the Celtic territories painted a picture of escalating supernatural warfare that threatened the very existence of human civilization in Iberia. The giant clans, previously content with sporadic raids and territorial disputes, had apparently received new leadership that was transforming them into a coordinated force capable of systematic conquest.

"We have identified their new leader," Brennus reported with the grim authority of one who had witnessed evidence of powers beyond human comprehension. "He calls himself Balor of the Poisonous Eye, and the very mention of his name sends terror through warriors who have faced death in conventional battle without flinching. This is no ordinary giant, but a Nephilim king whose supernatural abilities exceed anything our people have encountered in living memory."

The descriptions of this new enemy that reached us from survivors of his attacks matched the most disturbing accounts from Hebrew tradition regarding the offspring of fallen angels. Balor possessed not only the physical strength characteristic of his kind but also malevolent spiritual powers that could corrupt the very ground upon which he stood, withering crops and poisoning water sources through his mere presence.

"His gaze alone can kill," Ambiorix added with the haunted expression of one who had witnessed such power firsthand. "I have seen warriors freeze in their tracks when his attention falls upon them, their very life force draining away until they collapse as withered husks. No human weapon can stand against such supernatural malice, and our traditional tactics prove useless against an enemy who commands both physical and spiritual forces."

The implications of these reports were staggering. The Celtic peoples, for all their courage and martial skill, were facing an enemy whose supernatural nature gave him overwhelming advantages in any conventional conflict. Without leadership capable of coordinating both military resistance and spiritual protection, the human settlements of Iberia would be systematically destroyed by forces that seemed to delight in corruption and devastation.

It was in this context that the true extent of Gathelus’ transformation became apparent. The Greek prince who had begun our journey as a skilled but essentially secular warrior had evolved into something far more significant—a divinely appointed leader whose supernatural gifts and spiritual authority made him uniquely qualified to oppose enemies that exceeded human limitations.

"The strength that the Most High has granted you," Jeremiah explained to Gathelus during one of their private discussions, "was not given merely for your personal development or the protection of our immediate mission. Like the gifts bestowed upon the judges and kings of ancient Israel, divine blessing carries responsibility for the protection of entire peoples and the establishment of righteous order in the earth."

The prophet's words carried the weight of divine commission, making it clear that Gathelus’ role in our mission had grown beyond simple military escort to encompass the sort of leadership that shaped the destinies of nations. The Celtic peoples had not merely found a capable commander—they had discovered the fulfilment of their most sacred prophecies regarding the Red Hand King who would unite the scattered tribes and establish a realm worthy of divine blessing.

"I understand the magnitude of what you are asking," Gathelus replied with the humility that had marked his response to every divine calling since his conversion. "These people have become more than allies or temporary companions—they are kinsmen whose blood calls to my own, fellow servants of the Most High whose suffering demands response from any who possess the power to provide relief."

The decision that emerged from these discussions was as inevitable as it was painful. Gathelus and Princess Scota would remain in Iberia to lead the unified Celtic confederation against the supernatural enemies that threatened human civilization, while the remainder of our company would continue the westward journey to fulfil the ultimate purposes of our mission in the distant islands that called so urgently through Tea Tephi's dreams.

The prospect of dividing the royal bloodline that had been preserved through such extraordinary trials was sobering beyond measure, yet the wisdom of such a course became increasingly apparent as we considered the divine purposes that had brought us to this crossroads. The sacred heritage we carried was not meant to be concentrated in a single location but rather to be planted in multiple realms where it could take root and flourish according to the various needs of the peoples prepared to receive it.

"The Lord's ways are manifold," Jeremiah observed with the profound understanding that came from decades of witnessing divine providence work through seemingly impossible circumstances. "His planting is vast, and His purposes are deeper than the deepest sea. What appears to be division is actually multiplication—the establishment of multiple centres of divine blessing that will serve different aspects of His eternal plan."

The twins, Nikias and Philippos, whose entrepreneurial activities had created extensive networks of social and economic connections throughout the Celtic territories, announced their decision to remain with Gathelus and Scota with characteristic directness.

"Brother," Nikias explained to me as we discussed the implications of their choice, "we have spent weeks building relationships and establishing trust with traders and craftsmen throughout these lands. The social networks we have created will prove invaluable in uniting the scattered tribes under the Red Hand banner, but only if we remain to maintain and strengthen these connections."

"Moreover," Philippos added with the practical wisdom that had made their business ventures so successful, "the economic foundations for a unified Celtic confederation must be established through ongoing effort and careful management. The temporary unity forged through shared military necessity will not endure without the sort of economic integration that requires sustained attention and specialized knowledge."

Their decision to remain was both logical and generous, as it represented a significant sacrifice of their own prospects for the sake of the greater good. The twins had become wealthy through their various enterprises during our journey, but they were willing to invest both their resources and their expertise in the establishment of a realm that would serve divine purposes rather than merely their personal advancement.

The most difficult decision involved Korax, whose friendship with Gathelus had deepened throughout our journey into a bond of brotherhood that transcended ordinary military relationships. The grizzled warrior's desire to remain with his closest companion was obvious to all who observed them, yet the practical needs of our continuing mission required his specialized knowledge and leadership capabilities.

"My friend," Gathelus said to Korax during one of their private conversations, his voice heavy with the emotional weight of necessary separation, "I would keep you by my side if circumstances permitted, but the protection of Jeremiah and Princess Tea Tephi requires the sort of military expertise that only you can provide. The enemies they will face in the western islands may prove even more dangerous than the giant clans that threaten us here."

Korax's response demonstrated the selfless loyalty that had made him such a valued member of our company. "I understand the necessity," he replied with the stoic acceptance characteristic of professional soldiers who had learned to subordinate personal desires to mission requirements. "The prophet and the princess carry the primary burden of our sacred trust, and their protection must take precedence over all other considerations."

The arrangement that emerged from these discussions provided a solution that addressed both practical needs and emotional bonds. Cador ap Geraint, whose skills had been proven in combat against Gathelus himself, would assume responsibility for the Greek prince's protection while Korax continued westward with the remainder of our company. The formal oath-taking that accompanied this arrangement became a ceremony that would be remembered throughout Celtic history.

"I swear by the Most High and by the honour of mine ancestors," Cador declared as he knelt before Gathelus with his sword across his knees, "that I will defend thy life with my own, shield thy family from all enemies, and serve thine cause with complete loyalty until death releases me from this obligation."

The solemnity of this oath was tempered by the characteristic Celtic humour that had made Cador popular throughout our company. "And perhaps most crucially," he added with a mischievous grin that drew hearty laughter from the assembled witnesses, "I pledge to protect thee from Princess Scota's justifiable wrath whenever thy legendary obliviousness to her romantic overtures reaches truly spectacular levels of masculine blindness. I shall consider it my sacred duty to translate her increasingly desperate hints into language even a battle-hardened warrior can comprehend, and to shield you from flying objects when your cluelessness finally drives her to violence. Truly, no giant or supernatural monster could pose a greater threat to thy well-being than an exasperated princess armed with whatever comes to hand."

Gathelus, not to be outdone, shot back with a wry smile, "Then let us make a pact, Cador: when the day cometh that Brigantia's patience with thy own heroic recklessness at last runneth dry, I shall stand ready to shield thee from whatever implements she may hurl in thy direction. Between the two of us, perhaps we shall survive the perils of ambiguous love as well as those of coherent battle!"

At this, both Scota and Brigantia—who stood nearby as witnesses—fixed the two men with a glare so formidable and perfectly synchronized that even the bravest warriors present felt a chill run down their spines. For a fleeting moment, it seemed the Fates themselves had paused to take note, and Gathelus and Cador exchanged a look that suggested they might, after all, have spoken a jest too far.

The selection of Celtic warriors who would accompany our continuing journey to the western islands required careful consideration of both martial capabilities and spiritual preparedness for the supernatural challenges that awaited them. The thirteen men who volunteered for this service represented the finest warriors from throughout the Celtic territories, each bringing specialized skills that would prove essential for the trials ahead.

Caratacus the Bold, whose reputation as a swordsman was exceeded only by his fame as a leader of men, would serve as the senior Celtic warrior in our reduced company. His experience in fighting giant clans had taught him the sort of tactics necessary for combat against supernatural enemies, while his natural authority would prove valuable in maintaining discipline during the challenges ahead.

Caswallon the Swift, whose speed and agility had made him legendary among Celtic scouts, brought knowledge of stealth and reconnaissance that would be essential for navigating territories where conventional approaches might prove suicidal. His ability to move unseen through hostile terrain had saved countless lives during previous campaigns against the giant clans.

Bran the Wise, despite his youth, possessed the sort of intuitive understanding of spiritual matters that made him particularly valuable for missions involving supernatural opposition. His ability to sense the presence of malevolent forces had proven crucial in several encounters with giant scouts, while his knowledge of ancient lore might prove essential for understanding the enemies we would face in the western islands.

Llyr of the Western Winds, whose mastery of seamanship equalled that of any Mediterranean sailor, would provide naval expertise that might prove crucial for island warfare. His knowledge of western waters and weather patterns could make the difference between successful navigation and disaster in the treacherous seas that lay ahead.

Pryderi the Strong, whose physical prowess had been demonstrated in the contests that preceded the great duel, brought the sort of raw power that might prove necessary for combat against enemies whose supernatural strength exceeded human limits. His courage in facing overwhelming odds had been proven in countless battles against the giant clans.

Gwydion the Clever, whose tactical innovations had revolutionized Celtic approaches to warfare, would serve as military advisor for operations in unfamiliar terrain. His ability to devise unconventional solutions to seemingly impossible problems had saved numerous Celtic settlements from destruction.

Nuada of the Silver Hand, whose skill with weapons was matched by his knowledge of Celtic law and tradition, would provide the cultural expertise necessary for establishing legitimate authority in whatever realms we might encounter. His understanding of royal protocol and diplomatic procedure would prove essential for the political aspects of our mission.

Lugh the Many-Skilled, whose mastery of numerous crafts and techniques made him invaluable for expedition support, would handle the practical aspects of maintaining our equipment and supplies during extended operations. His ability to repair weapons, construct shelter, and provide medical care could prove crucial for survival in hostile territory.

Manannan of the Mists, whose knowledge of ancient mysteries had made him a respected figure among Celtic druids, brought spiritual insights that might prove essential for understanding the supernatural forces we would encounter. His ability to perceive hidden dangers and interpret omens could provide crucial advantages in spiritual warfare.

Cuchulainn the Fierce, whose berserker rage in battle had become legendary throughout Celtic territories, would serve as shock troops for situations requiring overwhelming force. His ability to inspire terror in enemies and fight with supernatural ferocity had proven decisive in numerous encounters with giant clans.

Finn the Fair, whose diplomatic skills had prevented numerous conflicts through negotiation rather than warfare, would handle relations with whatever peoples we might encounter in the western islands. His ability to establish trust and find common ground could prove essential for securing local cooperation.

Cormac the Just, whose reputation for fairness and wisdom had made him a respected judge among Celtic peoples, would provide the moral authority necessary for establishing legitimate governance in new territories. His understanding of justice and law would be crucial for winning the loyalty of potential subjects.
Diarmuid the Beloved, whose charismatic personality had made him popular throughout Celtic society, would serve as liaison between our company and whatever populations we might encounter. His ability to inspire confidence and affection could prove invaluable for securing popular support for our mission.

The formal ceremony that marked the selection of these warriors and their oath-taking became a significant event in Celtic history, establishing bonds of loyalty and purpose that would endure through whatever trials lay ahead. Each man understood that he was volunteering for a mission that would likely require the ultimate sacrifice, yet their commitment to the sacred purposes we served overcame any personal considerations of safety or comfort.

The emotional climax of our preparations came with the formal parting of the two royal sisters, whose bond had been forged through shared trials and mutual dependence during the most challenging period of their lives. The prospect of separation, possibly permanent, required both women to confront the reality that their individual destinies might diverge despite their common heritage and shared mission.

"Sister mine," spake Tea Tephi unto Scota as they embraced for what might be the final time, their tears flowing freely as they contemplated the uncertainties that lay ahead, "though we part in body, we remain united in spirit and purpose. The blood that floweth in our veins doth carry the same promises, and the God who hath preserved us through such trials shall surely watch over both our paths."

Scota's response carried the mixture of sorrow and determination that had marked her character throughout our journey. "The Lord hath shown us that His purposes are greater than our personal desires," she replied with the regal dignity that had never abandoned her despite the hardships she had endured. "If He doth require us to serve in different realms, then we shall trust that His wisdom exceedeth our understanding and that His blessings shall follow both our lines."

The prophecy that Jeremiah spoke over the parting sisters would be remembered and repeated throughout the generations that followed, as it addressed not only their immediate separation but the ultimate purposes that would be fulfilled through their descendants in ages yet to come.

"My daughters," the prophet declared with pastoral tenderness, "though the Lord hath not revealed to me the precise details of what lies ahead, I am convinced in my spirit that the bloodline He hath preserved through fire and sword shall be planted in distant realms, each serving different aspects of His eternal plan. May the eastern branch find its destined place among the islands of the sea, establishing a throne that shall endure through the ages, while the western branch spreads throughout the lands of the setting sun, creating peoples united under divine covenant."

Jeremiah's words carried additional weight as he continued with his heartfelt encouragement that spoke of hope for future reunification and ultimate triumph over the forces of darkness that opposed divine purposes in the earth.

"Though separated by seas and centuries, these two branches of the sacred tree shall not remain forever divided," Jeremiah continued, his voice carrying the weight of prophetic certainty. "In the fullness of time, when the purposes of the Most High do require unity rather than division, the descendants of both sisters shall be brought together again in a kingdom that shall surpass in glory anything that hath been seen since the days of David and Solomon."

The cryptic nature of this prophecy left many questions unanswered, yet its promise of eventual reunification provided comfort to the sisters as they contemplated the painful necessity of their separation. The divine purposes that required their division would ultimately lead to even greater unity, though the fulfilment of such promises lay in the distant future.

The formal transfer of the sword of Goliath from Jeremiah to Gathelus marked the official recognition of the Greek prince's role as the primary defender of the sacred bloodline's western branch. The ancient weapon, which had served as a symbol of divine authority throughout our journey, would now represent the establishment of righteous rule in the Celtic territories.

"This blade," Jeremiah explained as he placed the sword in Gathelus’ hands, "hath been preserved through the ages as a sign of divine favour and supernatural authority. It hath tasted the blood of giants and hath been blessed by the prayers of prophets. May it serve thee well in the battles that lie ahead, and may its presence remind thee that the power of the Most High standeth behind those who serve His purposes."

The blessing that accompanied this transfer spoke of the great destiny that awaited Gathelus and his descendants in the western lands. "Thou shalt be a father of kings," the prophet declared, "and thy seed shall rule over many peoples. The Red Hand that marketh thy heritage shall become a banner of hope for the oppressed and a sign of divine favour for those who seek righteousness."

Brigantia's promise to Tea Tephi regarding the care of her sister provided additional comfort during this difficult parting. The Celtic maiden, whose friendship with both princesses had deepened throughout our sojourn in Iberia, spoke with the sincerity of one who understood the sacred nature of the bonds she was accepting.

"I swear by the Most High and by the honour of my ancestors," she declared as she knelt before Tea Tephi, "that I shall cherish thy sister as mine own blood, protect her from all enemies, and ensure that her children grow up knowing the sacred heritage they carry. Though thou dost sail to distant shores, Princess Scota shall never lack for the love and protection that a sister should provide."

The final preparations for departure were completed with the methodical efficiency that had marked every stage of our journey, yet the emotional weight of permanent separation cast a shadow over activities that should have been routine. The Sea Serpent was loaded with supplies for the unknown challenges that lay ahead, while the Celtic warriors who would accompany us made their final preparations for a voyage that would take them far from their homeland.

It was during these final preparations that I felt compelled to address a matter of crucial importance for future generations—the preservation of the remarkable events that would unfold in our absence. As the chronicler of our divine mission, I had recorded every significant detail of our journey thus far, but the separation of our company meant that the continuing saga of Gathelus and Princess Scota would unfold beyond my ability to witness and document.

Among the Celtic warriors who would remain with the royal couple was Taliesin Ciallmhar the Wise-Tongued, a renowned bard whose reputation for eloquent verse and faithful memory had spread throughout the Celtic territories. His skill in preserving the deeds of heroes through song and story was matched only by his literacy in both Celtic ogham script and Mediterranean letters, making him uniquely qualified for the sacred duty I wished to entrust to him.

"Taliesin," spake I as I approached the young poet with scrolls and writing materials that would serve his future work, "I would ask of thee a service that may prove as important as any sword-stroke in the battles to come. The deeds of Gathelus and Princess Scota, the establishment of their kingdom, and the fulfilment of the prophecies that have guided our mission—all these must be preserved for future generations who shall need to understand the divine purposes that shaped their world."

The Celtic bard received my request with the gravity appropriate to such a sacred responsibility. "I am honoured by thy trust, Baruch ben Neriah," he replied with the formal dignity that marked his people's approach to matters of historical preservation. "The preservation of heroic deeds and the recording of divine intervention have always been among the most sacred duties of my calling. I pledge that the saga of the Red Hand King and his queen shall be preserved in forms that shall endure through the generations."

I spent considerable time instructing Taliesin in the methods I had developed for maintaining accurate historical records while capturing the spiritual significance of the events we had witnessed. The challenge was to preserve not merely the external facts of what occurred but also the divine purposes that gave meaning to human actions and the supernatural elements that transcended ordinary historical narrative.

"Remember," I urged him as we reviewed the techniques for faithful chronicling, "thou shalt be recording not merely the rise of a new kingdom but the unfolding of ancient prophecies and the establishment of divine authority in lands that have known only darkness. The battles fought shall be more than military campaigns—they shall be spiritual warfare with eternal consequences. The children born shall carry bloodlines that stretch back to the throne of David and forward to the ultimate triumph of righteousness in the earth."

Taliesin's understanding of the sacred nature of his task was evident in his response. "I shall record not only what men see with their eyes but what the Most High doth accomplish through His chosen servants," he promised with the solemnity of one accepting a divine commission. "The songs I compose and the chronicles I write shall preserve both the human courage and the divine intervention that shape the destiny of our people."

As I prepared the materials that would enable him to maintain written records alongside the oral traditions of his Celtic heritage, I found myself moved to offer a prayer that would bind our separate missions together despite the physical distance that would soon divide us.

"O Lord God of Israel," I prayed with the authority of one who had witnessed countless manifestations of divine power, "as Thou hast preserved this sacred bloodline through fire and sword, through the collapse of earthly kingdoms and the perils of the sea, grant now that the deeds of Thy servants in these western lands may be faithfully recorded and preserved. Let the chronicles of their struggles and triumphs somehow find their way to my own quill and parchment, that the complete story of Thy purposes may be known to future generations."

The prayer carried more than personal desire for historical completeness—it reflected my growing understanding that the chronicles we were creating would serve purposes far beyond simple historical record. Future generations would need to understand the divine patterns that shaped the establishment of their kingdoms, the spiritual warfare that preceded earthly triumph, and the prophetic fulfilment that validated their authority as legitimate heirs to ancient promises.

"May the Most High grant success to Gathelus and Princess Scota in all their endeavours," I continued, my voice carrying the weight of prophetic blessing. "May their kingdom flourish according to divine purposes, may their children inherit both earthly authority and spiritual blessing, and may the records of their deeds inspire future generations to trust in the providence that guideth the affairs of nations."

Taliesin's acceptance of this sacred duty provided comfort as we contemplated the approaching separation. Though our company would be divided, the continuation of our chronicle would ensure that no significant element of the divine plan would be lost to future generations. The stories that would unfold in the Celtic territories would somehow find their way to my own records, creating a complete account of the remarkable purposes that had brought us safely through so many trials to this moment of purposeful division.

The last evening before our departure was marked by a feast that celebrated both the achievements of our time in Iberia and the hope for future success in the western islands. The food was abundant and the companionship warm, yet the knowledge that this gathering would never be repeated gave a bittersweet quality to the festivities.

As the Sea Serpent prepared to sail with the morning tide, the entire company gathered on the dock for final farewells that would echo through the generations. The two groups—those who would remain to establish the western branch of the sacred bloodline and those who would continue to the ultimate destination of our mission—faced each other with the mixture of sorrow and determination that marked all great moments in history.

"May the Lord watch between me and thee, when we are absent one from another," Jeremiah pronounced as he offered the final blessing over our divided company. "May His purposes be fulfilled in both our paths, and may the day come when all His promises are brought to completion in the earth."

The Sea Serpent's departure from Iberian waters marked the end of one chapter in our extraordinary journey and the beginning of another. Behind us lay the shores where half our company would remain to face the giant clans and establish a realm worthy of divine blessing, while ahead lay the mysterious islands that called so urgently through Tea Tephi's dreams.

The Celtic warriors who accompanied us stood at the rail, watching their homeland fade into the distance as they committed themselves to a destiny that would unfold in unknown lands among unfamiliar peoples. Their courage in leaving everything they had known for the sake of sacred purposes provided a reminder of the sort of faith that had sustained our mission through every trial and triumph.

As I recorded these events for future generations, I reflected upon the remarkable providence that had brought us safely through such extraordinary circumstances to this moment of purposeful division. The sacred bloodline that had been preserved through the fall of Jerusalem and the perils of the sea would now take root in multiple realms, spreading divine blessing throughout the western world according to the eternal purposes of the Most High.

The adventure was far from over, but the foundation had been laid for wonders that would echo through the ages. The Red Hand banner would fly over Celtic territories while the Branch of David flourished in the islands of the sea, and both would serve the greater glory of the God who had called us from the ruins of earthly kingdoms to establish His eternal reign among the nations.

The parting of the sisters marked not an ending but a multiplication of divine purposes, and the tears that marked their separation were seeds that would grow into mighty trees whose branches would shelter many peoples in the ages to come. The greatest adventures still lay ahead, but the sacred heritage would endure and flourish according to the promises of Him who had brought us safely through trials that would have destroyed any enterprise founded upon merely human wisdom and strength.

The mists of the western sea beckoned us onward toward destinies that existed more in prophecy than in the maps of mortal geographers, and we sailed toward them with the confidence that comes from knowing that the Lord of Hosts had prepared our path and would bring His purposes to perfect completion in His appointed time.
 
Chapter 13: The Landing at Howth

 As compiled by Baruch ben Neriah

 In which the sacred company reaches the Emerald Isle, ancient prophecies converge with Irish tradition, and dark forces gather to oppose the divine planting

Weeks had turned into months upon the vast, churning expanse of the Atlantic, that great western sea which separates the known world from the lands of mystery and prophecy. The Sea Serpent had proven herself worthy of her name, carrying us through tempests that would have claimed lesser vessels and calms that tested the patience of even the most seasoned mariners. Our Celtic companions, those thirteen champions led by Caratacus the Bold, had adapted to life aboard ship with the resilience characteristic of their people, though I could see in their eyes the longing for solid ground beneath their feet.

It was on the morning of what would prove to be our final day at sea that the divine providence which had guided our entire journey revealed its ultimate destination. The mists that had shrouded the horizon for days began to lift like a veil drawn back by an unseen hand, and there it appeared—a land of emerald green rising from the grey-blue sea like a jewel fashioned by the Creator Himself. Its cliffs, rugged and ancient beyond reckoning, were crowned with verdant forests that seemed to pulse with an otherworldly vitality, and the very air that reached us across the diminishing distance was rich with the scent of damp earth and blooming heather.

"Behold," Jeremiah declared with the authority of prophetic fulfilment, his weathered hands gripping the ship's rail as he gazed upon the approaching shores, "the land that the Most High hath prepared for the planting of His purposes. Here shall the Branch of David take root in soil sanctified by divine intention, and here shall the promises made to our fathers find their western fulfilment."

Princess Tea Tephi stood beside the prophet, her prophetic gifts allowing her to perceive dimensions of significance invisible to ordinary sight. "I see it now," she said softly, her voice carrying the wonder of one whose dreams were taking tangible form before her eyes. "The green hills do match exactly what I have witnessed in my visions—ancient stones arranged in sacred patterns, rivers that flow with the memory of forgotten wisdom, and a people whose hearts have been prepared by generations of waiting for something they could not name."

The final approach to landfall required all of Llyr's considerable seamanship, as the coastline revealed itself to be as treacherous as it was beautiful. Hidden rocks and shifting currents threatened ships that approached without proper knowledge, yet the Celtic navigator's understanding of western waters served us well. He guided the Sea Serpent through passages that seemed impossibly narrow, following the flight patterns of seabirds and the subtle changes in water color that spoke of safe channels.

We made landfall at Howth, a sheltered cove whose natural harbor provided protection from the fierce Atlantic swells. The year, by our careful reckoning maintained throughout the journey, was the third year following the fall of Jerusalem and the destruction of Solomon's Temple. The air that greeted us as we dropped anchor was fresh and clean, filled with the cries of gulls and the distant bleating of sheep that grazed on clifftop meadows. It was a moment of profound relief—the culmination of our long and perilous journey—yet also a beginning fraught with uncertainties that only divine wisdom could resolve.

As our ship's boats carried the first members of our company to shore, a small band of native Irish warriors emerged from the treeline with the silent efficiency of men accustomed to watching their coastlands for unexpected arrivals. They were clad in rough hides skilfully worked and decorated with patterns that spoke of cultural sophistication, armed with spears and axes whose craftsmanship revealed generations of metalworking tradition. Their faces bore the distinctive blue-painted designs that would become legendary among future chroniclers, and their expressions combined the wariness of professional sentries with the curiosity of a people who understood that significant events often arrived without warning.

The leader of this coastal patrol was a man of imposing stature whose eyes held the grey-green depths of the sea itself. His bearing spoke of noble blood and military authority, while the deference shown him by his companions marked him as a figure of considerable importance in the local hierarchy. The bronze torcs around his neck and the quality of his weapons suggested connections to the highest levels of Irish society.

"Hold there, strangers," he called out in the ancient Celtic tongue, his voice carrying across the water with the authority of one accustomed to being obeyed. "State thy purpose and thy lineage, for these are the protected shores of the Milesian people, and the High King's law doth extend to every stone and strand."

Jeremiah rose to his full height in the boat's stern, the morning light catching the silver of his hair and beard in a way that seemed to create a nimbus of divine authority around his aged features. In his hands he held the Harp of David, its ancient strings gleaming with the patina of centuries and the care of countless sacred ceremonies. When he spoke, his words carried the weight of prophetic commission and the dignity of one who had stood in the presence of the Almighty.

"I am Jeremiah, servant of the Most High God and prophet to the nations," he declared, his voice penetrating the morning air with clarity that seemed enhanced by supernatural power. "With me traveleth Tea Tephi, daughter of Zedekiah, last king of Judah, heir to the throne of David and bearer of promises that reach back to the foundations of the world. We come not as conquerors or raiders, but as servants of divine purpose, carrying sacred trust that seeketh its appointed place among thy people."

The Irish chieftain's expression shifted from suspicion to amazement as his gaze fell upon Princess Tea Tephi, whose royal bearing remained evident despite her travel-worn garments and the hardships of the sea voyage. There existed among the noble families of all peoples a recognition that transcended language and custom—the unmistakable mark of true royalty that could not be counterfeited or assumed. Here was breeding that spoke of ancient lineages and divine appointment, authority that had been tested by trial and proven by endurance.

"Thou speakest of high things, prophet," the chieftain replied, his tone now showing the respect due to persons of evident distinction, "yet the waters beyond these shores have been troubled by reports that strain belief. Tell me, hast thou encountered in thy travels any sign of the pirate scourge that hath plagued our shipping lanes? We have received word—unconfirmed and perhaps incredible—that a great battle was fought upon the western sea, and that forces beyond mortal ken- mention of sea-gods rising from the depths, a consuming fire sent from the heavens and a serpent of the sea were involved in the destruction of those who served the demon-god Moloch."

At the mention of that accursed name, several of our Celtic companions made protective gestures while Korax stepped forward with the grim satisfaction of a warrior who possessed tangible proof of remarkable claims. From the carefully maintained collection of trophies taken during our supernatural encounter, he produced items that left no doubt regarding the truth of the reports that had reached Irish shores.

"Behold the banner of Moloch's fleet," Korax announced, displaying the darkly magnificent standard that had flown from the Rephaim flagship. The fabric, woven with threads that seemed to absorb light rather than reflect it, bore symbols that caused the Irish warriors to step back instinctively. "Here too is armour stripped from their captain, and weapons forged in furnaces that burn with more than earthly fire. We did indeed encounter the servants of the demon-god, and only by the direct intervention of the Almighty did we escape their supernatural malice."

The testimony that followed, delivered with the compelling authority of eyewitness experience, transformed scepticism into wonder and suspicion into reverence. Jeremiah recounted the highlights of our extraordinary journey—the escape from burning Jerusalem with the sacred Stone of Destiny, the refuge provided by Pharaoh's court in Egypt, the divine dreams that had guided our path, the alliance forged with the Greek prince Gathelus, and the supernatural battle that had seen divine fire consume an entire fleet of demon-worshipping pirates.

"The sea-god thou hast heard described," the prophet explained, "was no heathen deity rising from the depths, but the visible manifestation of the Lord God of Israel asserting His dominion over waters He created. The serpent of the sea that destroyed our enemies was in fact our ship- the Sea Serpent and the fire that fell from heaven came from the throne of the Most High Himself."

Princess Tea Tephi added her own testimony, describing the prophetic visions that had guided their journey and the divine mandate that had brought them specifically to the shores of Éire. Her words carried the ring of authentic revelation, speaking of ancient promises that required fulfilment in these western islands and of purposes that connected the throne of David with the sacred stones of Irish tradition.

As the morning progressed and the full extent of our company's remarkable history became clear, the attitude of the Irish patrol underwent a transformation that bordered on the miraculous. What had begun as professional suspicion evolved into respectful curiosity, then growing amazement, and finally into something approaching reverence for visitors whose credentials included divine protection and supernatural victories.

"By the sacred groves," the chieftain declared, "if what thou speakest is truth, then thy arrival doth fulfil prophecies that our druids have preserved for generations. Come then, heroes of the western sea, let me escort thee to the presence of Eochaid Herremon, High King of all Éire, who ruleth from the sacred Hill of Tara."

The journey inland began with the exchange of gifts that established friendship between our companies and demonstrated the respect due to visitors of evident importance. The Irish warriors offered provisions for our journey and guides familiar with the inland paths, while we reciprocated with treasures from our Mediterranean travels—purple-dyed cloth from Phoenician markets, worked silver from Egyptian craftsmen, and scrolls containing wisdom literature that would enrich Irish learning for generations to come.

As we left behind the salt spray of the sea for the verdant heart of Éire, the land itself seemed to welcome our presence with displays of natural beauty that exceeded anything we had witnessed in our extensive travels. The countryside unfolded before us like a tapestry woven by divine hands—emerald fields dotted with ancient oak groves, winding rivers that reflected the ever-changing sky, and stone circles whose silent presence spoke of generations long past.

Jeremiah observed everything with the keen interest of one whose prophetic gifts allowed him to perceive the spiritual significance of geographical features. Yet his expression grew increasingly troubled as he noted the deep reverence the natives showed toward certain sites, the careful way they approached particular groves and springs, and the protective gestures they made when passing stone formations that clearly held religious significance.

"This land beareth the beauty of the Lord's creation," he confided to me as we travelled through a valley where standing stones marked what was clearly a place of ancient worship, "yet it hath been corrupted by the very abominations that brought judgment upon Israel. These stone circles, these sacred groves, these high places—they are the same iniquities that moved the Most High to scatter His people among the nations. If we are to establish righteous rule here, these stumbling blocks must be removed, and the people must learn to worship the Creator rather than the creation itself."

Our Celtic companions, led by the redoubtable Caratacus, adapted quickly to the Irish landscape and customs, finding in the local warriors’ kindred spirits who shared their appreciation for martial skill and their reverence for the sacred aspects of kingship. The bonds formed during these first days in Éire would prove crucial for the challenges that lay ahead, as our mission would require not merely the acceptance of the Irish royalty but the active cooperation of their warrior class.

Yet even as we journeyed toward what promised to be a momentous meeting with the High King, darker forces were already in motion to oppose the divine purposes that had brought us safely across the western sea. In hidden glades and secret strongholds throughout the island, servants of powers that predated human settlement were gathering to discuss the threat posed by our arrival.

In a moonless grove where ancient oaks twisted their branches into shapes that seemed designed to block out heaven's light, a council of beings met whose allegiance belonged to forces that had ruled the western islands before the coming of any human inhabitants. At the centre of this unholy gathering sat Morrigan, the Shadow Weaver, whose wisdom had been corrupted by centuries of trafficking with entities that delighted in chaos and destruction.

Once she had been among the most revered druids of her generation, possessing knowledge of the natural world and its spiritual dimensions that made her counsels sought by kings and common folk alike. But ambition had poisoned her gifts, and allegiance to the darker deities of the land had twisted her understanding until she saw the divine power of the Lord God of Israel not as salvation but as a direct challenge to her dominion over Ireland's mystical forces.

"The strangers have arrived," she announced to the assembled shadows, her voice carrying harmonics that seemed to originate from multiple sources simultaneously. "The one they call prophet doth bear power that threateneth to overturn the ancient arrangements we have so carefully maintained. With him cometh royal blood that would establish new patterns of authority, patterns that recognize no lordship save that of the foreign god they serve."

Among those who answered her summons was King Aodhán the Usurper, a figure whose ambition and ruthlessness had already marked him as the most dangerous political threat to High King Herremon's authority. Aodhán ruled a neighbouring realm through combination of military strength and alliance with forces that preferred to remain hidden from common knowledge. His desire for Herremon's throne was matched only by his hatred of anything that might strengthen his rival's position.

"Tell me plainly, Shadow Weaver," Aodhán demanded with the impatience of one accustomed to direct action, "what threat do these foreign wanderers pose to my plans? Herremon groweth stronger each season, and I cannot afford delays while thou dost spin prophecies and speak in riddles."

Morrigan's response carried the weight of supernatural perception enhanced by decades of trafficking with entities whose knowledge spanned centuries. "The threat is beyond anything thou dost comprehend, king of small vision. The woman they call princess doth bear bloodline that reacheth back to the throne of an accursed lineage blessed by the god who split the sea and brought down the walls of Jericho. Should she marry into Irish royalty and establish her line upon our sacred stones, the power of her divine heritage shall create a kingdom that shall endure for ages beyond counting."

The implications of this revelation struck the assembled council like a physical blow. The ancient powers they served had ruled the western islands through careful manipulation of local conflicts and gradual corruption of indigenous spiritual traditions. The establishment of a divinely sanctioned kingdom would threaten not merely their political influence but their very existence in the earthly realm.

"Then they must be destroyed," Aodhán declared with the straightforward brutality that had served him well in previous conflicts. "Rally the tribes that owe me allegiance, corrupt whatever alliances they attempt to form, and if necessary, meet them in open battle before they can establish themselves."

But Morrigan's wisdom, however twisted, recognized the complexity of the opposition they faced. "Direct confrontation would be foolish, king of hasty impulses. These strangers have already survived supernatural assault upon the western sea and do carry divine protection that would make ordinary weapons ineffective. We must be more subtle in our approach—sowing discord from within, corrupting the hearts of those who would support them, and turning their own righteousness into weakness."

It was then that Maeve the Serpent-Tongued emerged from the shadows where she had been listening to the council's deliberations. Her beauty was the sort that turned men's heads and clouded their judgment, yet those who possessed supernatural sight could perceive the coils of corruption that writhed beneath her fair exterior. She was a master of the arts that used attraction as weapon and seduction as strategy, capable of introducing poison into the highest levels of society while maintaining the appearance of innocence.

"Leave Herremon to me," she said with the confidence of one whose previous victories had involved the destruction of noble houses through the simple expedient of corrupting their leaders. "A king is only as strong as his judgment, and judgment doth fail when desire cloudeth reason. I shall position myself in his court, win his confidence, and then use his own passions to turn him against these foreigners who would challenge his authority."

The plan that emerged from this unholy council combined political ambition with supernatural malice in ways designed to exploit every weakness in the Irish social structure. Aodhán would continue building his military alliances while publicly maintaining the pretence of loyalty to the High King. Morrigan would work through her network of corrupted druids to spread fear and suspicion regarding the foreign influences that threatened traditional ways. Maeve would infiltrate the highest levels of Irish society, using her arts to create division between Herremon and his counsellors.

"Remember," Morrigan warned as the council prepared to disperse, "we face enemies whose god hath already demonstrated power beyond our previous experience. The fire that destroyed Moloch's fleet came from heaven itself, and the protection that doth surround these strangers hath already proven effective against supernatural assault. We must be patient, cunning, and absolutely ruthless in our methods."

As the dark council scattered to begin their various schemes, the servants of divine purpose continued their journey toward Tara, unaware of the forces marshalling against them yet protected by the same providence that had guided them safely across sea and land. The stage was being set for a confrontation that would determine not merely the fate of individual lives but the spiritual direction of an entire people.

The sacred Stone of Destiny, wrapped in its covering and carried with reverent care, pulsed with power that those sensitive to such things could feel even at a distance. Here was the foundation stone of kingdoms, the pillow upon which Jacob had received his divine vision, the physical symbol of promises that connected heaven with earth. Its presence in Éire would either establish a kingdom blessed by the Most High or provoke opposition that would test every aspect of the divine plan.

The prophecies spoken over the parting sisters in distant Iberia were approaching their fulfilment, as the eastern branch of the sacred bloodline drew near to its appointed place of planting. Yet the forces arrayed against this divine purpose were already mobilizing their opposition, setting the stage for struggles that would echo through generations and determine the spiritual heritage of countless souls yet unborn.

The landing at Howth had been accomplished, the initial contact with Irish authority had proven favourable, and the journey toward ultimate destiny had begun. But the true test of faith and divine purpose still lay ahead, in the halls of kings and the secret places where ancient powers plotted against the establishment of righteousness in the earth.

The Emerald Isle had received the sacred company with the beauty and promise characteristic of lands chosen for divine purpose, yet beneath its verdant surface, forces of opposition were stirring that would require all the wisdom, courage, and supernatural protection that the Most High could provide. The greatest challenges of the entire journey were yet to come, but the foundation had been laid for victories that would establish the throne of David in the western islands according to the eternal purposes of Him who calls the generations from the beginning.

As we approached the sacred Hill of Tara, where the High King held court and the Lia Fáil awaited its destined queen, I could not help but reflect upon the remarkable providence that had brought us safely through trials that should have destroyed any enterprise founded upon merely human wisdom. The God who had preserved the sacred bloodline through the fall of Jerusalem, the perils of the sea, and the opposition of supernatural enemies would surely complete His purposes in the green hills of Éire.

The landing at Howth was accomplished, but the true adventure was only beginning.
 
Chapter 14: The High King's Court

 As compiled by Baruch ben Neriah

 In which the sacred company journeys to Tara, ancient prophecies find fulfilment in the seat of Irish kings, and the eternal purposes of the Most High converge with the royal bloodlines of the western isle

The commander who had greeted us at Howth with such unexpected honour proved to be Fergus mac Roich, Knight-Commander of the Craobh Ruadh—the legendary Red Branch Knights who served as the elite guard of High King Eochaid Herremon at the sacred Hill of Tara. His bearing spoke of generations of noble blood, and the bronze torcs adorning his neck marked him as a chieftain of considerable standing within the complex hierarchy of Irish society. Yet it was his eyes that revealed the depth of character beneath the warrior's exterior—grey-green like the sea itself, holding wisdom that came from years of service to a king whose vision extended far beyond the immediate concerns of tribal politics.

As our enlarged company—now including both our Mediterranean companions and the Irish escort provided by Fergus—made its way through the emerald heart of Éire toward the ancient seat of high kingship, the landscape itself seemed to bear witness to the momentous nature of our mission. Rolling hills crowned with oak groves sacred to druidic tradition alternated with broad meadows where cattle grazed beneath skies that shifted from pearl-grey to brilliant blue with the swift changes characteristic of island weather. Stone circles, some so ancient that their builders had passed beyond memory, dotted the countryside like markers placed by divine hands to guide travellers toward their appointed destiny.
Fergus proved to be a man of considerable learning as well as martial prowess, conversant not only with the military arts that had earned him his position among the Red Branch, but also with the ancient lore that connected Irish tradition to the broader currents of world history. As we travelled, he spoke with evident pride of his king's achievements in unifying the scattered tribes of Éire, describing Eochaid Herremon as a ruler whose vision encompassed not merely the conquest of territory but the creation of a kingdom that could stand among the great powers of the world.

"Our High King," Fergus explained as we crested a hill that offered views stretching to the distant mountains, "is a man who doth understand that true strength cometh not from the sword alone, but from the establishment of laws that create justice, alliances that ensure peace, and traditions that bind people together across generations. He hath heard reports of thy journey that intrigue him greatly—tales of divine intervention upon the western sea, of ancient wisdom preserved through the fall of great cities, and of royal blood that doth carry promises reaching back to the foundations of the world."

The mention of these reports reminded me of the supernatural swiftness with which news had travelled across the waters to reach Irish shores even before our arrival. It seemed that the same divine providence that had guided our journey had also prepared the way for our reception, ensuring that the sacred company would find not suspicion and hostility, but curiosity and welcome from those who held power in this western kingdom.

Princess Tea Tephi, riding beside Jeremiah with the dignity befitting her royal status, engaged Fergus in conversations that revealed the sophistication of Irish court culture and the complexity of relationships between the various kingdoms that acknowledged Eochaid Herremon's supremacy. Her questions demonstrated the political acumen inherited through generations of Davidic royalty, while her observations about the spiritual dimensions of kingship found ready understanding in a people whose traditions recognized the sacred nature of royal authority.
