The gathering storm would soon break upon the sacred Hill of Tara, yet when it did, it would find not helpless victims but prepared defenders who had already demonstrated their ability to achieve the impossible through courage, cunning, and unwavering commitment to the divine purposes that justified their struggle against the accumulated malevolence of ages past and present.
 
Chapter 18: The War Council and the Sword of Methuselah

 As compiled by Baruch ben Neriah

 In which the High King convenes his military council to face the gathering darkness, a fierce young warrior seeks the means to combat supernatural evil, and ancient prophecy reveals the location of a holy weapon that may turn the tide of supernatural war

The dawn that broke over the sacred Hill of Tara three days after the knighting ceremony carried with it an ominous quality that even the most battle-hardened veterans of the Craobh Ruadh could sense. The very air seemed charged with the sort of spiritual tension that preceded great conflicts, while the morning mist clung to the hillsides with unnatural persistence that spoke of forces beyond natural weather patterns. It was on this morning that High King Eochaidh Herremon commanded the convening of his full military council, recognizing that the intelligence gathered by the Knights of the Scarlet Thread required immediate strategic response.

The great hall of Tara had been transformed from its ceremonial configuration to accommodate the detailed maps, tactical assessments, and strategic planning materials that would be required for comprehensive military deliberation. Tables had been arranged to provide clear sight lines to the massive wall maps that depicted not merely the geography of Éire but also the suspected positions of enemy forces, supply routes, and defensive installations that had been identified through the reconnaissance efforts of Caswallon the Swift and his companions.

Fergus mac Roich, Knight-Commander of the Craobh Ruadh, arrived early to oversee the final preparations for what would undoubtedly prove to be one of the most crucial strategic sessions in Irish military history. His bearing reflected the gravity of circumstances that had brought them to this point, while his experienced eye evaluated every detail of the arrangements to ensure that nothing would impede the flow of information or the clarity of tactical discussion that the situation demanded.

Accompanying Fergus was his most trusted lieutenant, a warrior whose reputation had grown with remarkable speed through the ranks of the elite guard despite his relative youth. Cian mac Morna stood barely twenty-five years of age, yet his mastery of both martial arts and military strategy had earned him recognition as one of the most promising young commanders in the kingdom. Tall and lean with the sort of deceptive build that concealed tremendous physical power beneath an apparently lanky frame, he possessed the broad shoulders and defined musculature that marked a warrior who had dedicated his entire existence to the perfection of combat skills.

His jet-black hair, kept precisely trimmed according to military regulation, framed features that would have been handsome in a conventional sense were it not for the intensity that burned in his piercing blue eyes. Those eyes held depths of controlled fury that spoke of personal losses that had forged him into a weapon of terrible efficiency, while his bearing conveyed the sort of focused determination that made experienced warriors unconsciously step aside when he passed. Every movement spoke of training that had been refined through years of relentless practice, creating reflexes that functioned with mechanical precision yet retained the fluidity that marked a true master of the martial arts.

The story of how Cian mac Morna had come to his position within the Craobh Ruadh was one that stirred the hearts of all who heard it, for it spoke of tragedy transformed into purpose through the sort of dedicated determination that created legends. Born into a minor noble family whose lands lay along the contested borders where Aodhán the Usurper's raids had been most frequent during the wars of succession, he had witnessed horrors at an age when most children concerned themselves with games and innocent pursuits.

The attack that had destroyed his family had come during one of the darkest periods of the conflict, when Aodhán's forces had been experimenting with supernatural allies whose nature defied conventional understanding. Cian, then barely eight years old, had been hidden by his parents in a concealed chamber beneath their manor house as inhuman screams echoed through the corridors above. Through gaps in the stonework, he had watched as creatures that appeared solid yet moved with the flowing motion of shadows had systematically hunted down every member of his household.

The memory that haunted him most deeply, burned into his consciousness with the sort of traumatic clarity that shaped entire lifetimes, was the sight of his five-year-old sister Niamh being dragged away by something that wore the appearance of a beautiful woman yet possessed eyes that held no trace of human warmth. The creature had moved through solid stone as though it were mist, her form somehow managing to be both physically present and incorporeal simultaneously, creating a visual impossibility that had shattered the eight-year-old boy's understanding of natural law.

The creature had paused directly above his hiding place, its terrible gaze seeming to penetrate the barriers that concealed him, yet for reasons he would never understand it had moved on without revealing his presence. Little Niamh's terrified cries had echoed through the corridors until they faded into the distance, leaving behind a silence that was somehow worse than the sounds of battle that had preceded it. From that moment, Cian mac Morna had dedicated his existence to acquiring the skills necessary to face such enemies and exact vengeance for the family that had been stolen from him.

The years that followed had been marked by training that bordered on obsession, as the young orphan threw himself into the study of warfare with an intensity that amazed even veteran instructors. Taken into the household of a distant kinsman who served among the Craobh Ruadh, he had access to the finest military education available in the kingdom, yet his true learning came from the relentless practice sessions that occupied every moment not devoted to formal instruction.

By the age of fifteen, he had mastered every weapon in the Irish arsenal, from the traditional sword and spear to the more exotic implements that skilled warriors employed for specialized combat situations. His understanding of unarmed combat exceeded that of men twice his age, while his knowledge of military tactics and strategic planning had earned the respect of commanders who initially viewed him as merely another ambitious young nobleman seeking glory through military service.

Yet for all his skill in conventional warfare, Cian carried within him a frustration that gnawed at his soul like a festering wound. Every technique he mastered, every weapon he perfected, every tactical innovation he developed—all were designed for combat against mortal enemies. The shapeshifters and supernatural creatures that served Aodhán the Usurper remained beyond his reach, protected by their otherworldly nature from the sort of physical violence that could destroy conventional opponents.

This limitation had driven him to seek knowledge from every available source, studying ancient texts that described battles between mortals and otherworldly forces, consulting with scholars about the nature of ethereal beings, and even approaching foreign wise men who possessed knowledge of different supernatural traditions. Yet despite all his research, the fundamental problem remained: his weapons, no matter how skilfully wielded, could not touch enemies that existed partially outside the physical realm.

As Cian took his position beside Fergus at the great table where the military council would convene, his expression reflected the internal conflict that had defined his adult life. Here was a warrior who had achieved mastery over every aspect of conventional combat, yet remained powerless to confront the enemies that had destroyed everything he held dear. The fierce hatred that burned in his eyes when Aodhán's name was mentioned was matched only by the desperate frustration of one who possessed the skill to exact vengeance yet lacked the means to reach his targets.

The arrival of the other council members transformed the great hall into a gathering that represented the full spectrum of Irish military and political leadership. The various dukes whose territories comprised the kingdom came with their own military advisors and intelligence reports, creating a assembly of experience and expertise that could address the multi-faceted challenges of the coming conflict. Each brought not merely their own forces and local knowledge, but also the concerns and perspectives of the populations they represented.

Duke Conall of Connacht, whose western territories bordered the sea lanes through which enemy reinforcements might arrive, carried maps showing the coastal fortifications that his engineers had been constructing under the guidance of Llyr of the Western Winds. His weather-beaten features spoke of a lifetime spent defending Ireland's Atlantic approaches, while his practical demeanour reflected the sort of no-nonsense approach to military problems that had made him invaluable during previous conflicts.

Duke Niall of Ulster, whose northern domains included the mountain territories where the giant clans maintained their traditional strongholds, brought intelligence reports compiled through cooperation with the tribal allies that Finn the Fair and his diplomatic team had recruited to the royal cause. His knowledge of the difficult terrain and extreme weather conditions that characterized his region would prove crucial in planning operations that might extend into those hostile environments.

Duke Fergal of Munster, whose southern territories encompassed the richest agricultural lands in Ireland, represented the logistical backbone upon which any extended military campaign would depend. His detailed understanding of supply chains, transportation networks, and resource allocation would determine whether Irish forces could maintain effective operations against enemies whose supernatural nature freed them from many conventional military limitations.

Duke Lorcan of Leinster, whose eastern territories included the landing sites where our sacred company had first set foot on Irish soil, brought perspectives shaped by recent experience of the supernatural phenomena that had marked our arrival. His forces had been among the first to witness divine intervention in human affairs, creating a foundation of faith that would prove essential in maintaining morale during the trials ahead.

The arrival of Caratacus the Bold and Finn the Fair, representing the Knights of the Scarlet Thread, brought to the council the tactical insights that had been gained through their successful guerrilla operations against enemy preparations. Their detailed knowledge of enemy capabilities, weaknesses, and operational patterns would provide the foundation upon which broader strategic planning could be constructed.

Jeremiah's entrance into the council chamber carried with it the sort of spiritual authority that transformed political gatherings into occasions of divine significance. His presence reminded all assembled that the coming conflict transcended mere territorial disputes or dynastic rivalries, representing instead a struggle between forces of light and darkness that would determine the spiritual destiny of the Irish people. Princess Tea Tephi accompanied him, her royal bearing and obvious wisdom earning her recognition as a full participant in deliberations that would shape the future of her adopted kingdom.

High King Eochaidh Herremon called the assembly to order with the sort of measured solemnity that befitted the gravity of their circumstances. "Noble lords and trusted advisors," he began, his voice carrying clearly through the great hall, "we gather this day to confront intelligence that confirms our worst fears regarding the nature and magnitude of the threats arrayed against our kingdom. The reconnaissance conducted by our Knights of the Scarlet Thread has revealed enemy preparations that exceed our most pessimistic projections."

The High King's opening remarks set the tone for presentations that would systematically destroy any remaining hopes that the coming conflict might be resolved through conventional military means. "Sir Fergus," he continued, addressing the Knight-Commander of the Craobh Ruadh, "we would have your assessment of our defensive capabilities in light of what has been discovered regarding enemy strength and composition."

Fergus mac Roich rose with the dignity befitting his rank and experience, yet his expression reflected the sort of grim determination that marked warriors who understood the magnitude of challenges facing them. "Your Majesty," he began, consulting the detailed reports that had been compiled through the intelligence-gathering efforts of the previous weeks, "our conventional forces number approximately twelve thousand trained warriors, supported by militia units that could potentially double that strength under emergency mobilization."

The Knight-Commander's presentation of Irish military capabilities was thorough and professionally delivered, yet it painted a picture that grew increasingly sobering as he progressed through the various categories of available resources. "Our cavalry comprises eight hundred mounted warriors of the highest quality, trained according to traditions that have proven effective against conventional enemies yet untested against supernatural opponents. Our infantry includes both heavy troops equipped for close combat and lighter forces trained for the sort of skirmishing operations that Sir Caratacus and his companions have employed so effectively."

Fergus paused to consult additional documents before continuing his assessment. "Our siege capabilities include engines constructed according to the best Celtic engineering principles, yet their effectiveness against fortifications that may be protected by supernatural means remains questionable. Our supply chains are robust enough to support extended operations within our own territory, yet they would face severe challenges if required to operate in regions where enemy forces could disrupt conventional logistics through otherworldly means."

The Knight-Commander's presentation reached its most sobering elements as he addressed the question of naval support and coastal defence. "Sir Llyr's innovations have provided us with capabilities that exceed our previous limitations, yet the enemy's access to creatures that can operate both above and beneath the waves creates challenges that conventional naval warfare cannot address. Our coastal fortifications can defend against mortal raiders, yet they offer questionable protection against enemies that may approach through supernatural means."

When Fergus concluded his presentation of Irish capabilities, he turned to the far more troubling question of enemy strength and composition. "Sir Caratacus," he said, addressing the Celtic champion whose reconnaissance had provided the intelligence upon which all subsequent planning would be based, "would you present your assessment of the forces arrayed against us?"

Caratacus the Bold stepped forward with the bearing of one who had looked upon enemy preparations and lived to report their findings, yet his expression carried the grim knowledge of a warrior who understood the magnitude of what they faced. "Your Majesty," he began, consulting maps that bore the detailed notations compiled during his dangerous surveillance missions, "the enemy force currently assembling for assault upon Tara numbers at least thirty thousand conventional troops, supported by supernatural allies whose capabilities transcend normal military assessment."

The champion's presentation of enemy capabilities struck the assembled council like a series of physical blows, each revelation worse than the last. "The conventional forces include professional soldiers drawn from multiple kingdoms that have allied with Aodhán the Usurper, barbarian contingents whose loyalty has been purchased through promises of plunder, and mercenary companies whose expertise in siege warfare poses serious threats to our defensive installations."

Caratacus paused to indicate specific positions on the tactical maps before continuing his sobering assessment. "Yet it is the supernatural elements that present the gravest threats to our survival. The undead forces number in the thousands, including creatures whose resilience to conventional weapons makes them nearly immune to normal military assault. The giant clans have committed contingents whose individual strength exceeds that of dozen mortal warriors, while their knowledge of mountain warfare creates tactical advantages that would be difficult to counter under the best circumstances."

The champion's expression grew even more grim as he reached the most troubling aspects of his intelligence report. "The shapeshifters that serve as elite troops and infiltration specialists possess capabilities that make conventional counter-intelligence operations nearly impossible. Their ability to assume any human appearance while retaining supernatural powers creates security threats that traditional methods cannot address."
Yet it was Caratacus's revelation of the enemy's ultimate leadership that struck the council with paralyzing horror. "Most troubling of all," he concluded, his voice carrying the weight of knowledge that no mortal should possess, "our intelligence confirms that the enemy forces operate under the direct command of entities whose power exceeds anything in our experience of natural warfare."

Duke Conall of Connacht was the first to voice the question that had been growing in every mind as the presentations unfolded. "Sir Caratacus," he said, his weather-beaten face reflecting the sort of concern that marked a veteran who had never previously encountered cause for despair, "our sources have reported the possible involvement of the ancient serpent known as Scáthgheal the Crimson Fury in these preparations. Can you confirm whether that monster has indeed allied himself with the usurper's cause?"

The silence that followed this question stretched through long moments as every member of the council contemplated the implications of what was being asked. Scáthgheal the Crimson Fury, the great serpent whose very name had become synonymous with supernatural evil throughout the known world, represented a level of threat that transcended conventional military assessment. His involvement in the conflict would transform their defensive preparations from difficult to impossible.

Caratacus's response confirmed their worst fears with the sort of matter-of-fact delivery that made the revelation even more horrifying. "Our intelligence confirms that Scáthgheal has indeed committed to supporting Aodhán's cause," he reported, his voice carrying no emotion despite the magnitude of what he was revealing. "His involvement explains several aspects of enemy capabilities that had previously puzzled our analysts."

The effect of this confirmation upon the assembled council was immediate and devastating. Duke Fergal of Munster leaped to his feet with an expression of barely controlled panic. "Then all is lost!" he declared, his voice carrying the despair of one who understood the hopelessness of their situation. "We cannot possibly defeat that monster! He will lay waste to everything we have built, reducing our kingdom to ash and memory!"

Duke Niall of Ulster rose to support his colleague's assessment with the sort of grim practicality that had made him effective in defending the northern territories. "The ancient serpent commands powers that make mortal resistance futile," he said, his voice reflecting the fatalism of one who had seen too much warfare to harbor illusions about impossible odds. "Our conventional forces might as well be children throwing stones at a mountain for all the effect they could have against such an enemy."

The debate that erupted among the assembled nobles reflected the psychological impact of learning that their enemies commanded supernatural support that exceeded anything in their experience of warfare. Voices raised in argument as some advocated immediate surrender to preserve what remained of their people, while others called for desperate last stands that would at least preserve Irish honour even if they could not preserve Irish independence.

"Perhaps we should consider negotiating terms," suggested Duke Lorcan of Leinster, his practical nature seeking solutions that might preserve something from the approaching catastrophe. "If the ancient serpent's involvement doth make military resistance impossible, then diplomatic options may offer the only hope for preserving our people from complete destruction."

"Negotiate with servants of darkness?" Duke Conall responded with the indignation of a warrior whose entire life had been devoted to defending Irish freedom. "Better to die as free men than live as slaves to supernatural tyranny! If we must fall, let us fall fighting rather than grovelling before entities that represent everything our ancestors died to oppose!"

The argument might have continued indefinitely, spiralling into the sort of paralyzing despair that had destroyed kingdoms more effectively than any military defeat, had not Jeremiah suddenly risen from his place at the council table. The prophet's movement carried with it such spiritual authority that the assembled nobles fell silent despite the passion of their disagreements, recognizing that they were about to hear words that would either doom them utterly or provide hope beyond their current understanding.

"Do not be afraid of that serpent!" Jeremiah declared, his voice carrying the sort of divine authority that had enabled him to speak truth to kings and nations throughout his prophetic ministry. "Scáthgheal may have committed to this conflict, yet he will not be allowed to intervene directly in the affairs of mortal men! The ancient serpent hath forgotten the constraints that limit his involvement in earthly warfare, and the Almighty will soundly and painfully remind him of the divine prohibition that doth govern such matters!"

The prophet's words struck the assembled council with the force of revelation, reminding them that supernatural entities operated under spiritual laws that constrained their actions even when their power exceeded human comprehension. The relief that flooded through the great hall was almost palpable as the nobles grasped the implications of what they were hearing.

"THE BAN," Jeremiah continued, using the term that encompassed the divine commandments that governed metaphysical beings, "specifically doth prohibit entities of Scáthgheal the Crimson Fury's nature from direct intervention in conflicts between mortal kingdoms. He may provide support and counsel to his allies, yet he cannot personally engage in battlefield operations without violating spiritual laws that would bring down divine judgment upon him."

The prophet's explanation of the supernatural legal framework that governed the coming conflict transformed the strategic situation from hopeless to merely extremely difficult. Yet even as the assembled nobles began to contemplate the implications of this revelation, Jeremiah's expression grew more serious as he addressed threats that remained within the bounds of spiritual law.

"The problem," he continued, his voice now carrying the weight of divine knowledge that encompassed dangers beyond conventional understanding, "is the entity known as the Morrigan. She representeth a supernatural force that is not permitted to exist in this terrestrial plane, yet her presence hath been confirmed among the enemy forces arrayed against us."

The name of the Morrigan struck those familiar with Celtic spiritual traditions with horror that exceeded even their fear of Scáthgheal the Crimson Fury. Where the ancient serpent was constrained by divine law, the Morrigan represented chaos that acknowledged no limitations beyond raw power. Her very existence in the physical realm violated the natural order that separated spiritual and material reality.

"The Morrigan," Jeremiah explained for those unfamiliar with Celtic supernatural lore, "is a battle-goddess whose influence doth transform warfare from organized conflict into senseless slaughter that serveth no purpose beyond the multiplication of death and suffering. Her presence doth corrupt both armies, turning soldiers into berserkers who lose all capacity for mercy, strategy, or even basic survival instinct."

The prophet's description of this entity's capabilities painted a picture of supernatural warfare that transcended conventional military understanding. "She feedeth upon death and conflict, growing stronger as casualties mount on both sides. Her ultimate goal is not victory for any particular faction, but the maximization of bloodshed that serveth her twisted appetite for destruction."

As the implications of this revelation settled upon the assembled council, Jeremiah's gaze fixed upon Cian mac Morna with an intensity that seemed to penetrate the young warrior's very soul. The prophet's expression suggested that he perceived something in the lieutenant that others might miss, recognizing capabilities or destinies that remained hidden even from their possessor.

"Do ye know," Jeremiah asked, addressing the entire council yet keeping his eyes fixed upon Cian, "of the lore surrounding the Great Sword of Methuselah? The legendary weapon that was wielded by the righteous patriarch during the pre-flood period when giants and Nephilim spirits walked the earth alongside mortal men?"

The question stirred recognition among the assembled nobles, for Irish tradition preserved memories of legendary weapons that had been created for warfare against supernatural enemies. Yet their knowledge remained fragmentary, composed of half-remembered stories whose details had been obscured by generations of oral transmission.

"The Great Sword," Jeremiah continued, his voice now carrying the power of prophetic revelation, "was forged according to divine instruction for the specific purpose of combating entities that existed partially beyond the physical realm. Its blade was blessed with properties that enabled it to affect supernatural creatures as readily as mortal flesh, while its spiritual resonance could disrupt the otherworldly energies that protected such enemies from conventional weapons."

The prophet's description of this legendary weapon's capabilities captured the attention of every person in the great hall, yet it was Cian mac Morna who leaned forward with the intensity of one recognizing the answer to prayers he had been offering since childhood. Here was a weapon that might enable him to confront the shapeshifters and otherworldly creatures that had destroyed his family.

"According to the ancient chronicles," Jeremiah continued, "the Great Sword of Methuselah was responsible for the destruction of nine hundred and eighty thousand Nephilim spirits during the period when such entities walked openly among men. Its power was so feared by supernatural enemies that they united their efforts to ensure its disappearance from the world of men."

The numbers mentioned by the prophet struck the assembled warriors with awe that bordered on disbelief. A weapon capable of accounting for nearly a million supernatural entities represented power that exceeded anything in their understanding of conventional warfare. Yet the authority with which Jeremiah spoke suggested that these were not mere legends but historical facts preserved through divine revelation.

"The sword did not remain in human hands after the great flood," the prophet explained, his voice carrying the weight of knowledge that reached across centuries of hidden history. "It was placed in a location where it would remain safe until the time came when mortal men would again face enemies that required supernatural countermeasures to defeat."

Jeremiah's revelation now reached its crucial point as he addressed the question that had been building in every mind since he began his exposition. "The Almighty hath shown me through divine vision the exact location where this holy weapon awaiteth the one destined to wield it against the forces of darkness that now threaten the Irish kingdom."

The prophet paused to ensure that his next words would receive the attention they deserved. "The Great Sword of Methuselah lieth buried in the coldest reaches of the northern territories, deep within the heart of giant country where few mortal men have ever ventured and fewer still have returned to tell of what they encountered."

The location described by Jeremiah presented challenges that would have daunted even the most experienced adventurers. The northern territories encompassed some of the most hostile terrain in the known world, where brutal weather conditions combined with supernatural dangers to create an environment that tested human endurance beyond normal limitations.

"The retrieval of this weapon," Jeremiah continued, "will require not merely courage and skill, but also divine guidance to navigate the spiritual obstacles that guard its resting place. The journey will be perilous beyond conventional understanding, yet it doth represent the only hope for acquiring the means to defeat the Morrigan and her supernatural servants."

The prophet's exposition of the weapon's special properties provided additional details that underscored both its potential effectiveness and the dangers associated with its use. "The Great Sword beareth blessings that make it uniquely effective against giant flesh, releasing spiritual plagues upon contact that corrupt supernatural healing abilities while inflicting damage that doth transcend normal physical limitations. Yet against mortal enemies, it doth function as a supremely crafted conventional weapon that dealeth devastating but predictable damage."

These capabilities made the weapon ideal for confronting the mixed force of mortal and supernatural enemies that threatened the Irish kingdom, providing advantages against otherworldly creatures while remaining effective in conventional combat situations.

As Jeremiah concluded his exposition of the Great Sword's history and capabilities, the assembled council found themselves contemplating a quest that offered genuine hope for victory yet demanded sacrifices that few would be prepared to make. The journey to retrieve such a weapon would require penetrating hostile territory controlled by giant clans whose strength exceeded human limitations, navigating supernatural obstacles that had been designed to prevent exactly such recovery attempts, and surviving weather conditions that challenged human endurance beyond normal limits.

It was at this moment that Cian mac Morna rose from his position beside Fergus with the sort of controlled intensity that marked warriors who had found their true purpose. His piercing blue eyes blazed with determination that spoke of years of preparation suddenly finding their proper focus, while his bearing reflected readiness to undertake challenges that would daunt men twice his age and experience.

"Esteemed Prophet," he declared, his voice carrying the conviction of one who had discovered his destiny, "I volunteer to retrieve this holy weapon from whatever dangers guard its resting place. If the Great Sword of Methuselah doth represent our only hope for defeating supernatural enemies that conventional weapons cannot touch, then I pledge my life to ensuring its recovery."

The lieutenant's offer demonstrated the sort of courage that had made him legendary within the Craobh Ruadh, yet his words carried additional meaning for those who understood the personal motivations that drove his dedication to this particular mission. Here was a warrior whose entire adult life had been shaped by the desire to confront the shapeshifters and otherworldly creatures that had destroyed his family, suddenly offered the means to achieve vengeance that had seemed forever beyond his reach.

"Give me the coordinates where this sacred weapon lieth hidden," Cian continued, his voice intensifying with the passion of one who had found purpose beyond personal advancement, "and if the King and my commander grant permission, I swear by all that I hold sacred that I will wield it against our supernatural enemies with skill that doth honour both the weapon's holy nature and the righteous cause it will serve!"

The fierce determination that blazed in his eyes spoke of commitment that transcended mere military duty, encompassing personal vengeance, sacred obligation, and recognition of divine purpose that connected his individual quest with the broader struggle for Irish independence. His offer represented not merely military volunteering but spiritual dedication that acknowledged the supernatural dimensions of the conflict they faced.

Fergus mac Roich regarded his lieutenant with the sort of measured assessment that marked experienced commanders evaluating proposals that could determine the outcome of entire campaigns. "Cian mac Morna," he said, using the formal address that befitted the solemnity of the moment, "the mission thou dost propose representeth dangers that exceed anything in our experience of military operations. Art thou prepared to undertake a quest that may require sacrificing thy life without guarantee that the weapon can be recovered or that its power will prove sufficient to turn the tide of battle?"

The Knight-Commander's question addressed the harsh realities that surrounded any attempt to penetrate giant territory during the hostile northern winter. Even setting aside the supernatural obstacles that guarded the weapon's resting place, the journey itself would test human endurance beyond normal limitations while providing numerous opportunities for death by exposure, starvation, or conventional violence.

Cian's response demonstrated the sort of unwavering commitment that had defined his character since childhood trauma had forged him into a weapon of controlled vengeance. "Sir Fergus," he replied, his voice carrying conviction that admitted no doubt or reservation, "I have spent my entire adult life preparing for the opportunity to confront supernatural enemies with weapons that can actually harm them. If this quest doth offer the means to achieve that goal, then I consider death during the attempt preferable to continued life knowing that such enemies remain beyond my reach."

The lieutenant's words struck the assembled council with their absolute sincerity, revealing depths of personal commitment that transformed military volunteers into sacred obligations. Here was a warrior who viewed the proposed mission not as dangerous duty but as the culmination of purposes that had shaped his entire existence.

High King Eochaidh Herremon himself spoke into the silence that followed Cian's declaration of commitment. "Lieutenant mac Morna," he said, his voice carrying the authority of royal judgment combined with the wisdom of a ruler who understood the value of dedicated service, "the courage thou dost display honoreth both thy family's memory and the kingdom thou dost serve. Yet wisdom demandeth that we consider not merely the nobility of thy intentions but also the practical requirements for mission success."

The High King's words reflected the sort of careful calculation that separated effective rulers from those who allowed emotional appeals to override strategic necessity. "The quest thou dost propose will require not merely individual courage but also specialized knowledge of the northern territories, understanding of giant culture and capabilities, and preparation adequate to survive conditions that have defeated previous expeditions equipped with greater resources."

Eochaidh Herremon's assessment of mission requirements demonstrated royal wisdom that encompassed both the strategic value of recovering the Great Sword and the practical challenges that made such recovery extraordinarily difficult. "We cannot afford to waste the life of one of our most promising young warriors on a mission that faileth through inadequate preparation, yet neither can we allow fear of failure to prevent attempts that might provide our only hope for victory."

The King's measured approach to evaluating Cian's proposal created space for the sort of detailed planning that would maximize chances for success while acknowledging the inherent risks that no amount of preparation could eliminate. "Prophet Jeremiah," he continued, addressing the source of divine guidance that had revealed the weapon's location, "what additional knowledge canst thou provide regarding the specific challenges that such a mission would face?"

Jeremiah's response demonstrated the sort of comprehensive divine revelation that encompassed not merely spiritual insights but also practical details necessary for mission planning. "Your Majesty," he began, consulting what appeared to be mental images provided through prophetic vision, "the Great Sword lieth buried beneath a stone circle that was raised by the first giants to settle in these lands, located in a valley that doth remain hidden from casual observation by supernatural mists that confuse normal navigation."

The prophet's description of the weapon's location provided geographical details that would be essential for mission planning while also revealing the spiritual obstacles that protected it from unauthorized recovery. "The guardians that watch over this sacred site include not merely the giant clans that control the surrounding territory, but also spiritual entities that were placed there specifically to prevent the weapon's misuse by those unworthy of its power."

These additional challenges transformed the mission from difficult to nearly impossible, requiring not merely exceptional combat skills and wilderness survival capabilities but also spiritual preparation adequate to overcome supernatural obstacles that had been designed by powers beyond human understanding.

"Yet the Almighty hath also shown me," Jeremiah continued, his voice carrying the authority of divine promise, "that the warrior destined to retrieve this weapon will be provided with guidance adequate to overcome every obstacle, protection sufficient to survive every danger, and spiritual authority necessary to claim the sword for righteous purposes."

The prophet's words suggested that success would depend upon divine intervention rather than merely human capability, transforming the mission from military operation into spiritual quest that acknowledged the supernatural dimensions of the conflict they faced.

As the war council contemplated the extraordinary proposal that had emerged from their strategic deliberations, it became clear that they were witnessing the convergence of individual destiny with cosmic purpose that would determine not merely the outcome of military campaigns but the spiritual direction of an entire people. The Great Sword of Methuselah represented hope for victory against supernatural enemies, yet its recovery would require faith that transcended conventional military planning.

The session that had begun with dire intelligence regarding enemy capabilities had been transformed through divine revelation into recognition that weapons existed to counter even the most powerful supernatural threats. Yet accessing such weapons would demand sacrifices and risks that tested human courage beyond normal limitations.

Cian mac Morna stood ready to undertake a quest that offered the possibility of achieving both personal vengeance and strategic victory, yet success would require divine blessing that could not be guaranteed through human effort alone. The young warrior who had dedicated his life to mastering conventional combat skills now faced the ultimate test of whether his preparation had been adequate for challenges that transcended normal military understanding.

The war council had revealed both the magnitude of threats facing the Irish kingdom and the potential means for overcoming them, yet the path forward would require courage, faith, and divine intervention that reached beyond anything in their previous experience of warfare. The gathering storm was approaching its climax, and the weapons necessary for victory lay hidden in the most dangerous territory their enemies controlled.

The stage was set for a quest that would determine whether supernatural evil could be defeated through mortal courage enhanced by divine power, or whether the forces of darkness would triumph through advantages that exceeded human ability to counter. The Great Sword of Methuselah awaited its destined wielder, yet the journey to claim it would test every element of character and capability that Cian mac Morna had developed through years of relentless preparation for this very moment.

The fate of kingdoms hung in the balance, and only time would reveal whether divine purpose or supernatural malevolence would ultimately prevail in the spiritual warfare that was about to determine the destiny of the Irish people and the future of righteous government in the western world.
 
Chapter 19: The Quest for Methuselah's Sword

 As compiled by Baruch ben Neriah

 In which the true heritage of Cian mac Morna is revealed, spanning back to the valorous days of Moses and Joshua, and a perilous quest into giant territory leads to both the recovery of a legendary weapon and the rescue of a maiden whose name carries echoes of tragedy transformed into hope

The war council's conclusion had left the great hall of Tara charged with an atmosphere of barely contained urgency, as if the very stones themselves understood that momentous decisions had been made that would reshape the destiny of kingdoms. Yet it was in the private chamber where High King Eochaidh Herremon had withdrawn with his most trusted advisors that the full magnitude of Cian mac Morna's proposed quest began to reveal itself in all its supernatural complexity.

Jeremiah stood before the assembled leadership with the bearing of one who carried knowledge that reached far beyond the immediate tactical concerns of the coming conflict. His weathered hands held scrolls whose ancient parchment spoke of genealogical records preserved through generations of careful stewardship, while his eyes held the depth of prophetic insight that could perceive connections spanning centuries of hidden history.

"The quest you have undertaken, young warrior," the prophet began, his voice carrying the weight of divine revelation, "is far more perilous than even my initial description conveyed. The Almighty hath shown me through vision that this mission must be accomplished in absolute solitude, for any who would accompany the destined bearer of Methuselah's Sword are bound by spiritual law to perish during the attempt."

The prophet's words struck the assembled council with the force of divine judgment, transforming what had appeared to be a dangerous but conventional military operation into something approaching a death sentence for all but the chosen individual. Even Fergus mac Roich, whose experience encompassed decades of the most hazardous combat operations, paled at the implications of what was being revealed.

"Your faith and trust in the Almighty will be tested beyond anything in thy previous experience," Jeremiah continued, his gaze fixed upon Cian with an intensity that seemed to penetrate the young warrior's very soul. "Yet thou dost not undertake this quest as a mere mortal relying upon human strength alone, for I know the true nature of thy lineage, Cian mac Morna, and the supernatural heritage that floweth in thy veins!"

The prophet's declaration of knowledge regarding Cian's ancestry created a moment of profound silence as every person present recognized that they were about to witness revelations that would transform their understanding of the young lieutenant's true nature and destiny.

"Your bloodline stretcheth back through the mists of time to the very days when Moses led the children of Israel from Egyptian bondage and Joshua conquered the Promised Land through divine intervention," Jeremiah proclaimed, his voice now carrying the authority of one speaking direct revelation from the throne of heaven. "Thy ancestor Mac-hir—whose name echoes through the generations in the Irish naming tradition of 'Mac' or ‘son of’ that marketh thine own heritage—was the firstborn son of Manasseh, who himself was the firstborn of Joseph, holder of the birthright blessing that Jacob proclaimed with prophetic authority."

The genealogical connections that Jeremiah began to unfold struck Cian with the force of lightning illuminating a landscape that had previously remained hidden in darkness. Here was explanation for capabilities and instincts that had always seemed to exceed normal human limitations, rooted in bloodlines that connected him to the very foundations of Hebrew history and divine purpose.

"When the patriarch Jacob blessed his grandsons Ephraim and Manasseh," the prophet continued, consulting the ancient records that contained the preserved genealogies of the tribes, "he declared with prophetic authority, 'Let my name be named upon them,' thus transferring the heritage of Israel itself to the house of Joseph through his sons. Since Jacob's name had been changed by divine command to Israel, this blessing established that the name Israel itself became the birthright inheritance of Manasseh, even as the sceptre remained with Judah according to separate prophetic promises."

The implications of this ancestral heritage began to penetrate Cian's understanding as he grasped that his lineage connected him not merely to Hebrew nobility but to the very foundations of the covenant promises that had shaped the destiny of nations. The birthright blessing carried with it not merely material inheritance but spiritual authority that transcended normal human limitations.

"Machir thy ancestor," Jeremiah continued, his voice growing more powerful as he recounted the heroic deeds preserved in sacred chronicle, "was described in the ancient records as 'a man of war' whose valour exceeded even the legendary warriors of his generation. The scriptures themselves proclaim his greatness, declaring that 'Machir was a man of war' who carved his name into history through deeds that required supernatural courage and divine blessing to accomplish."

The prophet's exposition of Machir's achievements painted a picture of warrior-leadership that established patterns of greatness that would echo through generations of his descendants. "When the tribes of Israel prepared to take possession of their inheritance in the Promised Land, it was Machir and his son Gilead who volunteered for the most dangerous assignments—the clearing of Bashan from the giant clans that had established their dominion in the eastern territories."

Jeremiah's description of these ancient campaigns provided context that explained the supernatural elements that had always characterized Cian's own military career. "The land of Bashan was the stronghold of giants whose size and strength exceeded normal human capabilities, yet Machir and his descendants systematically destroyed their kingdoms through warfare that required divine assistance to achieve victory. They conquered the realm of Og, king of Bashan, whose iron bedstead measured nine cubits in length and four cubits in breadth—a monument to gigantic stature that required supernatural courage to confront."

The prophet's recounting of these ancestral victories created recognition in Cian's mind of patterns that had characterized his own military service without his previous understanding of their significance. "Thy noble bloodline hath always been known for valour that exceedeth normal human limitations, young warrior, and I perceive in thee the same greatness that thy forefathers exemplified when they faced impossible odds through faith in divine provision rather than reliance upon merely mortal strength."

Jeremiah's revelation now reached its most personal and encouraging dimension as he addressed the supernatural protection that had characterized Cian's entire military career. "Do not fear to undertake this perilous mission in solitude," the aged prophet counselled, his voice carrying the warmth of paternal encouragement combined with prophetic authority, "for a mighty angel of the Lord hath been assigned as thy guardian from youth, protecting thee through trials that would have destroyed lesser men and enabling victories that appeared impossible through purely human capability."

The mention of angelic protection suddenly provided explanation for combat experiences that had puzzled Cian throughout his military service. "Thou hast noticed, hast thou not, during thy previous campaigns, that impossible things tend to occur whenever thou dost face mortal danger against overwhelming odds? When surrounded by enemies whose numbers should have guaranteed thy destruction, yet somehow achieving victory through means that defied rational explanation?"

Memory flooded through Cian's consciousness as he recalled the incident at Glen Coe where he had single-handedly faced a Pictish raiding party that outnumbered him more than a hundred to one. He had been exhausted from three days of continuous fighting, wounded from multiple previous engagements, and armed only with his sword and a handful of throwing knives when the enemy had finally cornered him in a narrow defile that should have become his grave.

The battle that followed had transcended anything in his understanding of conventional warfare. His sword had seemed to move with supernatural speed and precision, finding vital targets through armour that should have deflected his strikes, while his movements had displayed agility that exceeded his normal physical capabilities despite his wounds and fatigue. Enemy weapons that should have found their marks had somehow missed by impossible margins, while his own attacks had achieved devastating effectiveness that cleared the narrow pass of hostile forces in a matter of minutes.

"I remember," Cian said quietly, his voice carrying the wonder of one recognizing divine intervention in events he had previously attributed to exceptional training and fortunate circumstances. "At Glen Coe, when the Picts had me trapped and I should have died a dozen times over, yet somehow..." He paused, struggling to articulate experiences that had always seemed to exist beyond the realm of normal military analysis.

"Yet somehow thy sword found targets that conventional skill could not have reached," Jeremiah completed the thought with the authority of one who had witnessed similar divine interventions throughout his prophetic ministry. "Thy movements displayed coordination that exceeded human reflexes, and enemy attacks missed their marks by margins that defied the laws of probability. The angel assigned to thy protection manifested his presence through enhancement of thy natural abilities, transforming mortal skill into supernatural effectiveness."

The prophet's explanation provided context that transformed every puzzling aspect of Cian's military career from mysterious coincidence into evidence of divine purpose that had been shaping his destiny since childhood. "Such protection is not granted lightly or without purpose," Jeremiah continued, his voice carrying the weight of one who understood the spiritual significance of what he was revealing. "Thy bloodline carrieth obligations as well as privileges, and the angel who guardeth thee doth so in recognition of the role thou art destined to play in the cosmic struggle between light and darkness."

Understanding flooded through Cian's consciousness as he grasped the implications of what was being revealed. The supernatural protection that had preserved him through impossible odds, the combat abilities that exceeded normal human limitations, the instinctive understanding of tactics and strategy that had earned him recognition among the elite warriors of Ireland—all were evidence of heritage and destiny that reached far beyond his previous comprehension.

"The quest for Methuselah's Sword," Jeremiah concluded, "representeth not merely military necessity but the fulfilment of purposes that have been developing through thy entire lineage since the days when Machir first demonstrated that mortal courage enhanced by divine blessing could overcome supernatural enemies. Thou goest forth not as an orphan seeking vengeance, but as heir to a warrior tradition that hath been conquering giants for more than a thousand years."

The preparations for Cian's departure required careful balance between the practical necessities of wilderness survival and the spiritual requirements of a quest that would depend upon divine intervention rather than conventional military logistics. His equipment was selected not for quantity but for quality, recognizing that excessive gear would only burden him during the challenging journey into giant territory while providing no meaningful advantage against supernatural obstacles.

The armour he donned for this perilous mission represented the pinnacle of Celtic craftsmanship combined with innovations learned through years of specialized combat operations. Black leather formed the foundation—not the crude hide of common soldiers, but leather worked to the consistency of silk yet possessing the protective qualities of blade plate. Each piece had been treated with oils and substances known only to the master armour smiths of the Craobh Ruadh, creating protection that moved like a second skin while deflecting blades that would penetrate conventional leather armour.

Silver buckles and fittings adorned the black leather, each one precisely crafted and positioned not merely for decoration but for maximum functionality during the sort of rapid equipment access that meant the difference between life and death in close combat. The buckles bore intricate Celtic knotwork that spoke of generations of military tradition, while their positioning allowed for lightning-fast adjustments that could adapt the armour's configuration to changing tactical requirements.

The chest piece bore the embroidered insignia of House mac Morna—a clump of arrows crossed with an olive branch, worked in threads that that connected earthly symbols with divine protection. The clump of arrows represented the martial prowess and readiness for war that had characterized his family line, while the olive branch symbolized their ultimate desire for peace and just governance—embodying the ancient wisdom that if you want peace, you must prepare for war.

Pockets and pouches riddled the armour’s surface in patterns that appeared random to casual observation yet followed principles of tactical organization that maximized accessibility while maintaining the balance necessary for fluid movement. Each pocket served specific purposes—some held healing herbs and emergency provisions, others contained specialized tools for lock-picking, trap detection, and the sort of infiltration work that characterized elite operations, while still others held the throwing implements that could mean the difference between success and failure when conventional weapons proved inadequate.

The cape that completed his ensemble bore the heraldic symbol of the Craobh Ruadh—the Red Branch itself, worked in crimson threads that seemed to glow with their own inner fire against the midnight black of the cloak's fabric. This was no mere decorative addition but a tactical garment whose design incorporated features that enhanced both stealth and combat effectiveness. The cloak could be deployed to confuse enemy targeting while its weighted edges could be used as weapons in close quarters, and its material had been treated with substances that helped break up the wearer's outline during night operations.

His arsenal represented the culmination of years spent mastering every weapon that could serve the specialized requirements of elite warfare. His primary sword—the blade he had mentioned leaving behind—was indeed a masterwork of Irish metallurgy, yet for this mission he carried weapons selected specifically for their versatility and reliability under the extreme conditions he would face.

A short sword of exceptional balance hung at his left hip; its blade forged according to techniques that combined Celtic tradition with innovations learned from Mediterranean smiths. The weapon's length made it ideal for close-quarters combat while its weight distribution allowed for lightning-fast draw and deployment. The cross guard bore engravings that matched his family insignia, while the pommel had been weighted to serve as a striking weapon when the situation demanded improvised solutions.

Strapped to his right thigh was a spear designed for both mounted and dismounted combat, its ash shaft selected for its combination of flexibility and strength while its iron point had been forged with a slight curve that improved penetration against armour. The spear could serve as a lance during mounted charges, a staff weapon for ground combat, or even as a throwing weapon when circumstances required ranged engagement at distances too great for other implements.

His collection of throwing knives represented perhaps the most specialized aspect of his armament. Twelve identical blades were distributed throughout his gear in carefully planned locations that allowed for rapid access regardless of combat position or tactical situation. Each knife had been balanced to his precise specifications, with weight distribution that enabled accurate throwing at ranges from three to thirty paces. The blades themselves were forged from blade that held its edge even after piercing bone and armour, while their hilts bore subtle markings that allowed for grip recognition even in complete darkness.

Most innovative among his weapons were the matched pair of one-handed crossbows that represented cutting-edge military technology adapted from continental designs yet modified according to Irish tactical requirements. Each crossbow could be operated with a single hand while the other wielded a sword, allowing for simultaneous ranged and melee combat that gave him overwhelming advantages against opponents expecting conventional weapon combinations. The bolts were specially crafted for maximum penetration and carried tips that had been blessed according to rituals designed to enhance their effectiveness against supernatural enemies.

Throwing stars—a weapon learned from foreign merchants who had travelled the eastern trade routes—provided silent ranged options for situations where the sound of crossbow deployment might compromise stealth operations. These six-pointed implements had been crafted according to principles that balanced aerodynamic efficiency with penetrating power, enabling accurate deployment at ranges where conventional throwing knives might lack sufficient impact.

His mount, waiting in the stables of Tara with the patient readiness of a war-trained destrier, represented bloodlines that had been developed specifically for the requirements of elite cavalry operations. Dúnscáth—named for the legendary fortress where the greatest warriors of Celtic tradition had received their training—stood seventeen hands high with the sort of powerful build that could carry an armoured warrior through extended campaign operations while retaining the speed and agility necessary for reconnaissance and infiltration missions.

The stallion's coat gleamed black as a moonless night, unmarked by any white that might compromise stealth during night operations, while his temperament combined the fierce loyalty of a guard dog with the tactical intelligence that enabled him to respond to battlefield conditions without constant guidance from his rider. Dúnscáth had been trained not merely as transportation but as a combat partner whose hooves and teeth could serve as weapons while his supernatural awareness of danger provided early warning that had saved Cian's life on multiple previous occasions.

His provisions were calculated to sustain him through a journey whose duration could not be predicted with certainty, yet remained light enough to preserve his mobility during what would undoubtedly prove to be a mission requiring stealth and speed rather than sustained siege operations. Travel rations prepared according to military standards, healing herbs selected for their effectiveness against both conventional wounds and supernatural afflictions, and basic survival equipment that could mean the difference between success and failure in the hostile northern territories.

Most importantly, he carried with him the spiritual preparation that had been provided through intensive consultation with Jeremiah and the Hebrew priests of our company, creating understanding of the supernatural forces he would encounter and the spiritual authority necessary to overcome obstacles that mere physical courage could not address. Sacred symbols blessed according to Hebrew tradition, and most crucially, the faith that came from understanding his true heritage and the divine protection that accompanied it.

The journey northward into giant territory required navigation through landscape that grew increasingly hostile with every mile travelled beyond the borders of civilized territory. The rolling hills and gentle meadows that characterized the heart of Ireland gradually gave way to rougher terrain where ancient forests harboured creatures that avoided contact with human civilization, while the weather patterns shifted from the mild maritime climate of the southern regions to the harsh continental conditions that made the northern territories nearly uninhabitable for normal human populations.

Yet it was not merely the natural obstacles that marked his passage into enemy territory, but the supernatural oppression that seemed to increase with every step toward his destination. The very air carried a weight of malevolent attention that pressed upon his consciousness like a physical burden, while the landscape itself seemed to resist his progress through subtle alterations of familiar landmarks that created navigation challenges beyond anything in conventional military experience.

The giant settlements that dotted the northern territories represented a form of civilization that operated according to principles fundamentally different from human society. Massive stone structures whose construction required strength that exceeded normal human capabilities, agricultural practices adapted to the needs of beings whose size and appetites dwarfed those of mortal men, and defensive installations that combined primitive engineering with supernatural enhancements that created formidable obstacles for any who would challenge giant authority in their own territory.

Cian's infiltration of this hostile environment required all the stealth techniques he had mastered during his training with the Craobh Ruadh, enhanced by the supernatural awareness that his angelic protection provided regarding threats that normal human senses could not detect. His movement through giant territory took on an almost ethereal quality as he found himself guided by instincts that seemed to anticipate patrol patterns and surveillance methods before they became apparent through conventional observation.

The closer he approached to the location that Jeremiah's visions had revealed as the resting place of Methuselah's Sword, the more apparent it became that he was operating under protection that exceeded anything in his previous experience of covert operations. Guard posts that should have detected his presence somehow remained unaware of his passage, while tracker beasts whose supernatural senses should have identified his scent failed to raise alarms that would have brought giant warriors converging upon his position.

It was during the final approach to his objective that the full magnitude of his supernatural protection became undeniably apparent. A patrol of six giant warriors, each standing more than twice his height and armed with weapons that could have crushed him with a single blow, passed within arm's reach of his concealed position without displaying any awareness of his presence. The angelic intervention that concealed him from their enhanced senses created a sphere of supernatural invisibility that enabled him to observe their movements and patrol patterns while remaining completely undetected.

The site where Methuselah's Sword lay hidden proved to be everything that Jeremiah's prophetic visions had suggested and more besides. A valley concealed by supernatural mists that confused normal navigation lay nestled between peaks whose stone formations seemed to pulse with malevolent energy, while the very air carried emanations of spiritual corruption that pressed upon the consciousness of any who dared approach the sacred location.

At the centre of this hidden valley stood a stone circle whose construction predated any human civilization in the known world, its massive monoliths arranged according to principles that hurt the mind to contemplate while radiating power that made even the surrounding giant settlements seem primitive by comparison. Each stone bore inscriptions in scripts that had been ancient when the first giants settled in these lands, while the spaces between them shimmered with energies that existed partially beyond the physical realm.

The defensive enchantments that protected the buried sword created an atmosphere of dread so oppressive that even Cian's supernatural courage wavered as he approached the perimeter of the sacred site. The very ground seemed to resist his footsteps, while whispers in languages that predated human speech echoed through his consciousness with promises of destruction that would befall any who dared violate the ancient protections.

Visual manifestations of the protective spells created illusions of horror designed to break the will of potential intruders before they could approach close enough to threaten the buried weapon. Phantoms of previous visitors who had died attempting to claim the sword rose from the corrupted earth, their spectral forms reaching toward him with the desperate hunger of those condemned to eternal torment for their presumption in challenging powers beyond mortal comprehension.

The spiritual oppression that emanated from the stone circle intensified with each step toward the centre where ancient power had buried the legendary weapon. Cian felt his strength draining away as if the very act of approaching his objective was consuming his life force, while his vision began to blur with hallucinations that painted pictures of failure and death that seemed more real than the physical landscape around him.

It was when the defensive enchantments reached their peak intensity, creating waves of supernatural terror that threatened to shatter his sanity entirely, that Cian finally understood the true nature of the test he faced. This was not merely a trial of physical courage or military skill, but a spiritual ordeal that required absolute faith in divine protection against forces that exceeded human ability to comprehend or resist through mortal strength alone.

Falling to his knees on ground that seemed to burn with cold fire, Cian lifted his voice in prayer that carried all the anguish and desperation of a soul facing obstacles that transcended anything in his experience of worldly trials. "God of my fathers, the One True God who the prophet Jeremiah serves, who gave victory to Joshua over the giants of Canaan and blessed Machir with conquest over the kingdoms of Bashan—heed, I pray, your servant's call in this hour of extremity!"

His voice broke with emotion as the full weight of his heritage and the magnitude of his mission pressed upon his consciousness. "I am Cian mac Morna, descendant of Machir the man of war, heir to bloodlines that have served Your purposes since the foundations of the world were laid. I come not seeking glory for myself but instruments of righteousness that may serve Your justice against enemies who would corrupt Your creation and destroy those who trust in Your protection!"

The prayer that poured from his lips carried the accumulated faith of generations who had faced impossible odds through trust in divine provision rather than reliance upon human strength. "—grant now to Your servant the means to serve Your purposes against supernatural enemies who mock Your authority and threaten Your people!"

As Cian's prayer reached its climax, pouring out his soul before the throne of heaven with the sort of desperate faith that moves mountains and commands the attention of angels, the oppressive spiritual atmosphere that had threatened to crush his spirit began to dissipate like morning mist before the rising sun. The whispers of condemnation faded into silence, while the phantoms of the dead dissolved back into the corrupted earth from which they had emerged.
The change that swept through the hidden valley was both sudden and complete, as if a switch had been thrown that deactivated every defensive enchantment simultaneously. The stone circle that had radiated malevolent power moments before now stood silent and inert, its ancient inscriptions fading into illegibility while the supernatural energies that had protected the site for millennia simply winked out of existence.

Where moments before the very air had been thick with spiritual oppression that threatened to overwhelm any mortal who dared approach, now there was only the crisp mountain atmosphere of a remote valley where ancient secrets lay waiting to be reclaimed by one whose heritage and faith had proven sufficient to overcome obstacles that had defeated every previous challenger.

Rising from his knees with wonder that bordered on awe, Cian approached the centre of the stone circle where the ground showed clear evidence of recent supernatural disturbance. The earth itself seemed to part before his advance, revealing glimpses of worked stone that had been hidden beneath centuries of accumulated soil and debris.

The excavation that followed required no tools beyond his bare hands, as the ground yielded its buried treasure with the same divine assistance that had neutralized the protective enchantments. Layer by layer, the ancient hiding place revealed itself until his fingers closed around the hilt of a weapon that had been forged according to divine instruction for warfare against enemies that existed partially beyond the physical realm.

Methuselah's Sword, when he finally drew it from its resting place of millennia, proved to be far different from his expectations of what a legendary weapon should appear. Rather than the gleaming blade of supernatural craftsmanship that his imagination had envisioned, he found himself holding what appeared to be an ordinary great sword whose ancient construction spoke of skilled but entirely conventional metallurgy.

The blade showed the wear of incredible age without displaying any of the supernatural radiance or obvious enchantment that he had expected from a weapon credited with destroying nearly a million supernatural entities. Its balance was adequate but not exceptional, its edge serviceable but not remarkable, and its overall appearance suggested that it would be outclassed by the sword he had left behind at Tara in terms of both craftsmanship and effectiveness.

"My great sword I left at home is far superior to this," Cian muttered aloud, examining the legendary weapon with disappointment that bordered on disbelief. Here was the instrument that was supposed to provide victory against supernatural enemies that conventional weapons could not touch, yet it appeared to be nothing more than an aging relic whose only distinction was its remarkable state of preservation.

It was as he stood contemplating the apparent ordinariness of his prize that sounds from the surrounding forest indicated he was no longer alone in the hidden valley. Voices carried on the mountain air—harsh, guttural speech that spoke of beings whose nature differed fundamentally from human civilization, accompanied by other sounds that suggested the presence of captives being moved against their will.

Concealing himself among the stones of the ancient circle, Cian crept toward the source of the disturbance with the stealth techniques that had served him well during his infiltration of giant territory. What he discovered when he reached a position that allowed observation of the approaching group filled him with horror that transcended anything in his previous experience of wartime atrocities.

Two shapeshifters whose forms hovered between the material and ethereal realms moved with the flowing grace of beings that existed partially outside normal physical law, their appearance shifting between beautiful human forms and something far more sinister that hurt the eyes to observe directly. Their conversation carried the sort of casual malevolence that spoke of creatures for whom the suffering of mortals represented nothing more than tools to be employed for supernatural purposes.

Flanking them were two giants whose massive frames dwarfed even the largest human warriors, their crude armour and primitive weapons doing nothing to disguise the terrible strength that made them individually capable of destroying entire companies of mortal soldiers. Between them, bound with chains that showed evidence of repeated escape attempts, was a young woman whose defiant bearing spoke of courage that refused to be broken despite circumstances that would have shattered weaker spirits.

"Good!" declared one of the shapeshifters in a voice that carried harmonics of otherworldly malice. "We have acquired the virgin that our mistress requires for the ritual. I trust you oafs have maintained her purity and kept her body unharmed for the purposes we intend?"

"We have!" rumbled one of the giant guards, his voice carrying the sort of defensive pride that suggested the task had been more challenging than anticipated. "She has been a slave in our service ever since the mistress brought her to us, and we have followed instructions precisely regarding her... preservation."

"We are glad to be rid of her," added the second giant with obvious relief. "She has been more trouble than her worth—always attempting escape, poisoning our food supplies, setting fire to our storage facilities, and even managing to release some of our human cattle before we could recapture them. But we always catch her," he concluded with grim satisfaction. "We always catch her, and this time there will be no opportunity for further mischief."
The shapeshifter's response carried tones of satisfaction that made Cian's skin crawl with revulsion. "Excellent news. We require her blood—specifically, the blood of a pure virgin—to reactivate the enchantments that have been protecting that accursed sword. We have felt the protective barriers weakening over the past few hours, and immediate action is required to restore the defences before anyone can claim the weapon for purposes contrary to our mistress's designs."

Understanding flooded through Cian's consciousness as he grasped the true nature of what he was witnessing. The ritual being planned would not merely result in the murder of an innocent victim, but would also restore the supernatural protections that had been neutralized through divine intervention, potentially trapping him in the hidden valley or forcing him to face the same impossible obstacles that had defeated every previous challenger.

More importantly, the casual discussion of torture, enslavement, and ritual murder filled him with the sort of righteous fury that his ancestors had felt when confronting the giant kingdoms of Bashan. Here were enemies whose cruelty transcended mere military opposition, representing the sort of supernatural evil that required divine judgment administered through mortal instruments of righteousness.

The maiden herself, despite her bonds and evident exhaustion from the harsh treatment she had endured, displayed courage that reminded him of the Celtic warrior traditions that honoured bravery above all other virtues. Her refusal to show fear in the face of approaching death spoke of character that demanded protection regardless of the personal cost involved in providing it.

Rising from concealment with the sort of controlled fury that his training had taught him to channel into devastating effectiveness, Cian gripped Methuselah's Sword with new understanding of its purpose. Perhaps the weapon's power lay not in supernatural radiance or obvious enchantment, but in its sanctification for use against enemies whose evil exceeded normal human comprehension.

"Release the woman!" he commanded, his voice carrying across the clearing with authority that spoke of noble bloodlines and divine commission. "Face judgment for thy crimes against innocence, servants of darkness!"

The effect of his sudden appearance upon the assembled group was immediate and dramatic. The giants reacted with the sort of startled confusion that marked beings unaccustomed to being challenged by lone mortals, while the shapeshifters hissed with recognition that their plans had been discovered by someone whose presence in the hidden valley suggested capabilities that exceeded normal human limitations.

"How did a mortal penetrate the barriers that protect this sacred site?" demanded one of the shapeshifters, its form beginning to shift toward something more suited for combat than the beautiful human appearance it had been maintaining. "No human should be able to approach this place without triggering alarms that would have brought every guardian within leagues to investigate!"

"It mattereth not," snarled the second shapeshifter, its transformation revealing claws and fangs that spoke of supernatural predator designed for the efficient destruction of human life. "One mortal, no matter how skilled, cannot stand against beings whose power doth transcend the limitations of flesh and bone. We shall add his blood to the ritual and strengthen the protections beyond any future challenge!"

The battle that erupted as the shapeshifters and giants moved to eliminate this unexpected threat to their plans would be remembered among the greatest single combats in the annals of supernatural warfare. Cian's movement across the clearing toward his enemies displayed the sort of fluid grace that his training had developed through years of relentless practice, enhanced by the supernatural awareness that his angelic protection provided regarding threats that normal human senses could not anticipate.

Methuselah's Sword, whatever its outward appearance might suggest, proved to possess capabilities that became apparent only when wielded against supernatural enemies.
