Eochaidh's response carried the authority of one who spoke not merely for himself but for eternal purposes that transcended political disputes. His voice rang across the battlefield with clarity that reached every combatant, Irish and rebel alike.

"I fight for justice and righteousness, Aodhan mac Niall!" he replied with royal dignity unmarked by the tremendous exertions of battle. "I fight for the protection of the innocent and the fulfilment of divine will in the earth! If that makes me a puppet, then I am proud to serve such a master!"

The duel that should have followed between the two kings never took place, for as Eochaidh advanced with his bloodied sword raised high and divine power radiating from his very presence, Aodhan's courage finally failed him completely. The sight of the High King moving through his forces like an avenging angel, apparently untouchable and unstoppable, broke the last of the rebel's resolve.

"Retreat!" Aodhan shouted to his remaining forces, wheeling his horse away from the approaching nemesis with panic that infected his entire command structure. "Fall back to our own territories! This battle cannot be won! The very heavens fight against us!"

The effect of their leader's flight upon the remaining enemy forces was immediate and complete. Warriors who had fought with desperate courage suddenly found their will broken, and what had been an organized army became a mob of fleeing individuals seeking only to escape the field of their humiliation and defeat.

It was at this crucial moment that the Knights of the Scarlet Thread, who had been positioned in concealment to execute flanking manoeuvres, found their tactical mission transformed into something far more satisfying. Pryderi the Strong and Cuchulainn the Fierce had been growing increasingly frustrated as they watched the main battle unfold without their direct participation, their warrior souls chafing at what they perceived as exclusion from the glory of combat.

"After them!" roared Pryderi as he saw the enemy forces streaming away from the battlefield in disorganized retreat, his massive form practically dancing with excitement at the prospect of finally engaging the enemy. "Let none escape to trouble our borders again! This is our chance for glory!"

Cuchulainn's legendary battle fury, which had been building throughout the day as he watched others claim the honours of combat, finally found its outlet as he led his wing of knights in pursuit of the fleeing enemies. The berserker rage that made him famous throughout the Celtic world transformed him into something barely human—a force of pure destruction that cut down fleeing warriors with terrifying efficiency.

But it was the invocation of their traditional challenge that transformed the pursuit from military necessity into competitive entertainment of the highest order. As the Knights of the Scarlet Thread emerged from their concealment to intercept the fleeing enemy, Pryderi's booming voice carried across the field with an announcement that brought grins to the faces of every warrior within hearing.

"Attention, Knights of the Scarlet Thread!" Pryderi bellowed with the enthusiasm of a man who had found unexpected treasure. "I hereby invoke the sacred 'Ten Barrels of Mead Winner-Take-All Challenge!' Whichever knight can inflict the most damage upon these retreating cowards shall claim the entire prize!"

The effect of this proclamation upon the assembled knights was electric. What had been a simple pursuit of defeated enemies instantly transformed into a competitive sporting event of the most violent and entertaining variety. Warriors who had been merely following orders suddenly began calculating strategies for maximizing their individual tallies while ensuring that their personal rivals achieved lesser scores.

Cuchulainn's response to this challenge was immediate and characteristic. "Challenge accepted, you oversized mountain of muscle!" he called back with fierce joy. "Prepare to drink water for the next month while I enjoy the finest mead in Ireland!"

The pursuit that followed became the stuff of legend, as two of Ireland's mightiest warriors competed to determine who could wreak the most havoc among the fleeing enemy forces. Pryderi's massive frame allowed him to strike down multiple opponents with each swing of his great sword, while his natural strength enabled him to literally throw enemy warriors into their companions with bone-crushing force.

Cuchulainn's approach was more artistic but equally devastating, as his berserker transformation allowed him to move with supernatural speed while striking with precision that turned individual combats into brief demonstrations of martial supremacy. The Hound of Ulster seemed to flow through the retreating ranks like liquid death, each movement calculated for maximum efficiency and spectacular effect.

"Seventeen!" Pryderi called out as his sword claimed another group of fleeing warriors. "And that last one counts as two because he was riding a horse!"

"Twenty-three by my count!" Cuchulainn replied with savage glee, his blade work creating patterns of destruction that left enemy formations scattered like leaves in a hurricane. "And I am not even counting the ones who died of fright!"

The other Knights of the Scarlet Thread threw themselves into the competition with enthusiasm that bordered on the religious, each man determined to prove his worth in this ultimate contest of martial skill. The pursuit became a running festival of competitive violence, with warriors calling out their individual scores while performing increasingly spectacular feats of combat artistry.

Sir Brendan, watching this display from his position with the main force, shook his head with the fond exasperation of a veteran who had witnessed similar competitions before. "Those two will turn any battle into a sporting event," he observed to his companions. "But I shall wager the enemy will not soon forget the lesson they have learned today."

The slaughter continued all the way to the borders of the kingdom, where traditional protocols finally called a halt to the pursuit. But by then, Aodhan's army had been so thoroughly destroyed that it would be generations before any similar force could threaten the peace of Ireland.

As the Knights of the Scarlet Thread finally regrouped to tally their scores, the competitive nature of their pursuit had produced a result that was as psychologically devastating to their enemies as it was satisfying to themselves. The sight of Irish warriors treating the pursuit of their army as entertainment rather than serious warfare had broken something fundamental in the spirit of Aodhan's followers.

"Final count!" Pryderi announced as the knights gathered around their impromptu scorekeepers. "Forty-three confirmed kills, not counting the ones who surrendered!"

Cuchulainn's grin was visible even through the blood that covered his features. "Forty-seven, and I was being conservative with my counting! Looks like you'll be buying the mead for quite some time, my oversized friend!"

The good-natured argument that followed regarding the precise counting methods and what constituted a confirmed kill provided entertainment for the entire force as they began their return journey to Tara. The transformation of military pursuit into competitive sport had served multiple purposes—eliminating enemy forces, maintaining morale, and providing the sort of legendary tale that would be told around hearth fires for generations to come.

As the sun set over the battlefield, painting the sky in shades of gold and crimson that seemed to celebrate the divine victory, King Eochaidh stood among his warriors with royal dignity unmarked by the tremendous exertions of the day. His armour was splattered with the blood of his enemies, yet he himself bore no wounds, divine protection having proved as effective as prophetic promises had indicated.

"Let this day be remembered," the king proclaimed to his assembled forces, his voice carrying across the field where the bodies of the enemy lay scattered like the remnants of a broken dream. "Let it be recorded for future generations that when the forces of darkness gathered their full strength against the people of the Most High, divine power proved more than sufficient for complete victory."

Jeremiah, who had observed the entire battle from beside the Ark of the Covenant, spoke with prophetic authority about the significance of what had been accomplished. "This victory transcends military conquest," he declared, his aged voice carrying the weight of divine revelation. "Today the power of the Almighty has been demonstrated before all Ireland, and none will dare question the divine authority that has established righteousness in this land."

The Levites who had carried the Ark through the battle showed no sign of the exhaustion that marked the other warriors, their faces still radiant with the supernatural energy that had flowed through them during the conflict. The sacred chest itself seemed to pulse with inner light, as if the divine presence within had been stirred to active participation in the victory and was only slowly returning to its normal state of concealed power.

"The Ark has shown its power," observed Zadok, the senior Levite, with profound satisfaction. "As it did for our fathers in the conquest of Canaan, it has brought victory over enemies both natural and supernatural. The Lord of Hosts has indeed gone out before His people, and no force in heaven or earth can stand against His chosen vessels."

As I moved among the victorious warriors, recording their accounts of the supernatural events they had witnessed, I was struck by the unanimous testimony regarding the divine nature of their triumph. Every man spoke of strength beyond his normal capabilities, of protection that turned aside mortal weapons, of courage that overcame natural fear in the face of supernatural enemies.

The transformation of Ireland that began with this single day's victory would echo through the centuries that followed. The supernatural forces that had plagued the island for generations had been broken and scattered beyond any hope of recovery, while the human enemies of righteousness had been so thoroughly defeated that rebellion would not raise its head again for generations to come.

More significantly, the demonstration of divine power before all the people had established beyond question the spiritual authority that lay behind the earthly kingdom. No one who witnessed the events of that day could doubt that the Most High had chosen Ireland as a realm where His purposes would be fulfilled and His righteousness established for all time.

The Battle of Divine Wrath, as it came to be known in the songs and stories of future generations, had established more than political supremacy—it had demonstrated the reality of divine intervention in human affairs and the power available to those who trusted completely in the purposes of the Almighty. The kingdom that emerged from this victory would be unlike any other in the world, a realm where divine authority was not merely theoretical but demonstrably real and practically effective.

As we made our way back to Tara under the light of stars that seemed to shine with unusual brilliance, I reflected upon the remarkable providence that had brought us through such extraordinary trials to this moment of complete triumph. The sacred bloodline had been preserved, the divine weapons had been restored, and the kingdom had been established upon foundations that no earthly power could shake.

Tomorrow would bring new challenges and opportunities, but tonight brought the peace that comes from knowing that divine purposes had been accomplished through human faithfulness and divine power working in perfect harmony. The chronicles I was recording would serve not merely as historical documents but as testimonies to the reality of divine intervention and the rewards that come to those who trust completely in the will of the Almighty.

The greatest adventure might yet lie ahead, but the foundation had been laid for wonders that would surpass even the remarkable events we had already witnessed. Ireland had been established as a realm where the Most High reigned through earthly kings who served as His faithful servants, and no power in heaven or earth could shake what God Himself had built upon the rock of divine purpose.

 End of Part II
 
Chapter 21: The Hunt for the Shadow Weaver

As compiled by Baruch ben Neriah

In which Cian mac Morna and Sir Caswallon the Swift undertake the most perilous mission of the age, penetrating the very heart of supernatural darkness to confront Morrigan the Shadow Weaver in her accursed lair

As the thunderous clash of armies echoed across the plains before Tara and the divine power of the Ark of the Covenant devastated the supernatural forces arrayed against the kingdom, two figures moved with silent purpose through the pre-dawn darkness, embarking upon a mission that would determine the ultimate fate of Ireland and perhaps the entire Celtic world. I, Baruch ben Neriah, chronicler of these extraordinary events, had witnessed their departure from the royal chambers where final preparations for this most perilous of quests had been completed under the Prophet Jeremiah's solemn blessing.

Cian mac Morna moved through the corridors of Tara with the deadly grace of a predator born for war, Methuselah's Sword strapped across his broad shoulders where its blessed blade caught and reflected the torchlight with an inner fire that spoke of celestial power barely contained within mortal metal. The legendary weapon seemed to pulse with anticipation, as if it sensed the approaching confrontation with forces that had opposed holy purposes since the dawn of time.

Beside him walked Sir Caswallon the Swift, whose legendary speed and stealth had made him the perfect choice to guide this desperate mission into the very heart of enemy territory. The Celtic scout's weathered features bore the grim determination of one who understood that they embarked upon a quest from which return was far from certain, yet his bearing carried no trace of fear—only the professional focus of a warrior who had dedicated his life to impossible missions.

"The ships await us at the hidden cove," Caswallon reported in the low tones that had served him well during countless reconnaissance missions behind enemy lines. "Lysander hath the Sea Serpent prepared for immediate departure, while Llyr hath assembled his finest warriors aboard the Gae Bolga for the supporting operations along Aodhan's coastline."

The mention of the Irish vessel's name—Gae Bolga, after the legendary spear of the great hero Cuchulainn—brought a grim smile to Cian's features as he appreciated the symbolic appropriateness of launching their mission aboard a ship named for a weapon of legendary power. Like the mythical spear that never missed its target, their quest must succeed against impossible odds if the kingdom were to survive the supernatural threats that surrounded it.

The strategy that Jeremiah had devised for this most crucial of missions was as audacious as it was necessary. While the main battle raged before Tara's walls and drew the attention of every preternatural creature in Morrigan's service, two elite forces would strike at strategic targets that could tip the balance of the entire conflict. The Sea Serpent would deliver Cian and Caswallon to the point where the scout's previous reconnaissance had been blocked by concealment spells, allowing them to complete their hunt for the Shadow Weaver herself, while both vessels would then proceed to liberate the coastal settlements under Aodhan's oppressive rule.

"The liberation of the coastal ports serveth multiple purposes," Jeremiah had explained during their final briefing, his aged features grave with the weight of divine revelation. "Not only will it provide strategic staging areas for future operations, but the freed populations will serve as witnesses to the power of the Most High when His judgment falleth upon the enemies of righteousness."

The prophet's instructions regarding the cryptic signal they were to await had troubled the veteran warriors who would execute the coastal operations. "Hold thy positions until ye hear the dragon's roar," Jeremiah had commanded with the authority of divine inspiration, "and then advance upon Aodhan's stronghold with all speed, for that sound will mark the completion of our primary mission and the beginning of the final assault."

Korax, whose practical military experience had served the company well throughout their long journey, had voiced the concerns shared by many regarding this unusual directive. "Prophet," he had said with the respectful directness that marked his character, "the mention of a dragon's roar doth trouble me greatly. If that accursed creature Scáthgheal the Crimson Fury the Ancient is somehow involved in this signal, our forces may find themselves facing dangers beyond any we have previously encountered."

The prophet's response had carried the patient authority of one accustomed to explaining divine purposes to those who could see only earthly concerns. "Trust in the Lord of Hosts, faithful Korax," Jeremiah had replied with gentle firmness. "His ways are not our ways, and His methods often confound human understanding. Yet His word hath proven true throughout our journey, and He will not fail us in this crucial hour."

Korax's acceptance of these cryptic instructions had demonstrated the faith that had sustained their company through countless trials. "If the God of Israel hath spoken through thy lips, Prophet, then His servant will obey without question, though the path seemeth shrouded in mystery. Thy words have been tested and proven true too many times for doubt to find purchase in a soldier's heart."

The harbor where their vessels waited was shrouded in the sort of mist that seemed to answer divine purposes by concealing righteous endeavours from hostile observation. The Sea Serpent rested at anchor like a sleeping leviathan, her battle-scarred hull bearing witness to the countless trials she had weathered since their departure from Egyptian waters so many months before. Yet there was something in her bearing that spoke of eagerness for this final, crucial mission.

Lysander stood upon the vessel's deck with the bearing of a captain who had found his true calling in the service of divine purposes. The Greek sailor's transformation from mercenary opportunist to devoted servant of righteousness had been one of the remarkable spiritual developments of their long journey, and his commitment to this most dangerous mission reflected the depth of that change.

"The ship is ready for immediate departure," he reported to Cian as the warrior came aboard, his voice carrying the professional confidence that had made him such a valued member of their company. "The crew doth understand that we sail into waters where conventional navigation may prove inadequate, and they have prepared their hearts for whatever supernatural challenges we may encounter."

The Gae Bolga, anchored nearby, presented an equally impressive sight as Llyr of the Western Winds completed his final preparations for the coastal liberation operations. The Irish vessel had been selected specifically for her shallow draft and exceptional manoeuvrability in coastal waters, qualities that would prove essential for the hit-and-run tactics required to free Aodhan's oppressed subjects.

The Irish warriors who filled her decks represented the finest fighters from throughout Eochaidh's kingdom, each man handpicked for both martial skill and unwavering loyalty to the divine purposes that guided their mission. Their faces bore the grim determination of men who understood that they fought not merely for earthly conquest but for the eternal triumph of righteousness over the forces of darkness.

"Sir Cian," Llyr reported to Cian as he came aboard to review the supporting force, "these men have sworn sacred oaths to follow your mission with their lives if necessary. They understand that the liberation of our coastal peoples requires courage that transcends ordinary military service, and they are prepared to demonstrate that courage in whatever trials await us."

The formal blessing that Jeremiah pronounced over both vessels and their crews created an atmosphere of solemn dedication that transformed routine military preparation into something approaching sacred ritual. The prophet's words carried the weight of heavenly authority as he invoked the protection and favour of the Most High upon this most crucial of missions.

"O Lord God of Israel," Jeremiah prayed with his hands raised toward the star-filled heavens, "as You once sent Your servant David against the giant Goliath, and as You delivered Israel from the hosts of Pharaoh, grant now success to these Your faithful servants who venture into the very stronghold of supernatural evil to accomplish Your righteous purposes."

The prophet's blessing continued with specific invocations for each aspect of their dangerous mission. "Protect Cian mac Morna as he bears the sword of the patriarch into battle against the Shadow Weaver. Guide Caswallon the Swift as he leads Your champion through territories where darkness seeks to confound all righteous purpose. Strengthen the arms of Korax and his warriors as they liberate the oppressed and establish beachheads for the final triumph of Your kingdom."

As the vessels prepared to depart on the morning tide, the full scope of the strategic gamble they were undertaking became apparent to all who witnessed their departure. The main battle before Tara would serve as the ultimate diversion, drawing every available enemy force to a single point where the Ark of the Covenant could devastate them with sacred power, while these smaller forces struck at the preternatural foundation of enemy power.

The Sea Serpent's departure from Irish waters marked the beginning of a journey that would take them along coastlines where Aodhan's influence had spread like a plague, corrupting not only the political loyalties of the population but the very spiritual atmosphere of the land itself. The signs of this supernatural contamination became apparent almost immediately as they sailed past headlands where the very rocks seemed to radiate malevolent energy.

"The corruption spreads," Caswallon observed as he pointed toward shores where no birds flew and no fishing boats ventured, despite waters that should have teemed with life. "Morrigan's influence poisons everything it touches, turning fertile lands into wastelands and driving away all creatures that serve the purposes of life and growth."

The evidence of preternatural malice was indeed overwhelming as they sailed past settlement after settlement where the normal activities of human civilization had been replaced by an ominous silence that spoke of populations living in constant fear. The few figures visible along the shores moved with the furtive behaviour of people who had learned to fear not only human enemies but the very air they breathed.

"These people cry out for liberation," Lysander observed with the righteous anger that had motivated his transformation from mercenary to crusader. "Every hour we delay means additional suffering for innocent souls who have committed no crime beyond the misfortune of falling under Aodhan's dominion."

Yet the strategic necessity of their mission required them to resist the immediate impulse to begin liberation operations before reaching the point where Cian and Caswallon would disembark for their assault on Morrigan's stronghold. The coastal populations would indeed be freed, but only after the primary mission had been accomplished and the preternatural foundation of enemy power had been destroyed.

The rendezvous point that Caswallon had selected for their disembarkation lay at the mouth of a river that flowed from the highland regions where his previous reconnaissance had detected the strongest concentrations of concealment spells and spiritual malevolence. The location offered both easy access to the interior and sufficient concealment to prevent their landing from being detected by enemy observers.

"This is where my trail went cold during the previous mission," Caswallon explained as they approached the marshy coastline that offered perfect concealment for their small landing party. "The very air grows thick with supernatural influence as you penetrate deeper into the highlands, and ordinary navigation becomes impossible when the enemy's concealment spells distort both sight and direction."

The formal farewell between Cian and the naval forces that would continue to the coastal liberation operations was marked by the sort of emotional restraint that characterized professional warriors facing uncertain separation. Each man understood that success in both missions was crucial for the ultimate triumph of their cause, yet the prospect of permanent separation cast a shadow over their parting.

"May the Lord of Hosts watch over your mission," Korax said as he clasped Cian's forearm in the traditional warrior's farewell, his weathered features reflecting the mixture of pride and concern that marked a veteran's feelings toward a comrade embarking on an especially dangerous assignment. "Strike swift and true, young champion, and know that every man among us would gladly take your place if such substitution were possible."

Cian's response carried the humble confidence that had marked his character throughout his service to the divine mission. "Your prayers and the strength of your sword arms in liberating our people will serve the cause as surely as any blow I might strike," he replied with sincere gratitude. "Trust in the providence that has brought us safely through so many trials, and we shall meet again when victory has been achieved."

The landing itself was accomplished with the stealth that had made Caswallon legendary throughout the Celtic territories, as the scout's intimate knowledge of enemy patrol patterns allowed them to reach the shore without detection. The small boat that carried them from the Sea Serpent to the river mouth moved through the pre-dawn darkness like a shadow upon the water, leaving barely a ripple to mark their passage.

Once ashore, the two warriors found themselves in a landscape that seemed designed by malevolent intelligence to frustrate and confound any righteous purpose. The very ground beneath their feet felt somehow corrupted, as if the earth itself had been poisoned by prolonged exposure to eldritch evil, while the air carried an oppressive quality that made breathing an effort and clear thinking a constant struggle.

"The concealment spells begin here," Caswallon observed as he pointed toward hills that seemed to shimmer and shift whenever observed directly, creating the sort of visual distortion that made accurate navigation impossible through conventional means. "Beyond this point, we must rely upon spiritual guidance rather than earthly landmarks, for the enemy's magic seeks to lead travellers in circles until exhaustion and despair make them easy prey."

The journey into the highland regions where Morrigan's stronghold lay hidden became an ordeal that tested both their physical endurance and spiritual fortitude. For days they pressed forward through terrain that seemed to resist their every step, following trails that appeared and disappeared without warning while constantly vigilant against the preternatural enemies that haunted these accursed lands.

The undead warriors they encountered during their approach were unlike anything in Cian's previous experience, being not merely reanimated corpses but creatures that had been twisted by prolonged exposure to Morrigan's corruption into forms that retained human intelligence while serving completely malevolent purposes. These enemies fought with the skill of veteran soldiers combined with the relentless determination of creatures that felt neither pain nor fear.

"These were once warriors of our own people," Caswallon observed grimly as they stood over the remains of a patrol they had destroyed after a vicious midnight ambush. "See the remnants of Irish mail and Celtic weapons? Morrigan's corruption can transform even the bravest hearts into instruments of evil if they lack the spiritual protection that comes from faithful service to the Most High."

The banshees that haunted the upper reaches of their route presented a different but equally dangerous challenge, as these otherworldly entities sought to destroy their enemies through spiritual rather than physical assault. Their mournful wails carried curses designed to drain courage and hope from mortal hearts, while their very presence created an atmosphere of despair that could break the will of even the strongest warriors.

Yet the divine protection that surrounded their mission proved more than adequate for these preternatural challenges. Methuselah's Sword seemed to radiate a barrier of holy power that turned aside the banshees' spiritual attacks, while Cian's faith, strengthened through trials and divine revelation, provided an anchor of hope that no amount of eldritch malevolence could shake.

"The enemy grows more desperate as we approach our destination," Cian observed after they had destroyed yet another patrol of corrupted warriors who had attempted to ambush them near a cursed spring where the very water seemed to writhe with malevolent life. "Each attack reveals the increasing fear that our approach generates among the servants of darkness."

It was during the fourth day of their approach, when exhaustion and constant vigilance had begun to take their toll despite their supernatural protection, that they heard the sound that would guide them to their ultimate destination. The inhuman scream that echoed through the highland valleys carried such anguish and rage that it seemed to shake the very foundations of the earth, while its otherworldly character left no doubt regarding the identity of its source.

"Morrigan herself!" Caswallon breathed with a mixture of excitement and dread as they crouched behind a boulder outcropping, straining to determine the direction from which that terrible cry had originated. "But what could cause the Shadow Weaver to give voice to such torment? I have never heard such agony expressed by any creature, mortal or supernatural."

The answer lay in events that had unfolded earlier that very day at the great battle before Tara. Morrigan had personally led her supernatural host against the kingdom's walls, her very presence on the battlefield amplifying the malevolent power of every creature under her command. Yet when the Ark of the Covenant was brought forth and its divine power unleashed, the Shadow Weaver had felt the full force of divine judgment strike her like lightning from heaven itself.

The holy fire that had consumed her minions had also burned through her own supernatural essence, inflicting wounds that transcended the physical realm and struck at the very core of her corrupted being. In agony beyond mortal comprehension, she had fled the battlefield with what remained of her personal guard, leaving behind the smoldering ruins of her once-mighty host as she sought the sanctuary of her hidden stronghold.

Following the source of that preternatural scream led them through a maze of rocky canyons and hidden valleys where the very landscape seemed to pulse with malevolent energy. Yet now they could observe fresh signs of disturbance—broken branches, disturbed earth, and the lingering traces of otherworldly passage that marked Morrigan's desperate flight from the battlefield where divine power had proven superior to her ancient malice. The concealment spells that had frustrated Caswallon's previous reconnaissance attempts grew stronger as they approached their destination, creating illusions and false paths designed to lead intruders away from their objectives.

Yet the agony that continued to echo from Morrigan's stronghold provided an auditory beacon that no amount of visual deception could confuse. Each new scream of rage and pain guided them closer to their destination, while the increasing frequency of these outbursts suggested that the Shadow Weaver's suffering was intensifying rather than diminishing.

It was as they crested a ridge overlooking a particularly desolate valley that they witnessed the moment they had been seeking. Through the gathering dusk, they observed a procession of preternatural figures moving with the desperate urgency of beings fleeing from overwhelming defeat. At their head stalked a form that could only be Morrigan herself—tall, terrible, and writhing with the sort of malevolent energy that made the very air around her seem to burn with unholy fire.

"There!" Caswallon whispered as they watched the Shadow Weaver approach what appeared to be an unremarkable section of cliff face, her remaining servants—a motley collection of shapeshifters, corrupted spirits, and lesser demons—trailing behind her like the remnants of a shattered army. "She doth enter her stronghold even as we watch!"

They observed in fascination as Morrigan gestured toward the cliff face and a hidden entrance materialized where none had been visible before, its opening revealing the sort of architectural grandeur that spoke of otherworldly construction far beneath the earth. The Shadow Weaver disappeared into this portal with her surviving minions, and the entrance sealed itself behind them with magic so powerful that it left no trace of its existence.

"Now we know the exact location," Cian observed with grim satisfaction as they marked the position of the concealed entrance. "But we must wait for her wounds to weaken her further before attempting our own entry. It must be some divine fire that struck her at Tara hence we must be patient as it will need time to do its work before we dare face her in her own stronghold."

"There!" Caswallon pointed toward a section of cliff face that seemed no different from the surrounding stone, yet something in his scout's instincts told him that their search was finally approaching its end. "The screams emerge from behind that rocky wall, yet I can detect no obvious entrance. The concealment magic here is stronger than anything we have previously encountered."

The discovery of Morrigan's hidden entrance required the sort of spiritual perception that transcended ordinary observation. Only when Cian placed his hand upon the pommel of Methuselah's Sword and allowed its hallowed power to flow through his senses did the illusion fall away to reveal a narrow opening in the cliff face, concealed by magic so powerful that it had remained hidden from human detection for centuries.

"The sword doth reveal what mortal eyes cannot perceive," Cian observed with wonder as the hidden passage became visible through the blessed enhancement of his perception. "This entrance hath been concealed not merely by magic but by the sort of spiritual barriers that can only be penetrated by weapons blessed with celestial authority."

The passage itself descended into the earth at a steep angle, its walls carved from living rock by supernatural means that had left the stone bearing an oily sheen that spoke of prolonged exposure to corrupting influence. The very air that flowed from this opening carried the scent of sulfur and decay, while strange echoes suggested vast chambers hidden in the depths below.

"We expected a damp cave filled with bones and corruption," Caswallon murmured as they began their descent into the hidden stronghold, their weapons ready for whatever guardians might challenge their intrusion. "Yet these passages speak of construction on a scale that doth rival the greatest works of human civilization."

The reality that awaited them in the depths beneath the highland cliffs exceeded their most extravagant expectations while simultaneously filling them with dread at the evidence of power they discovered there. Instead of the crude cave system they had anticipated, Morrigan's stronghold proved to be an underground palace that rivalled the legendary halls of Mag Mell in its grandeur and preternatural magnificence.

The entrance hall into which their passage led was a vast chamber whose ceiling disappeared into shadows far above, supported by pillars of black marble that had been carved with intricate designs depicting scenes of war and slaughter throughout the ages. The floor was paved with stones that gleamed like obsidian, their polished surfaces reflecting the light from braziers that burned with flames of unnatural colours.

Yet it was the weapons and armour that lined the walls of this incredible chamber that truly captured their attention and filled them with a mixture of awe and horror. Here was displayed the accumulated martial heritage of millennia, including weapons and armour from every age and culture that had ever known war, all preserved in perfect condition yet radiating an aura of corruption that marked them as instruments of eldritch evil.

"Behold the arsenal of the Shadow Weaver," Cian breathed as they moved carefully through this museum of martial splendour, his eyes taking in displays that included everything from bronze age spears to Etruscan machaira, from Celtic long swords to weapons of Eastern origin whose very designs suggested unearthly craftsmanship. "Every weapon here hath tasted blood in the service of darkness."

The armour displays were equally impressive and equally cursed, featuring everything from ancient bronze panoply to mail hauberks that had protected forgotten champions, all bearing the marks of supernatural enhancement that had made their original wearers formidable beyond mortal limits. Yet the corruption that permeated every piece made them worse than useless—wearing such equipment would have been tantamount to inviting possession by the malevolent spirits bound within the metal.

"Touch nothing," Cian warned as they moved deeper into the palace, his spiritual senses detecting the trap that these magnificent displays represented. "Each piece doth carry curses designed to corrupt whoever dares to claim them. This entire arsenal serveth as both armoury and snare, baiting the unwary with promises of unnatural power while binding them to Morrigan's service."

The palace complex that lay beyond this entrance chamber revealed the full scope of the Shadow Weaver's domain and the malevolent resources at her disposal. Corridors stretched in every direction, their walls adorned with tapestries that depicted scenes of conquest and corruption throughout history, while chambers glimpsed through open doorways contained treasures and artifacts whose very presence spoke of power stolen from the righteous and perverted to serve evil purposes.

Yet despite the magnificence of their surroundings, an atmosphere of decay and corruption permeated everything they observed. The supernatural beauty was hollow and false, like a magnificent facade concealing rottenness at its core, while the very grandeur of the displays spoke of vanity and pride that had long since passed beyond all reasonable bounds.

It was in the chamber they designated the Hall of Ancient Arms that Caswallon encountered the temptation that would transform their mission from stealth infiltration to desperate battle for survival. Among the countless weapons displayed along the walls of this vast room, one particular spear seemed to call to him with a voice that bypassed rational thought and spoke directly to the deepest instincts of the warrior's soul.

The Luin of Celtchar—for such was the identity of the weapon that drew his attention—hung among the other spears as if it were merely another trophy of conquest, yet its unusual character was immediately apparent to anyone with the spiritual sensitivity to perceive such things. The spear point gleamed with an inner light that spoke of power barely contained, while the shaft seemed to writhe with subtle movement that suggested a living creature rather than carved wood.

"Caswallon!" Cian called sharply as he noticed his companion's attention becoming fixed upon the cursed weapon with an intensity that suggested mystical compulsion. "Remember our mission! Do not allow thyself to be distracted by these temptations, no matter how appealing they may seem!"

Yet even as Cian spoke his warning, he could see that Caswallon's resistance to the spear's mystical allure was weakening with each passing moment. The weapon seemed to pulse with increasing intensity as it sensed the proximity of a warrior worthy of its terrible gifts, while its influence over the Celtic scout grew stronger despite his efforts to resist its call.

"I... I can feel its hunger," Caswallon murmured, his voice carrying a dreamy quality that spoke of mental faculties clouded by supernatural influence. "It doth thirst for blood, and it promiseth such power to whoever proves worthy of its service. With such a weapon, no enemy could stand against us, no mission would be beyond our capabilities."

The subtle movement of Caswallon's hand toward the spear shaft filled Cian with desperate urgency as he recognized the mortal danger his companion faced. The Luin of Celtchar was no ordinary cursed weapon but one of the most dangerous artifacts in Celtic legend—a spear that granted supernatural power to its wielder while slowly consuming their life force until they died in the moment of their greatest triumph.

"Fight its influence!" Cian commanded, placing his hand on Caswallon's shoulder in an attempt to break the mystic compulsion that was overwhelming his companion's judgment. "This is a trap designed to claim the strongest warriors and bind them to Morrigan's service! Remember thy oath of loyalty to King Eochaidh and thy duty to our sacred mission!"

But the warning came too late. Even as Cian spoke, Caswallon's hand closed around the spear shaft with a grip that seemed to weld flesh to wood through preternatural bonding. The moment of contact triggered a transformation that was both terrible and magnificent to behold, as the cursed weapon's power flowed into its new wielder while simultaneously setting in motion the process that would ultimately claim his life.

The alarm that sounded throughout Morrigan's palace was unlike any warning bell or horn that mortal ears had ever heard. Instead, it was a preternatural shriek that seemed to emerge from the very stones of the structure, alerting every guardian and servant within the underground complex that intruders had penetrated to the heart of their mistress's domain and dared to claim one of her most prized possessions.

The response to this alarm was immediate and overwhelming as creatures that had been spawned in the deepest pits of eldritch corruption poured into the Hall of Ancient Arms from every entrance and opening. Dozens upon dozens of corrupted human soldiers, their armour blackened by prolonged exposure to evil influence and their weapons dripping with preternatural poison, charged toward the two intruders with the mindless fury of creatures whose only purpose was destruction.

Shape-shifters flowed through the chamber entrances like liquid nightmare, their forms constantly shifting between human and beast, between solid flesh and writhing shadow, as they sought configurations that would prove most effective against the particular enemies they faced. These creatures possessed not only preternatural speed and strength but also the cunning intelligence that made them far more dangerous than mere beasts.

Werefolk bounded into the fray with lupine grace, their hybrid forms combining the worst aspects of man and wolf into instruments of otherworldly savagery. These creatures retained human intelligence while possessing the enhanced senses and predatory instincts of their animal aspects, making them particularly dangerous opponents who could coordinate pack tactics while wielding weapons with human skill.

The ghoulish undead that accompanied these more conventional enemies represented the deepest corruption of Morrigan's dark arts, being creatures that had once been human but had been transformed through necromantic ritual into instruments of pure malevolence. These beings retained their human intelligence while possessing preternatural resilience that made them nearly impossible to destroy through conventional means.

Yet the moment the battle was joined, it became apparent that Morrigan's servants faced opponents whose celestial empowerment far exceeded anything they had previously encountered. Methuselah's Sword in Cian's hands became an instrument of sacred destruction that carved through enemy ranks like lightning striking through darkness, while each stroke seemed to multiply its wielder's strength and speed beyond mortal limitations.

The Luin of Celtchar proved equally devastating in Caswallon's grasp, though its power came with a terrible price that was already beginning to manifest itself. The cursed spear writhed in his hands like a living serpent until it tasted blood, and once that first drop of enemy vitality touched its eldritch blade, it became an instrument of destruction that could strike down enemies from impossible distances while seeking targets with the accuracy of heavenly judgment.

"Together!" Cian called out as they stood back-to-back in the centre of the vast chamber, surrounded by enemies whose numbers seemed to multiply even as their comrades fell to divine and cursed weapons alike. "Let them see what the power of righteousness can accomplish when paired with the fury of those who serve just causes!"

The battle that followed would be remembered in song and story for as long as Celtic bards drew breath to celebrate the deeds of heroes, as two warriors armed with weapons of legendary power carved their way through enemies whose preternatural nature should have made them invincible against mortal opposition. Yet sacred authority and cursed magic proved equally effective against the servants of darkness, creating a symphony of destruction that echoed through every corridor of Morrigan's palace.

Cian moved through the enemy ranks like an avatar of celestial wrath, Methuselah's Sword seeming to anticipate every enemy attack while guiding his strikes toward vital points with accuracy that transcended mortal skill. Each blow from the patriarch's blade not only destroyed its immediate target but sent waves of holy power through the surrounding enemies, weakening their preternatural resilience and making them vulnerable to subsequent attacks.

Caswallon's fighting style with the Luin of Celtchar was entirely different but equally devastating, as the cursed spear's ability to strike at distance allowed him to eliminate enemies before they could close to melee range. The weapon moved in his hands with serpentine grace, its point finding throats and hearts with unerring accuracy while its otherworldly hunger drove it to seek ever more challenging targets.

Yet even as they dealt destruction among the enemy ranks with unprecedented efficiency, both warriors could sense that they faced only the outer guardians of Morrigan's domain. The corrupted soldiers and shape-shifters they destroyed were formidable opponents, but they lacked the sort of eldritch power that would be required to protect the Shadow Weaver herself from enemies who had penetrated so deeply into her stronghold.

"These are merely the foot soldiers," Caswallon observed during a brief lull in the fighting as they stood among the scattered remains of enemies whose corruption had been purged by sacred and cursed blade alike. "Where are the champions whose preternatural power would be required to guard approaches to Morrigan's throne? Why do we encounter only lesser servants when greater guardians should bar our path?"

The answer to his question became apparent as they pressed deeper into the palace complex, following corridors that led inevitably toward the central chambers where the Shadow Weaver herself maintained her court. The absence of greater guardians in the outer regions was not a sign of weakness but rather evidence of strategic concentration—all of Morrigan's most powerful servants had been gathered in the inner sanctum to provide a final line of defence against any force that might penetrate so far into her domain.

The approach to this inner sanctum led through chambers whose mystical grandeur exceeded even the magnificent displays they had encountered in the outer palace. Here were throne rooms whose black marble floors reflected scenes of conquest and corruption throughout history, while the walls bore frescoes that depicted Morrigan herself in various manifestations of her power as goddess of war and strife.
Yet underlying this unearthly magnificence was an atmosphere of fear and desperation that spoke of a domain whose foundations were being shaken by forces beyond its mistress's ability to control. The distant screams of agony that continued to echo through the palace corridors revealed that Morrigan's suffering had not diminished despite the passage of time, while the very stones of her stronghold seemed to tremble with each new cry of otherworldly anguish.

It was as they approached the massive doorway that marked the entrance to Morrigan's throne chamber that they encountered the first guardians whose power truly matched the importance of what they protected. The gateway itself was a marvel of arcane craftsmanship, standing nearly twenty feet in height and carved from single blocks of obsidian that had been polished to mirror brilliance, while its surface bore inscriptions in languages that predated human civilization.

The two giants who stood guard before this portal were beings whose mystical nature was immediately apparent to any observer. Standing fifteen feet in height and clad in armour that had been forged from metals unknown to mortal smiths, they possessed not only the physical strength characteristic of their kind but also the sort of eldritch resilience that made them nearly invulnerable to conventional weapons.

"Balor's kindred," Caswallon breathed as he observed the red eyes that glowed with malevolent intelligence behind the visors of their unearthly helms. "These are true Nephilim, offspring of fallen angels whose power hath been enhanced by centuries of service to the Shadow Weaver. No mortal weapon can prevail against such enemies."

Yet even as he spoke these words of warning, both warriors understood that retreat was not an option. Their mission required them to reach Morrigan herself, and no guardian, however formidable, could be allowed to prevent the completion of their divine commission. The weapons they carried had been specifically chosen for their ability to contend with eldritch enemies, and the moment of testing had finally arrived.

The battle that erupted before the obsidian gateway exceeded in fury and preternatural violence anything either warrior had previously experienced. The giants fought with a combination of otherworldly strength and millennia of combat experience that made them opponents worthy of the legendary weapons arrayed against them, while their armour proved resistant to all but the most perfectly placed strikes.

The first giant attacked with a massive sword that had been forged from meteoric iron and blessed with curses that could cleave through any armour known to mortal smiths. Each swing of this terrible weapon created winds that knocked smaller enemies from their feet, while its eldritch edge left burning scars in the stone wherever it struck the palace walls.

Yet Methuselah's Sword proved more than equal to this preternatural challenge, as its hallowed power allowed it to turn aside blows that should have shattered any earthly blade while responding with strikes that penetrated even eldritch armour. Cian moved with speed and precision that seemed to transcend mortal limitations, his every action guided by the sword's celestial intelligence as it sought weak points in his opponent's seemingly impregnable defences.

The second giant wielded a war hammer whose head had been carved from a single massive gemstone that pulsed with malevolent energy. This weapon could shatter castle walls with a single blow while sending forth waves of mystical force that stunned enemies and disrupted their ability to coordinate effective resistance.

Against this formidable opponent, Caswallon discovered the true extent of the Luin of Celtchar's cursed power. The spear seemed to anticipate every attack while guiding its wielder's movements with preternatural precision, allowing him to avoid blows that would have crushed any mortal warrior while responding with strikes that found the tiny gaps between armour plates where even otherworldly protection could be penetrated.

The culmination of this epic battle came when both giants, wounded and desperate, attempted a coordinated assault that should have overwhelmed any mortal opposition. Yet the sacred and cursed weapons they faced had been forged specifically for battles against such preternatural enemies, and their power proved more than adequate for the ultimate test.

Cian's final strike with Methuselah's Sword pierced the first giant's armour at the precise point where sacred power could overcome preternatural protection, while the blade's holy fire consumed the creature's corrupt essence from within. The giant's death cry echoed through the palace corridors like thunder, while his massive form crumbled to dust that was scattered by winds that seemed to emerge from the realm of the blessed.

Caswallon's victory over the second giant demonstrated the terrible effectiveness of the Luin of Celtchar when wielded by a warrior worthy of its power. The cursed spear struck from impossible angles with preternatural speed, finding weak points that should have been protected by the giant's armour and otherworldly resilience. Yet with each successful strike, the weapon's curse claimed a portion of its wielder's life force, hastening the inevitable price that would eventually be demanded for its service.

The throne chamber that lay beyond the obsidian gateway surpassed in arcane grandeur anything they had previously encountered in Morrigan's palace. The vast space stretched away in all directions, its ceiling lost in shadows that seemed to writhe with malevolent life, while the walls bore bas-reliefs that depicted the Shadow Weaver's victories throughout the ages.

At the centre of this incredible chamber stood Morrigan's throne—a seat of power carved from a single massive black diamond that reflected no light yet seemed to pulse with inner darkness. The throne was elevated upon a dais of polished obsidian that was surrounded by smaller seats where the Shadow Weaver's most trusted servants would normally hold court, though these positions were currently occupied by the remaining guardians who formed her final line of defence.

Yet it was Morrigan herself who commanded the attention of all who beheld her, for the Shadow Weaver's appearance revealed the full extent of the damage that had been inflicted upon her preternatural essence by the Ark of the Covenant's distant assault. The goddess who had once radiated power and malevolent beauty now showed signs of weakness and vulnerability that spoke of heavenly judgment working its inexorable will.
Her raven-black hair, once lustrous as polished jet, now showed streaks of silver that spoke of otherworldly aging brought on by spiritual trauma. Her perfect features bore lines of pain and exhaustion that no amount of magical enhancement could completely conceal, while her eyes—once burning with malevolent intelligence—now carried a haunted quality that revealed the depth of her suffering.

When Cian's gaze fell upon this figure, his world momentarily ceased to exist around him as recognition struck with the force of divine revelation. The face before him was one that had haunted his dreams for over seventeen years, one that had been burned into his memory during the most traumatic moment of his youth. This was the creature who had emerged from the shadows of his family's modest dwelling in the hills of Leinster, who had spoken words of power that had rendered his entire family helpless, who had taken his beloved sister Niamh away into the darkness tormented by intense grief and uttermost guilt at his inability to prevent the abduction.

The righteous fury that filled Cian's heart in that moment transcended mere human anger, becoming something approaching the wrath of divine justice itself. Every fibre of his being called out for vengeance against this creature who had destroyed his family's peace and stolen away the sister he had loved more dearly than his own life. The years of wondering about Niamh's fate, the countless nights spent imagining the horrors she might have endured, the guilt of having failed to protect her—all of these emotions crystallized into a burning desire to extract payment for the suffering this sorceress had caused.

"Thou," he breathed, his voice carrying the weight of seventeen years of grief and anger. "I know thy face, daughter of darkness. Thou art the one who stole my sister from our home when she was but a child of five summers."

The diminished host of minions that surrounded her throne included the surviving shapeshifters whose forms flickered constantly between configurations as they sought defensive arrangements that might prove effective against the weapons they faced. Banshees circled overhead with mournful cries that carried curses designed to weaken their enemies' resolve, while corrupted spirits that had never possessed human form writhed through the air like living smoke.

Yet despite their reduced numbers and their mistress's obvious weakness, these remaining guardians possessed the sort of desperate courage that made them formidable opponents. They understood that failure meant not merely death but the complete destruction of everything they had served throughout their preternatural existence, and this knowledge drove them to fight with a fury that transcended ordinary desperation.

The battle that erupted in the throne chamber showcased the perfect coordination that had developed between Cian and Caswallon throughout their dangerous mission. The two warriors moved like dancers performing a deadly ballet, their weapons complementing each other's capabilities while their tactical awareness allowed them to exploit every advantage their divine and cursed arms provided.

Cian's approach emphasized the sort of direct assault that maximized Methuselah's Sword's divine power, as he carved through enemy ranks with strikes that seemed to burn away corruption wherever they touched. The sword's holy fire proved particularly effective against shapeshifters, whose otherworldly flexibility became a liability when faced with divine power that could destroy them regardless of what configuration they assumed.

Caswallon's fighting style took full advantage of the Luin of Celtchar's ability to strike at distance, as he eliminated banshees and corrupted spirits before they could close to effective range. The cursed spear seemed to seek targets independently, its point finding vital spots with accuracy that suggested mystical intelligence guiding every thrust and cut.

Yet even as they carved their way through the remaining guardians with devastating efficiency, both warriors could sense that their ultimate challenge was only beginning. The creatures they destroyed had been formidable opponents, but they paled in comparison to the power that radiated from Morrigan herself, even in her weakened condition.

It was as they completed the destruction of her minions that the sound they had been dreading echoed through the throne chamber—the thunderous march of countless feet accompanied by the unearthly howling that announced the approach of Morrigan's ultimate reserve. An innumerable host of undead warriors, preserved by necromantic arts from every age and culture that had known war, poured through the chamber entrances in a tide of eldritch malevolence.

These were not the relatively weak undead they had encountered in the outer palace, but elite warriors who had been granted preternatural resilience and enhanced combat abilities through centuries of service to the Shadow Weaver. Each possessed the martial skills they had developed in life, enhanced by otherworldly strength and freed from the limitations of pain or fear that constrained mortal fighters.

"Go!" Caswallon commanded as he positioned himself between Cian and the approaching horde, the Luin of Celtchar writhing in his hands like a serpent eager to taste the blood of worthy opponents. "Complete our mission while I hold them back! Morrigan's weakness is our opportunity, but only if thou dost strike before she can regenerate her full power!"

The sacrifice that Caswallon offered was both necessary and heroic, as his legendary speed and the cursed spear's arcane effectiveness made him uniquely qualified to delay the undead host long enough for Cian to complete their primary objective. Yet both warriors understood that this separation likely meant permanent farewell, as no mortal fighter could indefinitely withstand the assault of innumerable preternatural enemies.

Cian's advance toward Morrigan's throne was accompanied by the sort of celestial presence that seemed to make the very air shimmer with holy power. Methuselah's Sword blazed with inner fire as it sensed the approaching confrontation with evil that had opposed sacred purposes since the dawn of time, while its wielder felt strength flowing into his limbs that transcended any merely human capability.

Yet it was Morrigan's transformation that truly revealed the magnitude of the battle that was about to unfold. The Shadow Weaver's reaction to being directly challenged by a mortal warrior armed with a weapon of divine authority triggered a metamorphosis that displayed the full extent of her otherworldly nature for the first time since their arrival in her stronghold.

"Marú? DEATH?" she shrieked with laughter that carried the sound of clashing swords and dying screams, her voice echoing with the weight of millennia. "Thou dost dare speak of slaying ME? An Mhórrígan, bandia an chogaidh! I who was ancient when thy ancestors crawled from primordial mud! I who have drunk the blood of kings and feasted upon the screams of the dying since time immemorial!"

Her voice rose to a crescendo that seemed to shake the very foundations of reality. "Morthrán beag! Thou art but a mewling whelp, a brief flicker of flesh that shall be snuffed out ere the next moon wanes! Think ye to vanquish HER who is war incarnate, strife given form, the eternal hunger for battle that burns in every warrior's heart? Ní féidir leat mé a mharú! You cannot destroy that which IS destruction itself!"

The transformation that followed exceeded anything in Celtic legend or mystical lore, as Morrigan assumed her true form as the goddess of war and strife. Her humanoid appearance dissolved like smoke to reveal a being of eldritch power whose very presence seemed to distort reality around her. Six additional arms sprouted from her sides, each grasping a different weapon that had been forged in the arcane fires of her domain.

In her eight hands, Morrigan wielded a sword that dripped with the blood of kings, a spear that had never missed its target, a mace that could shatter mountains, a war hammer that could level cities, a bow whose arrows never failed to find their mark, an axe that could cleave through any armour, a whip that could tear the soul from its victim's body, and a dagger that could kill with the slightest scratch.

Her transformed appearance was both magnificent and terrible to behold, as divine power corrupted into preternatural malevolence created a presence that seemed to fill the entire throne chamber with malevolent energy. Her eight weapons moved in perfect coordination, creating a web of deadly blade that should have been impossible for any mortal opponent to penetrate or survive.

The battle that followed would be remembered as one of the greatest single combats in the history of mystical warfare, as a mortal warrior armed with divine authority faced a goddess whose power had been accumulated through millennia of conflict and corruption. The very foundations of Morrigan's palace trembled with each clash of their weapons, while the unearthly energies they unleashed created phenomena that transcended ordinary understanding.

Morrigan's assault was a symphony of destruction that utilized every advantage her otherworldly nature provided. Her eight weapons attacked from all angles simultaneously, creating patterns of death that should have overwhelmed any mortal defence, while her goddess-enhanced speed and strength turned each strike into a potential killing blow.

Yet Methuselah's Sword proved equal to this ultimate challenge, as its divine power allowed Cian to anticipate attacks that came faster than human perception could follow while responding with counters that gradually wore away at his opponent's seemingly impregnable defences. The sword's holy fire burned away corruption wherever it touched, while its divine intelligence guided its wielder's movements with precision that matched otherworldly perception.

The turning point in this epic battle came when Cian, driven by desperation and guided by divine inspiration, managed to penetrate Morrigan's defence with a strike that severed one of her eight arms. The wound caused by Methuselah's Sword evoked recent pains of injury the Shadow Weaver sustained when she tasted the power of the Ark of the Covenant, as the divine power bound within the blade began to consume her arcane essence from the point of contact.

The cleansing holy light that spread from the sword wound through her severed limb filled Morrigan with a terror she had not experienced since her original corruption in the ancient days before recorded history. The sacred fire that burned through her preternatural form was not merely destroying her physical manifestation but threatening to purge the very essence of evil that defined her existence.

"Ní féidir é seo a bheith fíor! This cannot be!" she screamed as the holy corruption spread from her wounded arm toward her heart and core of power, her voice cracking with disbelief and ancient rage. "Níl aon arm daonna in ann an bunús síoraí cogaidh féin a scrios! No mortal weapon can destroy the eternal essence of war itself! Mise cogadh ina chruth daonna! I am conflict incarnate, strife given form, agus fad is a bhfuil fios ag daoine ar fhearg agus ar fhuath, and as long as beings know anger and hatred, ní féidir mé a scrios! I cannot be destroyed!"

Yet even as she spoke these words of defiance, Morrigan could feel the inexorable advance of heavenly judgment through her preternatural form. The wound inflicted by Methuselah's Sword continued to spread despite every effort she made to contain it, while the holy fire consumed corruption that had been accumulating for millennia.

Cian's final assault came as Morrigan's attention was focused on the spreading sacred fire that threatened her very existence. With all the skill he had developed through years of training and celestial empowerment, with every ounce of strength and speed that otherworldly blessing could provide, he struck with Methuselah's Sword at the precise moment when his opponent's guard was compromised by panic and pain.

The blow that ended the battle—and Morrigan's millennia of malevolent existence—was perfectly placed to penetrate the Shadow Weaver's preternatural defences and reach the core of darkness that sustained her immortal evil. The hallowed power of the patriarch's blade consumed this essential corruption like fire devouring dry timber, while holy light blazed forth from the wound with intensity that illuminated every corner of the vast throne chamber.

As Morrigan lay dying, her otherworldly essence unravelling under the assault of celestial power, Cian knelt beside her failing form with grim satisfaction. The moment had come for her to understand the full significance of this judgment, to know exactly whose hand had brought about her destruction.

"Look upon me well, Shadow Weaver," he said, his voice carrying the authority of one who speaks for sacred justice. "See the face of the one thou didst fail to destroy. Seventeen years ago, thou didst take my sister Niamh from our home, believing thou hadst claimed the last of the mac Morna bloodline. But thy corruption blinded thee to the truth. I am Cian mac Morna, son of Cumhall, inheritor of the legacy thou didst seek to destroy. The boy thou didst leave for dead hath grown to become the instrument of thy doom."

Recognition flickered in Morrigan's dying eyes as she understood the terrible irony of her defeat. The very bloodline she had tried to extinguish had produced the warrior who now stood over her vanquished form, while the sister she had forcibly acquired would be avenged by the brother she had failed to kill.

"In the name of the Most High," Cian pronounced with authority that seemed to echo from realms beyond mortal perception, "and by the power of righteousness that cannot be conquered by any evil, I consign thee to the Abyss from whence thou dost belong! Let the darkness that spawned thee claim what corruption hath wrought, and let the light of sacred justice purge the earth of thy malevolent influence!"

The destruction of Morrigan created a preternatural explosion that sent waves of celestial power throughout her entire domain, purging corruption that had accumulated over centuries while dismantling the magical foundations that had sustained her underground palace. The very stones of the structure began to dissolve as the evil that had shaped them was consumed by holy fire, while dimensional rifts opened to claim the remains of the Shadow Weaver's essence.

The innumerable host of undead warriors that had been pressing their attack against Caswallon's increasingly desperate defence simply dissolved into dust and shadow as the necromantic power that sustained them was consumed by the same holy light that had destroyed their mistress. Weapons and armour that had been enhanced by eldritch corruption crumbled to rust and decay, while the cursed treasures accumulated over millennia lost their malevolent power and became mere reminders of vanquished evil.

Yet even as victory was achieved, the price of their triumph became apparent. The destruction of Morrigan's palace created a temporal vortex that sought to drag everything within her domain into the dimensional rifts that were claiming the Shadow Weaver's essence. The entire structure began to sink toward the Abyss, following its mistress into the realm of eternal punishment where her evil would be contained forever.

"Cian!" Caswallon called out as he realized the magnitude of their peril, his voice barely audible over the otherworldly winds that howled through the collapsing palace. "The entire stronghold doth follow its mistress into destruction! We must escape immediately or be dragged down with the corruption we came to destroy!"

The race for survival that followed tested every ounce of speed and endurance the two warriors possessed, as they fought their way through corridors that were dissolving around them while dimensional forces sought to claim them along with everything else in Morrigan's accursed domain. The cursed spear that had served Caswallon so well throughout their mission now became a burden that slowed his movement, yet he could not release it without losing the eldritch enhancement that might prove necessary for their escape.

The journey to the surface became a nightmare of collapsing passages and disappearing staircases, as the very architecture of Morrigan's palace unmade itself in response to her destruction. Walls became insubstantial while floors opened into chasms that led directly to dimensional rifts, forcing the fleeing warriors to find new routes through a constantly changing maze of preternatural destruction.

Yet the hallowed providence that had protected their mission throughout its dangerous course did not abandon them in their moment of greatest peril. Passages that should have collapsed remained stable just long enough for them to pass, while routes that had been blocked by rubble somehow cleared at the moment when alternative paths seemed impossible to find.

They emerged from the hidden entrance just as the highland cliffs began to sink into chasms that opened to swallow everything associated with Morrigan's corruption. The very landscape that had been poisoned by her presence for centuries was being cleansed by celestial power, while fresh winds carried away the eldritch miasma that had made the region uninhabitable for righteous purposes.

As they stood gasping for breath on solid ground that trembled with the final convulsions of preternatural destruction, both warriors understood that they had witnessed something unprecedented in the history of warfare between good and evil. The Shadow Weaver, whose power had seemed eternal and whose influence had corrupted entire civilizations, had been completely destroyed by heavenly judgment working through mortal instruments.

"It is finished," Cian breathed as he watched the last remnants of Morrigan's domain disappear into dimensional rifts that sealed themselves against any possibility of the corruption ever returning. "The source of eldritch evil in these lands hath been purged forever, and the victory we have achieved here will echo through the ages."

Yet even as they celebrated their triumph, both warriors could feel the eldritch energies that had been unleashed by Morrigan's destruction creating resonances throughout the spiritual realms. Somewhere in the distance, a sound like the roar of a mighty dragon echoed across the highlands—the very signal that Jeremiah had promised would mark the completion of their mission and the beginning of the final assault against all remaining enemies of righteousness.

The mission was complete, the Shadow Weaver was destroyed, and the forces of darkness that had threatened to overwhelm the kingdom of light had lost their preternatural foundation. The price had been terrible—Caswallon's life was slowly being consumed by the cursed spear he could never release—but the victory they had achieved would ensure that future generations could live free from the sort of eldritch malevolence that had plagued the world since the dawn of human civilization.

As they began their journey back toward the coast where their allies waited for the signal to advance, both warriors understood that they had participated in something that would be remembered long after their own names were forgotten. The hunt for the Shadow Weaver had ended in complete victory, and the age of sacred justice in the Celtic lands had begun.

The dragon's roar that continued to echo across the highlands was more than a signal for military action—it was the voice of a new age being born, an age when the children of light would no longer cower before the servants of darkness, but would stand forth as champions of heavenly purposes that could not be defeated by any power that hell itself might devise against them.
 
Chapter 22: The Crimson Fury

As compiled by Baruch ben Neriah

The Fury Unleashed! In Which the Ban is Broken and the Hunter becomes the Hunted

The roar that shattered the highland silence was not merely a sound but a manifestation of primal rage that had been building for millennia beneath the ancient prohibition that bound all dragonkind to neutrality in mortal affairs. Deep within the caverns that honeycombed the mountains surrounding Aodhan's fortress, Scáthgheal the Crimson Fury—most terrible of the remaining Celtic dragons—felt the moment of Morrigan's destruction like a physical blow to his immortal essence.

The Shadow Weaver had proven far more than a fleeting ally unto the mighty wyrm; nay, she stood as the cunning artificer of his deliverance from that abysmal torment which had shadowed his essence since the dawn of his ancient being, weaving spells of shadow and subtlety to shatter the chains of his enduring agony. For within the colossal cranium of Scáthgheal lingered a grievous wound, mortal in its infliction yet immortal in its curse—a gash no elixir of restoration nor incantation of mending could ever seal, dealt by the bold blades of mortal champions in epochs long faded into the mists of legend, which had kindled in his primordial soul a seething abhorrence for all humankind, a rancour that surged through his veins like molten fury, blazing with an intensity fiercer than the infernal tempests of his own scorching exhalations.
It was Morrigan who had offered him purpose beyond mere vengeance, who had shown him how his hatred could serve a greater darkness while providing him with the means to reclaim something he had thought forever lost—a sense of belonging, and the hope of kinship in a world that had cast him out. The Shadow Weaver's fortress was more than a domain to guard; it had become the last sanctuary for his wounded spirit, a place where, under Morrigan's care, the curse of madness and deathless pain that had haunted him since time immemorial was held at bay. Without her, Scáthgheal knew that torment would return, unrelenting and absolute, and he would be condemned to wander eternity in agony with no hope of solace. Thus, when Morrigan fell, his grief became a force so desperate and consuming that it shattered the ancient ban: to avenge the one who had soothed his suffering, and to defy the fate of endless torment that now awaited him.

The ancient ban that prevented dragons from taking direct action in mortal conflicts had been established in ancient times by heavenly command and still held sway over all lands, a heavenly prohibition woven into the very fabric of reality to prevent the great wyrms from destroying entire kingdoms in their wrath. Yet the pain that tore through Scáthgheal's being as he felt Morrigan's essence dispersed into the Abyss was sufficient to shatter the mystical restraints that had held his fury in check for so long.

"FAITHLESS MORTALS!" bellowed the wyrm in a voice that reverberated like the cataclysmic rumble of fracturing earth through the shadowed labyrinths of the mountain's bowels, as his colossal, sinuous bulk unfurled with malevolent grace from the gilded sanctum of treasures wherein he had upheld his eternal watch through the relentless march of forgotten ages. "YE HAVE ANNIHILATED THE SOLE ENTITY WHO COMPREHENDED THE ABYSSAL MALICE THAT SEETHES AND CONSUMES WITHIN THESE PRIMORDIAL BOSOMS, A VENOMOUS WRATH THAT SHALL NOW ENGULF YOUR FRAIL REALMS IN UNQUENCHABLE TORMENT AND EVERLASTING DESOLATION!"

The Crimson Fury's emergence from his subterranean domain was marked by geological convulsions that sent avalanches cascading down the mountainsides while the very earth trembled beneath the weight of his terrible purpose. Scáthgheal's scales gleamed like burnished copper in the morning light, each one bearing the scars of countless battles fought across the centuries, while his eyes burned with the crimson fire that had given him his epithet.

His wings, spanning nearly two hundred feet from tip to tip, cast shadows like storm clouds as he launched himself into the highland skies with the single-minded determination of one who has finally found an outlet for grief too long contained.
