●     Morrigan, the Shadow Weaver: A powerful, malevolent druidess who champions the old pagan gods and is the primary supernatural antagonist.  
●     Mur Ollamain: (Gaelic for "University of the Prophets"). The center of learning established by Jeremiah at Tara to teach the Law of God and other arts.  
●     Nephilim: Giants mentioned in Genesis 6:4, believed by some to be the offspring of divine beings and human women. The pirate captain in the narrative is identified as a surviving Remnant.  
●     Ollam Fodhla: (Gaelic for "Great Prophet" or "Lawgiver"). The name and title given to Jeremiah in Ireland.  
●     Pharez and Zerah: The twin sons of Judah. Their descendants represent the two royal lines of the tribe of Judah. The kings of Jerusalem descended from Pharez, while the Milesian kings are linked to Zerah. The "healing of the breach" refers to their reunion.  
●     Red Hand of Ulster: An ancient symbol in Irish heraldry, particularly Ulster, believed to be linked to the scarlet thread tied around Zerah's hand at birth.  
●     Scota: The younger daughter of King Zedekiah. She marries Gathelus and settles in Iberia (Spain), giving her name to the Scotti people (the Scots).  
●     Scotti: The ancient name for the Irish, and later the Scottish people, derived from Scota.  
●     Sidhe: The fairy folk of Irish mythology. In the narrative, a corrupted faction serves Morrigan and Aodhán.  
●     Stone of Scone: The coronation stone of Scottish and later British monarchs, identified with the Lia Fáil/Jacob's Pillow.  
●     Tahpanhes: A fortified city in Egypt where Jeremiah and the princesses sought refuge.  
●     Tara: The ancient spiritual and political capital of Ireland, the seat of the High Kings, and the site where the Davidic throne is re-established.  
●     Tea Tephi: The elder daughter of King Zedekiah of Judah. As the "tender twig" of prophecy, she carries the Davidic bloodline to Ireland and becomes its queen.  
●     Torah: The Hebrew name for the Law of Moses, the first five books of the Bible, which Jeremiah establishes as the law of the new kingdom in Ireland.  
●     Tuatha Dé Danann: (Gaelic for "People of the Goddess Danu"). The mythological race of gods and heroes who ruled Ireland before the coming of the Milesians.  
●     Zerahite Lineage: The descendants of Zerah, son of Judah, who migrated early from the Holy Land to establish kingdoms in Greece, Asia Minor, and eventually Iberia and Ireland (Milesians).  
Appendix G: The Ten Tribes of Israel: Modern National Identities  
A Note on Interpretation  
The identification of modern nations with the ancient Ten Tribes of Israel is a core tenet of British Israelism and related theological theories. This interpretation is based on biblical prophecies of Israel's scattering, their eventual migration "northwest" from Assyrian captivity, and specific blessings promised to the descendants of Jacob (Israel). While widely debated by mainstream academia, this framework provides a significant foundation for the narrative of The Stone and the Scepter.  
1. The Scattering of Israel  
The northern ten tribes of Israel were taken into Assyrian captivity in two main phases, around 733 B.C. and 722 B.C. (2 Kings 15, 17). They were resettled in what is now northern Iraq and Iran. Over centuries, these tribes were "lost" to conventional history, seemingly disappearing from the Middle East. However, prophecies indicate they would migrate northwest from their captivity.  
2. Joseph: Ephraim and Manasseh  
The birthright promises of national greatness were given to Joseph's sons, Ephraim and Manasseh (Genesis 48-49; 1 Chronicles 5:1-2).  
●     Ephraim: Prophesied to become a "multitude of nations" or a "company of nations" (Genesis 35:11; 48:19). In this interpretation, Ephraim is identified with Great Britain and the British-descended nations of the Commonwealth (Canada, Australia, New Zealand, etc.). The British Empire, which ruled over a quarter of the Earth's landmass and a third of its population, is seen as the fulfilment of this prophecy. The phrase "his hand also in the sea" (Psalm 89:25) is often applied to Britain's naval dominance.  
●     Manasseh: Prophesied to become a "great single nation" (Genesis 48:19). Manasseh is identified with the United States of America.  
3. Judah  
The tribe of Judah, from whom the Davidic royal line descends, is identified with the Jewish people scattered across the globe. The narrative of The Stone and the Scepter highlights how the royal line of Judah (through Tea Tephi) was transplanted to the Ephraimite nation of Ireland, and eventually to the British monarchy, fulfilling the prophecy that Judah would retain the "scepter" (kingship) until the Messiah's return (Genesis 49:10).  
4. Dan  
The tribe of Dan was known as seafarers (Judges 5:17). They are identified with the Danaans of Greece and later with the Irish people (Hiberni), particularly the common populace, who were settled in Ireland before the Milesian (Zerahite) arrival. The name "Ireland" itself (Eire-land, Erin) is sometimes linked to Dan.  
5. Other Tribes (Briefly)  
While not central to the narrative, other tribes are also identified:  
●     Reuben: Often linked to France.  
●     Simeon and Levi: Largely dispersed among other tribes, particularly Judah.  
●     Benjamin: Many remained with Judah, but some are traced to Norway and Iceland.  
●     Gad: Linked to Switzerland and Sweden.  
●     Asher: Linked to Belgium and Holland.  
●     Naphtali: Linked to Sweden and Finland.  
●     Zebulun: Linked to Holland.  
●     Issachar: Linked to Finland.  
●     Aser (Issachar/Asher/Gad): The Norse Aesir, from whom Odin descended, are sometimes linked to these tribes, particularly in the Caucasus region.  
This framework suggests that the British monarchy, being a fusion of the Davidic line (Judah) and ruling over Ephraim, represents a unique fulfillment of prophecy, where the scepter tribe governs the birthright tribe.  
Appendix H: Brutus the Trojan and the Covenant Land  
A Note on Early British History  
The early history of Britain, particularly before the Roman conquest, is shrouded in myth and oral tradition. Geoffrey of Monmouth's History of the Kings of Britain (c. 1139) is a key source for the legend of Brutus, though largely dismissed by modern historians as historical fact. However, within the context of our narrative, these legends take on new significance as echoes of a deeper, divinely orchestrated history.  
1. The Legend of Brutus  
Brutus (or Brwt in Celtic), a legendary figure, is traditionally considered the first king of Britain, from whom the island is said to have derived its name. According to the legend, Brutus was a descendant of Aeneas, the Trojan hero who fled the destruction of Troy and founded the Roman lineage. This places Brutus firmly within the Zerahite royal line (as the Trojan royal house descended from Zerah, son of Judah).  
The story recounts that Brutus, after accidentally killing his father, was exiled from Italy. He wandered through Gaul (France), gathering other dispersed Trojans, and eventually arrived in Britain (then called Albion) around 1100 B.C., in the time of the prophet Samuel. A dream foretold his destiny: to find an island beyond Gaul, once occupied by giants, that would become a "second Troy" and from which "a race of kings will be born... and the round circle of the whole earth will be subject to them."  
2. Brutus's Arrival and Legacy in Britain  
Tradition states that Brutus and his entourage landed in Devon, southwest England, at Totnes. The "Brutus Stone" in Fore Street, Totnes, still marks the spot where he supposedly stepped ashore. He is credited with founding London, initially named Trinovantum or New Troy. The Trinovantes, an ancient Celtic tribe near London, may have derived their name from his capital.  
3. The Name "Britain" and the "Covenant People"  
While modern etymology links "Britons" to the Brythonic Celts, the narrative suggests a deeper, Hebrew connection. The word "Brython" or "Brwth-ayn" is similar to "Brwt" (Brutus). In Hebrew, "Brit" (b'rit) means "covenant." Thus, "Britain" could mean "land of the covenant" or "covenant people" (Brit-am). This connection aligns with Britain's identity as a leading nation of Israel (Ephraim) and the recipient of God's covenant blessings.  
4. Brutus and the Zerahite Line  
Brutus, as a descendant of the Trojan royal house, is part of the Zerahite lineage of Judah. His story, therefore, represents an early "planting" of the Zerahite royal seed in Britain, long before Jeremiah's arrival in Ireland. This establishes a foundational Zerahite presence in the British Isles, which would later intermarry with other Jewish and Israelite lines, including the Davidic line brought by Tea Tephi.  
Appendix I: The Greek Connection: Danaans and Spartans  
A Note on Israelite Migrations to Greece  
Beyond the traditional biblical narrative confined to the Holy Land, certain theories propose early Israelite migrations to the Aegean region, particularly involving the tribe of Dan and descendants of Zerah. These theories suggest a hidden Israelite influence on early Greek civilization.  
1. The Danaans (Tribe of Dan)  
●     Biblical Clues: The tribe of Dan is noted for its seafaring nature (Judges 5:17).  
●     Historical Accounts: Greek historians like Hecataeus of Abdera (4th century B.C.) and Diodorus of Sicily (1st century B.C.) mention that some of the "aliens" expelled from Egypt (often interpreted as Israelites during the Exodus) migrated to Greece under leaders like Danaus and Cadmus. The famed Greek poet Homer often used the term "Danaans" for the Greeks, a name they called themselves (Danai or Danoi).  
●     Scholarly Connections: Nineteenth-century ethnologist Dr. Robert Latham and later scholars like Cyrus Gordon have connected the Argive Danai with the Israelite tribe of Dan.  
●     Cultural Links: The practice of circumcision among the Colchi (a people near the Black Sea, linked to Greek colonization) and Jews is noted as being brought from Egypt, further suggesting a shared origin.  
2. The Spartan Connection  
●     Ancient Letters: The most compelling evidence for an Israelite-Greek connection comes from letters exchanged between the king of the Lacedemonians (Spartans) and the Jewish High Priest Onias in the 2nd century B.C. The Spartan King Areus wrote: "we have discovered that both the Jews and the Lacedemonians are of one stock, and are derived from the kindred of Abraham."  
●     Heraldic Emblem: The Spartan letter's seal, an eagle with a dragon (serpent) in its claws, is identified as the heraldic emblem of the tribe of Dan (Genesis 49:17: "Dan shall be a serpent by the way, a viper by the path").  
●     Confirmation: The Jewish High Priest Jonathan later affirmed this kinship, stating it was "well satisfied about it from the sacred writings."  
3. Zerahite Presence in Greece  
As detailed in Appendix D, descendants of Zerah (Judah's son), such as Darda (Dardanus, founder of Troy) and Calcol (Cecrops, founder of Athens), also established royal lines in Greece and Asia Minor. This suggests a complex intermingling of Israelite tribes (Dan, Judah) in the Aegean region, contributing to the diverse heritage of figures like Gathelus.  
4. Implications for the Narrative  
This "Greek Connection" provides a rich historical tapestry for Gathelus's background, explaining his "Greek" identity as actually rooted in an ancient Israelite heritage. It also justifies the presence of "Danaans" (Tuatha Dé Danann) in Ireland, suggesting they are earlier waves of Israelite migrants, setting up the dynamic between them and the later Milesian (Zerahite) arrivals.  
Appendix J: The Trojan Legacy and European Royalty  
A Note on Royal Lineages  
The concept that many European royal houses trace their lineage back to the legendary city of Troy is a recurring theme in medieval genealogies and chronicles. Within the framework of our narrative, this connection is seen as a direct link to the Zerahite branch of the tribe of Judah, fulfilling the prophecy that the scepter would not depart from Judah, even through its dispersed lines.  
1. The Royal House of Troy: Descent from Zerah  
●     Dardanus: The traditional founder of Troy's royal house, Dardanus, is identified with the biblical Darda (or Dara), a son/descendant of Zerah, son of Judah (1 Chronicles 2:6). This establishes Troy as a Zerahite kingdom.  
●     Genealogical Link: Medieval genealogies, including those in James Anderson's Royal Genealogies, trace nearly all European royal families back to the house of Troy. This suggests a widespread diffusion of Zerahite bloodlines throughout Europe's aristocracy.  
2. Odin (Woden/Votan): The Ancestor of Northern European Royalty  
●     Historical Figure: Norse sagas and historical accounts (like Snorri Sturluson's Edda and Heimskringla) suggest that Odin, chief god of the Teutonic pantheon, was a real historical king or chieftain. Archaeological evidence and genealogical calculations (e.g., by Thor Heyerdahl) place him in southern Russia (near the Sea of Azov, identified as Asgard) around the 1st-3rd centuries A.D.  
●     Migration to Scandinavia: Odin is believed to have led his people (the Aesir) from the Caucasus region (a known area of Israelite dispersion after Assyrian captivity) to Scandinavia, establishing new dynasties.  
●     Hebrew Name: Odin's name is linked to the Hebrew "Aud'n" or "Odn," meaning "Lord" (Adon), a title of headship.  
●     Trojan Descent: Snorri's Edda explicitly traces Odin's lineage back to the house of Troy, through figures like Thor and Tror (Tros), and ultimately to Priam and Dardanus.  
●     Impact: Odin becomes the common ancestor for many royal houses of Northern Europe, including the Saxons, Danes, and Normans, all of whom eventually contributed to the British monarchy. This means a significant portion of European royalty carries the Zerahite lineage of Judah.  
3. The Fusion of Lines  
The Trojan legacy, through Odin and other migrations, ensured that the Zerahite bloodline was deeply embedded in European royalty. When the Davidic line from the Pharez branch (through Tea Tephi) was planted in Ireland, and later merged with the Scottish and English thrones, it intermarried with these existing Zerahite royal families. This process reinforced the Jewish heritage of the British monarchy countless times over, ensuring the fulfillment of God's promise that the scepter would not depart from Judah.  
Appendix K: Joseph of Arimathea and the Line of Nathan  
A Note on Post-Christ Davidic Lineage  
While the main narrative focuses on the Solomonic line of David through Tea Tephi, some traditions propose a separate, yet equally significant, continuation of the Davidic lineage through David's son Nathan, particularly involving Joseph of Arimathea. This provides an additional layer of Jewish heritage to the British royal family.  
1. The Line of Nathan and Jesus's Genealogy  
●     Nathanic Descent: Luke's Gospel (Luke 3:23, 30-33) traces Jesus's lineage through his mother Mary back to David's son Nathan, distinct from the Solomonic line of his adoptive father Joseph (Matthew 1:1-16).  
●     Joseph of Arimathea's Kinship: Joseph of Arimathea, a wealthy and influential member of the Jewish council (Sanhedrin), is traditionally believed to have been a close relative of Jesus, possibly Mary's uncle or great-uncle, thus placing him in the royal line of Nathan. His claim to Jesus's body after the crucifixion, despite fear of the Jews, suggests a legal right as next of kin.  
2. Joseph of Arimathea and Britain  
●     Glastonbury Legend: Medieval traditions, particularly from Glastonbury Abbey in Somerset, England, claim that Joseph of Arimathea traveled to Britain as head of 12 missionaries, establishing the first Christian church in England (c. A.D. 37 or 63).  
●     Tin Trade Connection: Joseph is associated with the ancient tin trade between Britain (the "Tin Islands" or Cassiterides) and the Mediterranean. This suggests he had established contacts in Britain before his missionary journey. Evidence of "Jews' Houses" (smelting ovens) and place names like Marazion ("sight of Zion" or "bitterness of Zion") in Cornwall further suggest early Jewish involvement in British mining.  
●     Royal Favor: King Arviragus (a British high king) is said to have granted Joseph and his followers land in Glastonbury, indicating a favorable reception, possibly due to Joseph's noble status and existing connections.  
●     Royal Converts: Roman history mentions Claudia (Gladys), daughter of British high king Caradoc, who was adopted by Emperor Claudius and converted to Christianity in Rome (c. A.D. 60s), along with her husband Pudens and brother Linus. Some theories suggest they were converted in Britain, potentially through Joseph's influence, further linking early British royalty to Christianity and the Davidic lineage.  
3. Joseph's Descendants and British Royalty  
●     Founding Dynasties: Grail romances, though fictional, sometimes depict Joseph founding a line of kings or a "grail family" in Britain. Some genealogies (e.g., in the Welsh Pantliwydd Manuscripts) trace King Arthur's lineage back to Joseph.  
●     Bran the Blessed: Joseph is associated with Bran the Blessed, a British ruler believed by some to have been an early Christian convert and an ancestor of several Welsh royal families.  
●     Fusion with Zerahite Lines: This Nathanic line, through intermarriage with early British kings (who were likely descendants of Brutus and thus Zerahite), would have further infused the Davidic bloodline into the British aristocracy. The later intermarriages of the British monarchy with other European royal houses (many of whom were Zerahite) would have reinforced this Jewish heritage multiple times over.  
4. The Roman Purge and Flight  
The Roman Emperor Domitian (A.D. 81-96) ordered a purge of all who were of David's line, fearing the advent of Christ's earthly kingdom. This persecution likely prompted many Davidic descendants to flee to outlying areas of the Empire, including Britain, providing another plausible reason for the strengthening of Davidic presence there.  
Appendix L: The Nobility: Also Jewish  
A Note on European Aristocracy  
The theory of a widespread Jewish heritage within European royalty extends to the broader nobility, suggesting that the "breeding out" of Jewish bloodlines through intermarriage is a misconception. This is based on the historical origins of nobility and the patterns of intermarriage.  
1. Nobility as Extended Royalty  
The most common origin of nobility is simply the extended family of royalty. Since European royal families are traced back to the Zerahite line of Judah (and later fused with the Davidic line of Perez/Nathan), their extended families, who formed the aristocracy, would also largely share this Jewish heritage.  
2. Landed Gentry and Warrior Class  
Historically, land in the Old World was granted by kings or conquered. Those who received land grants were often already of social status, or military supporters of the king. The warrior class, from which many nobles descended, often had a distinct ethnic origin.  
●     Scots (Milesians): In Ireland, the Milesian Scots who took over from the Tuatha Dé Danann (tribe of Dan) were largely of Jewish extraction, many having descended from Zerah. Irish historian Thomas Moore noted that St. Patrick applied the name "Scots" to the "high and dominant class" (nobles/military), while the common people were "Hiberionaces" (Hiberni or Hebrews, likely Danites). This indicates a Jewish aristocracy ruling over a broader Israelite populace. The Scottish UiNialls (O'Neills) of Ulster, who transferred the high kingship to Scotland, were heavily Jewish, bearing the Red Hand of Zerah.  
●     Early British Nobility: The line of Brutus of Troy (Zerahite) likely diffused throughout early Celtic British nobility.  
3. Jews Among the Scythians and Teutons  
The Anglo-Saxons and other Teutonic lines (Scandinavians, Northern Europeans) are traced back to the Israelites taken into Assyrian captivity (late 700s B.C.). The Assyrians also deported many from Judah (Jews, Benjamites, Levites).  
●     Jats/Jutes: Some scholars connect the "Jats" (a caste in Northwest India, bordering Persia/Parthia) with the "Jutes" of Europe (who gave their name to Jutland, Denmark, and were early invaders of Britain). The name "Jute" (Yuten in German/Scandinavian) is phonetically linked to "Juden" (German for Jews), suggesting a Jewish origin for this warrior class. Hengist and Horsa, the Jutish Vikings who founded the Kingdom of Kent (first Teutonic kingdom in England), were likely Jewish and descended from Odin (Zerahite).  
●     Danes: Danish Vikings, also descended from Odin, further mixed Jewish nobility with Anglo-Saxon populations.  
●     Normans: The Norman nobility, who conquered England in 1066, were Danish Vikings who had settled in northern France. They intermarried with the Frankish (French) nobility. The Frankish kings (Sicambrians) traced their descent from the House of Troy (Zerahite). Thus, the Norman chiefs were almost certainly Jewish, being descended from Odin. The Norman Conquest further infused Jewish nobility into England.  
4. The Intermarriage of European Royalty and Nobility  
Through thousands of years, European royalty and nobility largely intermarried within their own class. As many of these royal families were already of Zerahite (and later Davidic) descent, these intermarriages continuously reinforced the Jewish heritage of the European aristocracy, including the British royal family. This suggests a divinely orchestrated "family planning" to ensure the widespread Jewish descent of European royalty and nobility, fulfilling the prophecy of Judah's enduring scepter.
